content.text:
## 1. Jurisdictional Limits — Maximum Amount You Can Sue For

Alabama small claims courts (a division of the District Court in each county) have an **upper limit of $6,000** per case (not including interest and court costs) ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,promulgated%20by%20the%20Supreme%20Court)). This $6,000 cap applies uniformly to **all plaintiffs** – individuals, sole proprietors, partnerships, LLCs, corporations, and even government bodies – there is no higher or lower cap based on the type of party. Alabama law gives the district court *exclusive* jurisdiction over civil matters up to $6,000 ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,promulgated%20by%20the%20Supreme%20Court)), so you cannot demand more than that in a single small claims case. 

- **No annual filing limits:** Unlike some states, Alabama does *not* impose a limit on the number of small claims cases one plaintiff can file per year. Businesses and frequent filers can use small claims repeatedly, provided each claim is within $6,000. (However, Alabama does bar certain bulk collection practices – e.g. a collection agency suing on an assigned debt must hire an attorney ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,represented%20by%20a%20licensed%20attorney)) ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,original%20owner%20of%20the%20claim)) – see Section 13 on business rules.)

- **No claim-splitting to evade the cap:** You **cannot split one larger claim into multiple $6,000 lawsuits** to get around the jurisdictional limit. For example, if someone owes you $9,000 on one contract, you may not file two suits for $4,500 each – courts generally consider that an abuse of process. All amounts from one transaction should be combined into a single claim. If your claim exceeds $6,000, you’ll have to either abandon the excess amount or file in the regular District or Circuit Court (which has higher jurisdictional limits). Alabama’s small claims statute doesn’t explicitly discuss claim-splitting (this is simply a general legal principle), so when in doubt, it’s safer to file in a higher court or reduce your demand to $6,000.

- **Historical note:** The small claims limit in Alabama was **raised from $3,000 to $6,000** in 2015 by legislative Act 2015-224 ([www.troymessenger.com](https://www.troymessenger.com/2015/08/05/small-claims-court-increases-claim-amount-limitations/#:~:text=Small%20business%20owners%20and%20others,3%2C000%20jurisdictional%20limitation%20any%20longer)). This change allows more cases to be handled cheaply; a decade-old source that mentions a $3,000 limit is now outdated. Currently, $6,000 is the maximum for any one case.

## 2. What Types of Cases Qualify — *Everything* You Can Sue For

**Almost any dispute over money or property valued at $6,000 or less can be heard in Alabama small claims court** ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,promulgated%20by%20the%20Supreme%20Court)). The key is that you are suing for monetary compensation or the return of personal property – not for injunctions or purely punitive matters (see the “Cannot Sue For” section for exceptions). Below is an exhaustive list of claim types commonly brought in small claims, organized by category. For each, we note if it’s allowed in Alabama, give real-world examples, and highlight any Alabama-specific rules or statutes that apply. If a special statute governs the claim (for example, double damages for a landlord’s bad-faith handling of a deposit), we cite it. Always remember that the **$6,000 cap** applies to the *total* amount you seek (including any punitive or statutory damages), so some claims may be better suited for a higher court if the value is above $6,000.

### Contract & Money-Owed Disputes

- **Breach of written contract:** Yes – small claims can enforce written agreements (e.g. service contracts, sales contracts) up to $6,000. For example, if you paid a contractor $4,000 to remodel your kitchen and they never did the work, or if a customer owes you $2,000 on a written agreement and hasn’t paid, you can sue for damages. Small claims courts routinely hear contract cases as long as the amount is within the limit ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,promulgated%20by%20the%20Supreme%20Court)). *No special pre-filing steps* are required for contract claims in Alabama (though sending a polite demand letter is good practice). Just be sure to bring a **copy of the signed contract** to court. Alabama follows ordinary contract law: if the contract is valid and the other party breached it, you can recover the unpaid amount or the cost of the unfinished work. If the contract itself provides for attorneys’ fees or other costs, note that you **cannot recover attorney fees in small claims unless you actually hired an attorney to represent you** ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,represented%20by%20a%20licensed%20attorney)) ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,represented%20by%20a%20licensed%20attorney)) (most people don’t – see Section 10 on attorneys).

- **Breach of oral/handshake contract:** Yes – verbal agreements are generally enforceable in Alabama if you can prove the terms and the other party’s breach. Examples: you agreed in a conversation to sell your used lawnmower for $300 and the buyer never paid, or you paid someone $100 to fix your window and they never showed up. These can be brought in small claims. Alabama’s **Statute of Frauds** (Ala. Code § 8-9-2) requires certain contracts to be in writing (e.g. real estate contracts, agreements that can’t be performed within a year, sales of goods over $500 under the UCC), so if your oral agreement falls into those categories it might not be enforceable. But everyday small deals are fine. Be prepared to present any texts, emails, receipts, or witness testimony to prove the oral contract existed. No statute requires a written contract for small claims – it’s just easier to prove one if it’s written.

- **Unpaid personal loans (informal IOUs):** Yes – if you lent money to a friend or family member and they didn’t pay you back, you can sue for the balance (up to $6k). This is essentially an oral contract or promissory-note situation. Example: You lent your cousin $1,000 cash with the promise of repayment in 3 months, and 6 months have passed without payment. Bring any proof of the loan (messages, a written IOU, bank withdrawal receipts, etc.). Tip: Clearly identify the *legal name* and address of the person you lent to – if they’ve moved, find their current address for service.

- **Unpaid promissory notes:** Yes – if you have a signed promissory note (a written promise to repay a loan), it’s strong evidence of debt. Small claims can enforce notes up to $6k. Example: A former employee signed a promissory note to repay a $2,500 advance but hasn’t paid; you can file a claim attaching the note as proof. Under Alabama law, a promissory note is a form of contract – there’s no additional requirement beyond proving the note and the default. If the note has an interest rate or late fees, you can include the accrued interest up to the date of judgment (note that pre-judgment interest on a contract claim is allowed at the contract rate or 6% if unspecified) ([www.counselorlibrary.com](https://www.counselorlibrary.com/ai-statelaw-clc/finance-charge-rate-pre--and-post-judgment-rates-alabama.html#:~:text=Finance%20Charge%20Rate%20%3E%20Pre,Code)), but the total of principal + interest should not exceed $6,000 in small claims.

- **Unpaid invoices or accounts receivable:** Yes – businesses or individuals can sue for unpaid **open accounts** or **invoices**. For example, a freelance graphic designer might sue a client for $1,200 of unpaid invoices, or a supplier might sue a shop owner for $5,000 in goods delivered but not paid for. These are treated as contract debts. In your Statement of Claim, describe the transactions, dates, and amounts due. It’s helpful to attach copies of the invoices and any proof of delivery or the client’s acknowledgement. Alabama does not require any special debt-collection license or procedure for an original creditor to sue in small claims. **Important:** If the debt was **assigned to you** (for instance, a finance company that bought a debt from the original business), Alabama *requires* that an attorney handle the case ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,original%20owner%20of%20the%20claim)) – small claims courts won’t let non-lawyer assignees file collection suits. This rule is meant to prevent unlicensed collection agencies from flooding the court (see Ala. Code § 12-12-31(d)). But if you’re the original party to the transaction (you sold the goods or lent the money directly), you may sue on your own.

- **Unpaid rent (residential or commercial):** Yes – a landlord can sue a tenant (or former tenant) in small claims for up to $6,000 in **unpaid rent** or other lease breaches that involve money. Example: Your tenant moved out owing 2 months’ rent ($1,800) – you can file a small claim for that amount. Or a commercial landlord might sue a small business tenant for $5,000 in rent arrears after eviction. *Note:* Small claims court **does not handle eviction (possession)** – that’s a separate **unlawful detainer** case filed in District Court (not on the small claims docket). But for the money part (back rent, damages, fees), small claims is appropriate. Alabama law doesn’t require any pre-suit notice for a claim of back rent (the termination notice required to evict is separate and does not recover money). If the lease or Alabama law provided for late fees or other charges, you can include those, but be mindful of the $6k cap. Bring a copy of the written lease (if one) and a **rent ledger** or receipts to show the amounts due. If you already evicted the tenant through court, you may have a **judgment for possession**; you can still use small claims to get a money judgment for rent if it wasn’t included, as long as it doesn’t exceed $6k.

- **Bounced or bad checks:** Yes – if someone paid you with a check that **bounced** (was returned for insufficient funds or account closed), you can sue for the amount of the check *and* possibly additional damages. **Alabama’s bad check law** (Ala. Code § 6-5-285) gives the holder of a worthless check a civil cause of action and even allows **punitive damages and attorney’s fees** in court’s discretion ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-6/chapter-5/article-16/section-6-5-285/#:~:text=The%20holder%20of%20a%20worthless,trying%20the%20case%20may%20assess)). In other words, you could recover more than just the face value of the check if the judge finds the bad check writing was wrongful. Example: A customer gave you a $500 check that was returned NSF and never made good – you can sue for $500 plus any bank fees you incurred, and the court *could* award something extra as punishment (though in small claims, punitive amounts tend to be modest). Note that **criminal** prosecution for bad checks is separate – you don’t need a criminal case to sue civilly, and you can sue even if the person was not charged criminally ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-6/chapter-5/article-16/section-6-5-285/#:~:text=The%20holder%20of%20a%20worthless,trying%20the%20case%20may%20assess)). Alabama requires that such an action be filed within **one year** of the bad check date ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-6/chapter-5/article-16/section-6-5-285/#:~:text=delivered%20the%20same%20to%20him,action%20may%20recover%20such%20damages)), so act promptly. It’s wise (though not legally required) to send a **certified demand letter** to the check writer giving them an opportunity to pay up before you sue – this can sometimes spur payment and it documents your good faith. If you do sue, bring the original bounced check (or bank copy with the dishonor stamp) and any bank notices as evidence. Alabama does **not** have a fixed statutory penalty like some states (e.g. some states allow double or treble the check amount); instead, the court can award compensatory and punitive damages it deems proper ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-6/chapter-5/article-16/section-6-5-285/#:~:text=date%20of%20the%20unlawful%20act,trying%20the%20case%20may%20assess)). Practically, many small claims judges will award the check amount plus your bank fee and perhaps a small additional sum for the trouble.

- **Open-book accounts / running tabs:** Yes – if you had an ongoing credit arrangement (a tab at a store, a customer account, etc.) and the person never paid the final balance, you can sue for that balance. Treat it like any other contract debt. For example, a feed store might sue a farmer for $3,500 owed on a feed account. Provide an **account statement** or itemized list of charges and payments, and a copy of any credit agreement if one exists. Alabama doesn’t impose special interest rate limits in small claims beyond general law – by default, unpaid accounts may earn 6% annual interest from when payment was due (if you choose to claim interest) ([www.counselorlibrary.com](https://www.counselorlibrary.com/ai-statelaw-clc/finance-charge-rate-pre--and-post-judgment-rates-alabama.html#:~:text=Finance%20Charge%20Rate%20%3E%20Pre,Code)), but you must plead for pre-judgment interest specifically and it counts toward the $6k cap.

- **Guarantor/co-signer liability:** Yes – if you co-signed a loan or guaranteed someone’s obligation and ended up paying it, you can sue the primary debtor for what you had to pay (this is essentially a contract of reimbursement). For instance, you co-signed a car loan that your friend defaulted on; you paid the bank $5,000 to settle it, and now you want your friend to repay you – small claims can handle that. Show the co-signer agreement and proof you paid. Likewise, if you were a *guarantor* for a lease or contract and had to cover the debt, you step into the creditor’s shoes and may sue the primary obligor. Alabama follows general suretyship law – once you pay as a guarantor, you have the right to recover that amount from the principal. No special rule complicates this in small claims (just ensure the amount is ≤ $6k). 

### Consumer and Customer Disputes

- **Refunds for defective products:** Yes – if you bought a product that turned out to be defective or not as advertised, and the seller won’t refund or repair, you can sue for a refund or the cost of repair (within $6k). Examples: a computer you bought for $1,200 that died right after purchase and the store refuses to honor the warranty; or a used appliance that never worked. These cases can be brought as breach of contract or warranty. Alabama has adopted the **Uniform Commercial Code (UCC)** for sales of goods (Ala. Code Title 7), which includes **implied warranties** that goods are merchantable and fit for ordinary purposes. If a product was sold “as is” or with warranty disclaimers, the seller might argue they disclaimed warranties – but those are defenses for court. In small claims, you just need to show the item was faulty and you didn’t get what you paid for. If a **written warranty** or service plan was involved (e.g. a manufacturer’s warranty on a used car or an appliance), bring a copy and evidence of your attempts to get service. Alabama doesn’t require any pre-suit notice to the seller beyond what the warranty might require (some manufacturer warranties require you to go to an authorized service center first, etc., so do that if applicable). You can sue for the cost of the item and any incidental expenses caused by the defect (e.g. repair costs, towing fees for a lemon car) as long as it’s under $6k. If the situation involves **fraudulent misrepresentation** (e.g. the seller lied about a product’s condition), you might also claim under Alabama’s fraud statutes or the Deceptive Trade Practices Act – see “False advertising” below.

- **Poor or incomplete services (contractor, repairman, etc.):** Yes – if you paid for a service that was not performed or was done badly, you can sue to get your money back or the cost to fix the poor work. Examples: a roofer was paid $3,000 and left the job half-done; an auto mechanic charged $800 but didn’t actually fix the problem; a wedding photographer took your $1,000 deposit and never showed up. These are common small claims. You’d sue for **breach of contract** or failure to perform services. Be ready to show what was agreed (a contract or estimate or at least a description of the job), what you paid, and how the service was deficient. If the work was done but **negligently**, you can also claim the cost to repair the damage. Alabama doesn’t have a specialized home improvement contractor statute for consumer suits, so it’s just general contract law – no need to, say, file a complaint with the state licensing board first (though if the contractor is required to be licensed and was not, that can be argued in your favor or reported separately). In small claims, you can’t demand the contractor *complete* the work (no specific performance), only money to compensate you. Check if the person was licensed (for example, Alabama requires a Home Builders licensure for jobs over $10,000 – which might not apply to a sub-$6k small claim but could indicate credibility). For auto repairs, if you were **overcharged beyond an estimate**, note that Alabama’s laws regarding auto repair are not as strict as some states (there’s no specific statute forcing a written estimate or limiting overcharges), but you can argue common-law fraud or breach of an implied contract that the charges would be reasonable. 

- **Auto repair disputes (shoddy work or overcharges):** Yes – a very common scenario. If a mechanic’s work was faulty and caused damage, or they charged for repairs not actually done, or kept your car too long causing you loss, you can sue. Example: you paid $500 for a brake job and the brakes failed the next day causing you to need towing and a second repair – you could sue the original mechanic for the $500 plus the towing and the cost of the second fix (if under $6k). Another example: a body shop gave an estimate of $1,200 but then billed $2,500 without justification – you could sue to recover the overpayment. Alabama doesn’t have a specific “Auto Repair Act” requiring estimates or giving treble damages for fraud (some states do), so your case will rely on general principles: breach of contract if they didn’t do what was promised, or fraud if they misrepresented what was needed. It helps to have a second mechanic’s statement about what was wrong with the work. Always bring *invoices, the work order/estimate, and photos* of any poor workmanship. If the dispute is over parts not replaced, ask for the old parts back (if you have them or can get an expert to testify none were new). Small claims judges often have to decide these based on credibility and any expert evidence you can muster (written statements from a certified mechanic can help if the amount justifies the effort). No pre-suit notice is required; just ensure you’ve given the shop a chance to make it right (many judges like to see that you *asked* for a refund or fix and were refused before suing).

- **Home improvement / contractor disputes:** Yes – if you hired a contractor or handyman for home repairs or remodeling and things went wrong, small claims is appropriate for losses up to $6k. Examples: a handyman charged $2,000 to remodel a bathroom but left it unfinished; a contractor’s subpar work caused $3,500 in water damage; or a painter took a $500 down payment and never started the job. These fall under breach of contract (and possibly negligence). In Alabama, contractors working on jobs **over $10,000** must be state-licensed, but for small claims-size jobs, licensing may not be mandated – check if your contractor *should* have been licensed or if they violated any local building codes, as these facts can strengthen your case (an unlicensed contractor cannot enforce a contract in Alabama, but you as the homeowner can still seek damages). There’s no state-run dispute board for contractors; you can go straight to court. If a contractor abandoned the job, you can sue for the cost to hire someone else to finish or to redo poor work. Bring any **contract or written estimate**, **proof of payments** (canceled checks, receipts), and photos of incomplete or bad work. If you had to pay someone else to fix it, bring that invoice as evidence of your damages. Also, Alabama’s **homebuilder licensure law** might allow you to report unlicensed activity to a board, but that’s separate from recovering your money. Small claims judges are used to these disputes – just be clear on what was agreed and how the contractor breached it.

- **Appliance or electronics warranty failures:** Yes – if you bought an appliance or electronic device that came with a warranty (or extended warranty) and the seller/manufacturer is not honoring it, you can sue for the cost of repair or replacement. Example: you purchased a used refrigerator “certified refurbished” with a 90-day warranty for $800, it died in 60 days, and the store won’t fix or refund – you can sue for that $800 and perhaps any food spoilage losses. Warranties are contracts, so this is a contract claim. Under the federal **Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act**, you actually can sue in state court for breach of warranty and even recover attorney fees if you had an attorney – but in small claims, you likely won’t have an attorney, so attorney fees aren’t an issue. Alabama doesn’t have a state-specific warranty act beyond the UCC implied warranties and Magnuson-Moss (federal). Key point: if the warranty required you to do something (e.g. return the product to an authorized center or call a number for service), do that prior to suing, or the judge may say you didn’t give the warrantor a chance. Bring the warranty terms, any communications with the company, and evidence of the defect (photos, repair reports). If you bought a service contract (extended warranty) from a third party and they aren’t paying for a covered repair, you can also sue them in small claims (just make sure to name the correct company – check your paperwork for the company’s legal name).

- **No-show or canceled services (event venues, caterers, photographers, etc.):** Yes – if you paid for an event service and the provider failed to show up or canceled at the last minute, causing you loss, you can sue for what you paid and related damages. Example: you put down a $1,000 deposit on a wedding photographer who never showed up – you can sue for the deposit and perhaps any extra cost you incurred to find a last-minute replacement (within the $6k cap). Another example: an event hall took your $500 reservation fee and then double-booked and kicked you out – you can claim the fee plus any extra you spent relocating your event. These cases are straightforward contract breaches. Provide the contract or booking agreement and proof of payment. If you suffered additional monetary losses due to the breach (e.g. you had to pay another caterer more at the last minute), you can claim those as consequential damages, but be prepared to itemize and prove them. Alabama law doesn’t impose special penalties for this scenario (no treble damages or anything), but a judge can certainly award a full refund and associated losses. Always attempt to resolve directly first – if the vendor offered a later make-up date or partial refund that doesn’t solve it, you can still proceed to court.

- **Property loss or damage by a business (dry cleaners, hotels, movers, storages):** Yes – if a company temporarily had custody of your property and it was lost, damaged, or stolen due to their negligence, you can sue for the value (up to $6k). Common examples: a dry cleaner ruined your dress ($200 damage); a valet service crashed your car; a storage facility’s roof leaked and destroyed $3,000 of your furniture; a moving company broke your TV during a move. These are typically **bailment** situations – you entrusted property and they had a duty of care. In Alabama, a bailee (the business in possession) can be held liable if they failed to exercise reasonable care and your property was damaged. No special pre-suit procedure is required, but it’s wise to file a **claim with the business’s insurance** if they have a process, as sometimes they’ll pay without litigation. If not, small claims is your recourse. Bring **receipts or evidence of the item’s value** (original purchase price, age, condition) and **photos of damage**. If the value is disputed, the judge will decide a fair value (often depreciated value for used items). Watch out: some service tickets or contracts (like a dry cleaner’s ticket or a storage contract) might have a **liability limitation or requirement to file a notice within X days**. For example, a dry cleaner might disclaim liability for certain items or a warehouse might cap liability at $0.50 per pound unless you declared a higher value. These terms can be raised by the business as a defense. Even so, you can still file in small claims and let the judge interpret those terms’ enforceability. Be sure to comply with any basic notice requirement (e.g. some storage agreements require you to report damage within 30 days of discovering it). If you did notify the business promptly and they refused to compensate, that’s all you need to show in court.

- ** Gym membership or subscription cancellations:** Yes – if you canceled a gym, spa, or other membership that was supposed to stop billing and they kept charging you (or refused to refund a prepaid amount), you can sue for the money. Example: you paid a $600 annual gym membership, canceled after 3 months due to a move (as allowed by the contract or state law), but the gym won’t refund the unused portion – you could claim the remaining $450. Alabama’s **health club law** (Ala. Code § 8-23-5) allows consumers to cancel under certain conditions (like moving 25+ miles away or if the gym closes) and get refunds for the unused term. If your situation fits one of those, cite that law; if not, rely on the contract terms or unfair practice. Another scenario: you properly canceled a cable/Internet or subscription service but were still charged, you can seek a refund of those unauthorized charges. Always *follow the contract’s cancellation procedure exactly* (e.g. written notice to a specific address). Retain proof of your cancellation notice and any confirmation. If the business has a corporate address, that might be where to send a demand or serve the lawsuit (for big companies like ATT, etc., you often serve their registered agent – check with the Alabama Secretary of State’s business entity search for a registered agent name/address). Alabama doesn’t have a unique **automatic-renewal cancellation law** (some states do), so rely on whatever the contract or general consumer protection principles provide. In small claims, judges often side with consumers if they clearly canceled and have proof but were still charged.

- **Auto sales disputes (used car issues, “lemon” cars, odometer fraud):** Possibly – small claims can handle certain used-car disputes, but keep in mind many car cases exceed $6,000 in damages. **Used Cars:** Alabama’s **Lemon Law** (Ala. Code § 8-20A-1 et seq.) technically applies **only to new (untitled) vehicles** and gives the manufacturer a chance to repair; it wouldn’t apply to a purely used car sale between private parties or non-manufacturer dealers. For a used car bought “as is” from a dealer, generally you cannot recover for defects unless there was **fraud** (e.g. dealer lied about the condition) or a **written warranty/service contract** that was breached. In small claims, you *can* sue a dealer for fraud or misrepresentation if, say, they sold you a car advertised as having 50k miles but it actually had 150k (odometer rollback), or they concealed a salvage title. Federal law (49 U.S.C. § 32710) provides a private right of action for **odometer fraud** with treble damages or $10,000 statutory damages (whichever greater), but $10,000 is above small-claims limit. If you have an odometer fraud case under that law, you might consider waiving the excess and suing for $6,000 (the judge in small claims cannot technically award more than $6k, even if the statute allows more). If the dealer explicitly **warrantied** the car (e.g. 30-day drivetrain warranty) and then refused to fix a covered problem, that’s a straightforward breach of warranty claim – you could sue for the cost of repairs. **Private seller deals**: If you bought a car from a private individual, typically it’s “as is” unless they specifically guaranteed something. However, if the seller **committed fraud** (e.g. rolled back odometer or lied “never in accident” when it was), you could sue for the difference in value. Alabama courts allow fraud claims in used car sales, but proving it requires evidence of the misrepresentation and that you relied on it. Note, Alabama follows **caveat emptor** (buyer beware) for used car sales *except* in cases of fraud or when the seller is a dealer who must disclose defects under certain regulations. In small claims, a judge may lean on fairness: if a car breaks down the day after sale and the seller clearly lied, you might get a favorable judgment. But if it’s just bad luck and no promises were made, you likely won’t. **New Cars (“Lemon Law”):** Alabama’s lemon law covers new vehicles in the first 24 months/24,000 miles and requires written notice to the manufacturer and a chance to cure defects. If you qualify, the manufacturer must either replace the car or refund minus usage. Suing under the Lemon Law often happens in circuit court (and manufacturers often resolve these through their arbitration programs). If your **new car’s defect is relatively minor** and under $6k (for example, you paid $5,000 for repeated repairs on a lemon and want reimbursement), you *could* attempt a small claim for those repair costs – but you *cannot* force the manufacturer to take the car back or give you a new car via small claims (that’s equitable relief, not money). Usually lemon law cases exceed $6k (since the car’s value itself is at issue), so small claims is rarely the venue. Instead, you might use small claims for ancillary issues like a **title/tag fee dispute** or a **refund of dealer add-ons** that didn’t get provided. **Recap:** Yes, you can sue in small claims for used car misrepresentations, undisclosed defects, or minor warranty breaches – just keep the claim focused on a sum of money (repair costs, diminished value, or refund) and under $6k. 

- **False advertising / deceptive trade practices:** Yes – Alabama has a broad consumer protection law known as the **Deceptive Trade Practices Act (DTPA)** (Ala. Code § 8-19-1 et seq.) which prohibits businesses from engaging in fraud, false advertising, and deceptive practices. Consumers have a **private right of action** under this law to recover **actual damages or $100 (whichever greater)**, and potentially up to **triple the actual damages** (at the court’s discretion for egregious cases) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,100%2C%20whichever%20is%20greater%3B%20or)), plus **attorney’s fees** if they win ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,reasonable%20attorney%27s%20fees%20and%20costs)). Small claims courts can hear DTPA cases as long as the amount sought (even if trebled) doesn’t exceed $6,000. For example, if a car dealer knowingly misled you about a car’s accident history causing you $2,000 in damages, the court could award up to $6,000 (triple) under the DTPA. *However,* note that the DTPA says such actions may be brought in circuit court ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,the%20unlawful%20act%20or%20practice)). Because Alabama’s district court has exclusive jurisdiction up to $6k ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,promulgated%20by%20the%20Supreme%20Court)), filing a DTPA claim under $6k in small claims is generally acceptable, but if you are seeking the **punitive/triple damages** that push the claim above $6k, you’d have to go to a higher court or waive the excess. Also, Alabama DTPA requires that upon filing the suit, the **clerk of court must mail a copy of the complaint to the Attorney General and local DA** ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,shall%20mail%20a%20copy%20of)) (the court will do this, but it’s good for you to be aware). Common deceptive-practices small claims: a business falsely advertised a product (bait-and-switch causing you financial harm), a seller rolled back an odometer (also covered by federal law, as mentioned), or a retailer continued to bill you for a service you canceled (could be “unconscionable trade practice” under the Act). If you intend to invoke the DTPA for enhanced damages, **mention it in your complaint** (Statement of Claim) by referencing the statute, and be prepared to explain how the conduct was unlawful under the Act (Ala. Code § 8-19-5 lists unlawful acts). The court has discretion to award up to 3× damages ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,practices%20were%20committed%20intentionally%3B%20and)). In practice, small claims judges might award just actual damages unless the conduct was clearly willful or part of a broader pattern. Remember that **frivolous or bad-faith claims under the DTPA can result in you paying the defendant’s attorney fees** ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=foregoing%20liability%20or%20in%20which,reasonable%20attorney%27s%20fees%20and%20costs)) – so use it only when justified.

### Property Damage and Loss

- **Vehicle accidents (minor collisions):** Yes – if you were involved in a car accident and the property damage is within $6,000 (after subtracting any insurance payouts), you can sue the at-fault driver in small claims for your **out-of-pocket losses**. This typically includes the cost of repairs or the totaled value of your car (minus salvage or insurer payments), plus perhaps rental car costs or towing. Example: another driver hit your parked car causing $4,000 in damage and their insurance only paid $3,000 – you could sue the driver for the remaining $1,000 (or sue your own uninsured motorist if applicable, though usually insurance disputes go to arbitration). Alabama is a **tort liability** state for auto accidents, meaning the at-fault party must pay. In small claims, it’s a standard negligence claim: you must prove the other driver was negligent and caused the damage. Use the police report, photos of the scene, repair estimates, etc. Alabama is one of only a few **contributory negligence** states – if the defendant proves you were even 1% at fault, you recover nothing. This harsh rule applies even in small claims, so be aware the other side might argue you were partially to blame. If evidence shows the other driver was entirely at fault, you should win your damages. Note: you **cannot sue for pain and suffering** or personal injury in small claims beyond the $6k cap – so most injury cases don’t fit (see “Personal injury” below). But you can certainly claim related out-of-pocket costs like a chiropractor bill or lost wages *if* you keep the total under $6k, and you’re comfortable handling the injury claim without formal discovery or experts (which is tough – minor whiplash cases under $6k are sometimes brought pro se, but anything serious should go to higher court due to complexity). Many accident property claims in small claims involve **no insurance or insufficient insurance** – if the other driver had no insurance, small claims is an option to get a judgment (though collecting it is another matter – see post-judgment section). If the other driver was on the job (commercial vehicle), you might technically have to sue the employer (who likely won’t be in small claims) – but often for small amounts, you sue the driver and they bring their employer or insurer into it. Keep in mind the statute of limitations: for property damage, it’s **2 years** from the accident (same as personal injury) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/8577/11-47-23.html#:~:text=Section%2011,View%20Our%20Newest%20Version%20Here)), so file within that time.

- **Damage to your property by another person:** Yes – whether it’s intentional or accidental, you can sue someone who damaged your personal property. Examples: a neighbor backed their riding lawnmower into your fence causing $800 damage; a friend borrowed your power tool and broke it and refuses to pay; a prankster broke your window; a bar bouncer threw your phone and destroyed it. All of these can be small claims. Depending on circumstances, the legal theory might be negligence or wantonness, or even intentional tort (conversion, vandalism). In small claims, you don’t need to plead the exact theory – just state what happened and your loss. Provide **repair estimates** or receipts for replacement parts, or if something is destroyed, evidence of its value (original cost and age). Alabama allows recovery of the lesser of repair cost or fair market value for damaged property (generally). If it was **intentional damage**, note that punitive damages can in theory be awarded (though rare in small claims). Alabama law does allow punitive damages for intentional torts with clear and convincing evidence ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2015/title-6/chapter-11/article-2/section-6-11-20/#:~:text=and%20convincing%20evidence%20proven%3B%20definitions,AL%20Code%20%C2%A7)), but small claims judges seldom award them unless it’s egregious and within the $6k cap. If someone *stole* from you, you actually have a claim for the value (civil theft or conversion). Even if they faced criminal charges, you can pursue a civil judgment in small claims for the value of the item. There’s a potential overlap with Alabama’s **theft of property** criminal restitution, but that’s separate – your small claims case is a direct civil recovery.

- **Tenant damage to rental property (by a tenant):** Yes – if you’re a landlord and a former tenant caused damage to your property beyond normal wear and tear, you can sue for those damages. Often landlords first apply the **security deposit** to such damage, but if the damage exceeds the deposit, or if no deposit was taken, you have a claim. Example: a tenant left with $3,000 of damage (holes in walls, broken appliances) and the deposit was $1,000 – you can sue for the remaining $2,000. It’s important to follow Alabama’s **security deposit law** (Ala. Code § 35-9A-201) when handling the deposit: the landlord must provide an itemized list of damages and refund the remainder within **60 days** of lease end ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=,tenancy%20and%20delivery%20of%20possession)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=35,tenancy%20and%20delivery%20of%20possession)). If you failed to do that, the tenant may counterclaim that you forfeited the right to keep any deposit and even owe double deposit as a penalty ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=both%2C%20is%20sufficient%20compliance%20with,this%20chapter)). So, ensure you sent the required accounting. That aside, you can still sue for actual damages. Bring **before-and-after photos**, **move-in checklist** if any, invoices for repairs, and any written lease clauses on tenant responsibility. If the damage was intentional (e.g. tenant smashed all the windows out of anger), you could also report to police for criminal mischief, but that’s separate – civilly you just sue for the cost of repairs. The statute of limitations for property damage is 2 years (for conversion or trespass) so don’t wait too long.

- **Landlord damage to a tenant’s property:** Yes – if you’re a tenant and your landlord’s actions (or negligence) caused damage to *your* personal property, you can sue the landlord. Examples: a landlord’s maintenance worker left your door unlocked and your TV was stolen; the landlord negligently allowed a roof leak that ruined your furniture; or the landlord wrongfully threw out the belongings you left behind after an eviction without following proper procedure. In Alabama, if a landlord **improperly disposes of a tenant’s personal property** after the tenant moves out, the tenant can sue for the value (there’s no specific statute awarding multiples – it’s actual value). Also, under Alabama’s Uniform Residential Landlord-Tenant Act, a landlord must make repairs to maintain a habitable premises (Ala. Code § 35-9A-204), so if their failure to repair (after notice) caused your property loss (say they didn’t fix a known plumbing leak which ruined your rug), you can claim that as negligence or breach of lease. Bring evidence that the landlord knew of the issue (e.g. your prior written repair requests). Another scenario: **illegal lock-out or utility shut-off** – if a landlord locked you out or cut off utilities to make you leave (which is unlawful self-help in Alabama), you can sue for any losses (e.g. cost of alternative housing, damage to your property, etc.). There’s no separate statutory penalty in Alabama for illegal eviction, but your actual damages can be recovered. Just be aware: if it was part of an eviction dispute, jurisdiction might also lie in the eviction case – but since you’re seeking money, small claims is fine. Two-year statute applies for property torts.

- **Tree, fence, or boundary disputes resulting in damage:** Yes – if a neighbor’s actions caused damage to your property, you can sue. Common example: A neighbor’s tree fell onto your fence or house ($4,000 damage). Tree law in Alabama generally holds that if the tree was healthy (“act of God” scenario), the *property owner* whose property was damaged bears the loss (i.e. your own insurance) – you can’t automatically make the neighbor pay just because it was their tree, unless you can show negligence (e.g. the tree was dead or obviously unsafe and the neighbor knew and did nothing). If you *can* show negligence (photos of a rotted tree, prior requests to trim it, etc.), you have a stronger case. Another example: a neighbor building a fence accidentally crossed onto your land and damaged your sprinkler system. Or they intentionally removed your fence. You can sue for the repair costs. Alabama, like many states, has a specific law about cutting someone’s trees **willfully**: cutting timber on another’s land without permission can lead to the offender owing double the timber’s value (see Ala. Code § 35-14-1 et seq.), but that’s more for logging disputes. In a small claims context, if a neighbor maliciously cut down your ornamental trees or hedges, you’d sue for their value (and you could mention that such acts are taken seriously – though punitive damages would likely be limited by the $6k cap). Provide evidence of the value of trees or cost to replant landscaping. Another scenario: **water runoff** – if a neighbor’s actions (like diverting a drainage ditch) caused water damage on your property, you can claim the cost to fix your eroded lawn or flooded basement, etc., under a theory of nuisance or negligence. Small claims can handle those as long as you’re only asking for money, not an injunction to stop the water (injunctions can’t be issued by small claims). So you could get $$ to compensate you, but not an order making them change their drainage (you’d need a higher court for that). Always document the damage with photos and maybe a contractor’s statement on cause.

- **Pet damage (one pet harming another’s property or pet):** Yes – if someone’s dog injures or kills your pet, or their pet destroys your property, you can sue the owner for the **value of the loss or cost of vet bills**. Example: your neighbor’s dog chewed up your $500 garden furniture; or their dog attacked and injured your dog and you paid $1,200 in vet bills. Alabama treats pets as personal property in terms of legal damages, so you generally recover economic damages (vet bills, fair market value of the pet if killed, etc.), not emotional distress. Alabama has a **dog bite statute** (Ala. Code § 3-6-1) that makes owners liable if their dog bites someone *on the owner’s property or if the person was there by invitation* – but for dog-on-dog or dog-on-property incidents, it’s just common law negligence. If a pet was known to be vicious and the owner let it roam, you might have a strong case for negligence per se (violating leash laws, etc.). In small claims, just show the damage (photos of your injured pet, vet invoices, repair costs for any property). If a *person* was bitten (see “Personal injury” below), note that small claims can hear minor injury cases too. Also, if your own pet caused damage to someone else (say your dog tore up a visitor’s expensive coat), that person could likewise sue you – small claims goes both ways. Alabama follows the rule that pet owners are generally liable for damage their pets cause (with some defenses like provocation). There’s no separate “pet court,” so small claims is appropriate for these incidents as long as the dollar amount is within limit.

- **Damage during a move (by movers or friends helping):** Yes – if you hire a moving company and they break or lose your items, you can sue for the value (unless a contract limitation applies – many movers have you sign waivers or valuation limits, e.g. 60 cents per pound under federal regs for interstate moves). But you can still bring the claim, and the judge will consider any contractual limits. Example: movers dropped your 55″ TV – you want $600 to replace it. Or if a friend volunteered to help you move and negligently smashed an antique, you can sue the friend (though you might debate whether to, but legally you can). Provide any evidence of the item’s condition and value (original receipts, online valuation for similar item). If suing a **licensed moving company**, check if it was an interstate move (then federal law and the Bill of Lading terms might govern) or intrastate (Alabama law, perhaps limited liability if you agreed). For small claims, just outline what happened and the cost. The mover might send a company representative (they can in small claims without attorney, as an employee, since Alabama allows corporate representation by a full-time employee ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=cases%20on%20the%20small%20claims,time%20employee%20of%20the%20corporation)) ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=by%20an%20attorney%2C%20the%20person,time%20employee%20of%20the%20corporation))). They may argue any contractual fine print – the judge will decide if that limits your recovery. If you had *moving insurance*, use that route first; but if they denied your claim or it didn’t fully cover you, you can proceed in court for the remainder.

### Landlord–Tenant Disputes (Financial Only)

*(Note: *Evictions* (actual possession of property) are handled in a separate process and **cannot** be obtained through small claims – see the “Cannot Sue For” section. But small claims can resolve the money aspects arising from rental relationships.)*

- **Unreturned security deposits:** Yes – this is one of the most common tenant claims. Under Alabama’s landlord-tenant law, a landlord must return a tenant’s security deposit (minus any lawful deductions for damages or owed rent) within **60 days** after termination of tenancy and delivery of possession ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=,tenancy%20and%20delivery%20of%20possession)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=35,tenancy%20and%20delivery%20of%20possession)). If they withhold any portion, they must send an **itemized list of damages/charges** within that 60 days ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=35,tenancy%20and%20delivery%20of%20possession)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=,itemized%20list%20of%20amounts%20withheld)). If the landlord fails to mail *either* the refund or an accounting within 60 days, the law says the landlord **“shall pay the tenant double the amount of the tenant’s original deposit”** as a penalty ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=both%2C%20is%20sufficient%20compliance%20with,this%20chapter)). In other words, the tenant can recover *twice* the deposit amount, plus the deposit itself (functionally that means double = deposit back plus an equal amount as damages). This is a powerful provision. Example: you paid a $800 deposit, moved out on May 31, gave your landlord your forwarding address, and 3 months have passed with no refund or letter – you can sue for $1,600 (double) under Ala. Code § 35-9A-201(f) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=,sufficient%20compliance%20with%20this%20chapter)). If the landlord did send an itemized list but you dispute the charges, you can sue for the disputed portion of the deposit. Common scenario: landlord claims $200 for “cleaning” and $100 for “yard repair” out of a $500 deposit which you believe is unwarranted – you can sue for the $300 withheld. The judge will decide if the charges were valid. Alabama law allows landlord to deduct for damages beyond normal wear and tear and any unpaid rent or fees – but not to impose arbitrary penalties. Also note: the landlord cannot demand a deposit exceeding one month’s rent (except for pets or changes to premises) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=,obligations%20under%20a%20rental%20agreement)), but that’s more a rule limiting deposit size; it doesn’t directly let you recover if you willingly paid more (unless you argue the contract was illegal). To win your deposit case, **show that you gave the landlord a forwarding address in writing** (required for the double-damages penalty to apply – if you didn’t provide an address, the landlord fulfills obligations by mailing to last known address) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=,a%20period%20of%2090%20days)). Bring proof of move-out date and any communications. If you’re pursuing the double penalty, cite the statute and point out the 60-day violation. Small claims is ideal for these cases since deposit amounts are usually well under $6k. Landlords often refund quickly after being served with the lawsuit, but if not, the court can award the deposit plus penalty and court costs. Keep in mind the statute of limitations: this would likely be viewed as arising from the lease contract, so potentially 6 years (written contract) or 6 years from when refund due, but practically, file within a year or two while evidence is fresh.

- **Refund of prepaid rent or fees:** Yes – if you prepaid last month’s rent or other charges and it wasn’t applied or refunded after you moved out, you can claim it. Example: you paid first and last month’s rent upfront; you properly gave notice and moved, but the landlord never applied that “last month” and is essentially holding an extra month’s rent. That money is owed back to you. Similarly, if you paid a pet fee or parking fee that was supposed to be refundable and it wasn’t returned, you can sue. These are straightforward breach of lease claims. Use the lease clauses and payment receipts as evidence. Often, these issues get rolled into deposit disputes – e.g. the landlord might try to count your last-month prepayment as a “deposit” or vice versa. The law in Alabama doesn’t explicitly address prepaid rent refunds beyond stating that deposits (which could include prepaid rent if defined as such) are limited to one month’s rent ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=,obligations%20under%20a%20rental%20agreement)). Just clearly articulate what funds you gave and what happened. If the amount is small (a few hundred), consider sending a written demand first referencing that you’ll pursue a small claim (some landlords will promptly pay when faced with that).

- **“Repair and deduct” situations / habitability disputes:** Alabama law **requires landlords to keep rentals habitable** (heat, water, structural integrity, etc.) ([tenant-rights.com](https://tenant-rights.com/alabama/applying-rent-deduction-for-repairs-in-alabama-renter-s-guide#:~:text=Understanding%20Alabama%E2%80%99s%20Law%20on%20Rental,Repairs)), but unlike some states, Alabama does *not* explicitly give tenants the right to unilaterally repair and deduct from rent. The proper legal remedy under the Alabama Uniform Residential Landlord-Tenant Act is: if a landlord fails to fix a serious condition affecting health and safety **within 14 days of written notice**, the tenant can terminate the lease and move out (if the issue materially affects health/safety) – see Ala. Code § 35-9A-401. However, *withholding rent or repairing and deducting* is not clearly authorized by statute. That said, many tenants do it out of necessity. In small claims, this typically comes up as a **counterclaim or defense** if a landlord sues the tenant for unpaid rent and the tenant argues “I withheld rent because you didn’t fix the AC, and I had to spend $300 on repairs myself.” Alabama courts might still allow the tenant to offset rent by the amount of reasonable repair costs in an extreme case, under general contract principles (the landlord materially breached by not providing habitable premises). If **you** are bringing a claim as a tenant for expenses you incurred due to the landlord’s failure to repair (e.g. you paid $200 for pest control that the landlord should have done, or you had to buy space heaters because the furnace broke), you can try to recover those costs. It’s a bit of a gray area – there’s no specific statute saying you can recover “repair and deduct” costs, but you’d argue the landlord breached the lease and you’re entitled to damages (the money you spent) as a result. Provide proof you gave the landlord notice of the problem and a chance to fix it, proof that it was not fixed timely, and receipts for what you paid. Another angle: *diminished value of rental* – if conditions were bad, a tenant might claim a partial rent abatement. This is tricky to quantify and not explicitly provided for in Alabama, but a judge might be sympathetic if, for instance, the tenant lived without working heat for a month in winter – they might rule the tenant owed less rent or even that the landlord owes the tenant for the hardship (though usually it’s just a reduction of rent owed). In summary, **habitability issues** can be raised in small claims, but often as a defense to a rent claim. As an affirmative claim, you’re essentially suing for damages due to the landlord’s violation of the lease or law. There’s no statutory penalty in Alabama for “bad faith failure to repair” aside from allowing lease termination. So you’d stick to actual costs incurred. If conditions were awful and caused you to leave, you might also sue for moving costs or difference in rent at a new place – but those may push beyond small claims complexity. Pick concrete monetary losses to claim.

- **Former landlord suing a tenant for property damages or unpaid rent:** Yes – small claims is the right venue for landlords to pursue ex-tenants for money, as long as it’s ≤ $6k. We’ve discussed unpaid rent above. For **property damage**, the landlord should follow the deposit law as mentioned. But sometimes damages exceed the deposit significantly. Landlords can sue for any **physical damage, cleanup costs, or other losses** caused by the tenant. Example: tenant skipped out and left the unit full of trash; you paid $800 for cleaning and hauling – you can sue for that. Or tenant’s pet destroyed the carpet; replacing it cost $2,000 – sue for that (minus any deposit you kept). Landlords can also claim any **unpaid utilities** the tenant was supposed to pay (if the lease says tenant pays final power bill and they didn’t, and it fell to you, include that). Be sure to *credit the tenant for any deposit you did keep* – the court will expect that. Also, a landlord **must mitigate damages** – you can’t let a unit sit empty for months and then charge the old tenant for all that lost rent unless you made reasonable efforts to re-rent. If you’re charging for **remaining lease term rent**, note that once you retake possession, you should try to re-rent; you can only charge the tenant for the vacant period that was reasonable. In small claims, large future rent claims are less common (those would exceed $6k or require bringing an eviction case in district court concurrently). But for a few months of leftover rent, you could pursue it here. Keep documentation (lease, photos of damage, receipts for repairs). Expect tenants to counterclaim for deposit or argue your claims are excessive. The judge will sort it out.

- **Wrongful retention of tenant’s belongings (landlord didn’t let tenant retrieve property):** Yes – if a landlord retained or disposed of a tenant’s personal property improperly, the tenant can sue for the value. Alabama’s landlord-tenant act says any of a tenant’s property **left behind** is considered abandoned **after 14 days** from tenancy termination (or abandonment), *or* if the landlord provides written notice of intent to dispose and 10 days pass, then the landlord can dispose of it. If the landlord tossed your stuff *earlier* than allowed or without notice, that could be wrongful. There’s no automatic multiplier (unlike some states). You just claim the fair value. Example: landlord locked you out and refused to let you get your furniture, eventually selling or destroying it – you could sue for the market value of that furniture. Provide as much detail as possible of what items and their value (photos from before, receipts, etc.). If the landlord believed it was abandoned, they might cite the law’s 14-day rule. If you didn’t actually abandon (e.g. you were only away for a weekend), that’s clearly wrongful. If you had moved out but simply couldn’t carry all stuff immediately and the landlord pitched it on day 2, that’s wrongful under the statute timeline. Small claims can sort out these facts. Note: If the value of belongings is sentimental but low market value, understand the court usually only awards market value. Keep the claim realistic and within evidence.

- **Disputes over lease fees (late fees, cleaning fees, pet fees, etc.):** Yes – either party can bring this up. If you’re a tenant who was charged some fee you believe is illegal or unfair (e.g. a $300 “redecoration fee” that wasn’t in the lease, or excessive late fees that violate a cap), you can sue to recover what you paid. Alabama doesn’t have a strict cap on late fees in the statute – but fees must not be unconscionable. If it’s in the lease you signed, courts often enforce it unless it’s exorbitant. If the landlord withheld part of your deposit for a **cleaning fee** and you dispute that you left the place unclean, that typically falls under deposit disputes (small claims judge will decide if the cleaning charge was warranted). If a **pet fee** was supposed to be refundable (some do a pet *deposit*), you can claim it like any deposit. If it was a non-refundable pet *fee*, you likely can’t get it back unless the lease was unclear. Likewise, if a landlord or apartment charged unlawful fees (like a lease processing fee not disclosed), a small claims judge could deem it improper and order a refund. On the flip side, a landlord could sue a tenant for unpaid **late fees or other charges** if provided in lease – but often those amounts are small and sometimes landlords roll them into a rent claim. Provide the lease clause and evidence of payment or charges to back your stance. This is generally contract interpretation – small claims judges will use common sense; truly unreasonable fees might not be enforced. 

### Employment & Wage Disputes

*(Note: Wage claims can sometimes be handled by government agencies – Alabama itself has limited state wage laws, so many workers rely on the U.S. Department of Labor for minimum wage/overtime issues. But a straightforward claim for earned wages can be brought in small claims as a breach of contract.)*

- **Unpaid wages or final paycheck:** Yes – if your employer (or former employer) didn’t pay you for work you performed, you can absolutely sue them in small claims for the amount owed (up to $6k). This includes situations like not receiving your final paycheck, missing regular pay, or unauthorized paycheck deductions. Alabama does not have a state labor department that handles individual wage claims (the Alabama Dept. of Labor directs wage/hour complaints to the U.S. Dept. of Labor ([adol.alabama.gov](https://adol.alabama.gov/faq/i-have-questions-about-pay-unpaid-wages-breaks-vacation-pay-fmla-etc/#:~:text=I%20have%20questions%20about%20pay%2C,7450%20or%20www.dol.gov%2Fagencies%2Fwhd))). There’s also no state law requiring immediate payment of final wages by a certain date (unlike, say, California’s waiting time penalties). So your remedy is either through the federal Fair Labor Standards Act (if minimum wage or overtime is involved) or simply a civil suit. In small claims, it usually boils down to: *you worked, you earned $X, and the employer didn’t pay.* Provide whatever evidence possible: pay stubs, time sheets, texts/emails where the boss acknowledges you’re owed money, etc. If you quit or were fired, note the date and that pay cycle. Under federal law (FLSA), if it’s a covered job, you are entitled to minimum wage and overtime; and if you sue under FLSA and win, the court can award double the unpaid wages as **liquidated damages** plus attorney’s fees. In small claims, since you likely won’t have an attorney, attorney fees don’t matter, but you *could* ask for the FLSA liquidated damages (basically 2× wages) for a clear wage violation. That might push above $6k if wages are high, so watch the total. Alternatively, you can just sue for the straight amount owed – it’s often simpler and closer to the contract. There’s no requirement to file a federal claim first; FLSA allows you to sue in state court. If your wage claim is more about a **promise** (e.g. a promised bonus or a commission that wasn’t paid), that’s a contract claim rather than FLSA. You’d claim the amount promised. Alabama’s general 6-year statute for written contracts or 6-year for unwritten (under $20) could apply – but practically, don’t wait years; wage claims should be filed within 2 or 3 years at most (FLSA has a 2-year standard, 3-year if willful). If you’re a **1099 independent contractor** not paid for your work, that’s also a contract claim you can bring (just because it’s “wages” in lay terms doesn’t involve labor laws – it’s simply money owed for services). Small business owners often use small claims to chase unpaid invoices for services they personally provided, which is similar. 

- **Unpaid overtime or minimum wage claims:** Yes – these are part of unpaid wage claims. If your employer didn’t pay overtime (time-and-a-half for hours over 40 in a week) and you were a non-exempt employee, or they paid below minimum wage, you can claim the shortfall. Example: you regularly worked 50 hours/week on a fixed salary that worked out to less than minimum wage or with no overtime premium – you can claim the additional amount you should have received under law. Calculate it clearly (e.g. “I am owed 10 hours/week of overtime at $15/hr for 10 weeks = $1,500”). Federal law covers this (FLSA), and as mentioned, you could ask for **liquidated (double) damages**. But if including double pushes you over $6k, you might waive the doubling or file in a higher court. Many employees might instead file a complaint with the U.S. Dept. of Labor Wage & Hour Division (which is free and can investigate multiple employees’ claims). However, that process can be slow. Small claims is faster if the amount is moderate and you have evidence. Note: In Alabama, some specific categories of workers (like **domestic workers, farm laborers, etc.**) may be exempt from overtime laws – check FLSA exemptions. If you sue a sophisticated employer in small claims, they might argue you’re exempt. If you truly are (say, you were a salaried manager meeting certain criteria), then you wouldn’t win overtime – so be sure you were legally entitled to it. But many wage issues in small claims are straightforward (e.g. you’re an hourly worker and they just didn’t pay final hours or OT). There’s no special state law beyond federal on this, so no additional state penalties. 

- **Unpaid commissions or bonuses:** Yes – if you earned a commission or bonus per an agreement and the employer didn’t pay, you can sue for that. For example, you sold a big project that entitled you to a $2,000 commission, but after you left the company they refuse to pay it. Alabama generally treats commissions as wages once earned, but because Alabama doesn’t have a special wage payment law, it falls to contract. Was there a written commission plan? Present it. If not, show past practice (e.g. “I always got 5% on deals – here are previous paycheck stubs showing commissions”). The main issue is whether the commission was *earned* under the terms (some plans require you to be employed at time of payment, etc.). Small claims will interpret the contract. Likewise for bonuses: if it was clearly promised (like “If our store hits $1M sales, you get $500 bonus” and it did, but no bonus paid), that’s enforceable. If it was discretionary (“year-end bonus if performance is good”), that’s harder to force unless you have it in writing. There’s no extra penalty for withheld commissions in Alabama law, but sometimes commissions are subject to **wage payment statutes** in other states – not here, though. So you just claim the amount. Ensure you’re suing the correct entity – e.g. if you worked for “ABC Corp”, sue that corp and serve its registered agent. Some people mistakenly sue their manager – but legally the company owes the commission (unless you were under a direct contract with an individual). 

- **Unreimbursed business expenses:** Yes – if your employer owed you reimbursement for expenses (mileage, purchases, travel) and didn’t pay, you can sue for those just like unpaid wages. Example: you spent $300 on work supplies on your personal card with the promise of reimbursement, but never got repaid. That’s a straightforward claim. Provide receipts and any company policy or emails approving the expenses. Alabama law doesn’t mandate expense reimbursement generally (unlike California, for instance); it depends on employer policy or agreement. But if it was agreed or obviously business-related, you have a strong case. Also, if your employer improperly deducted something from your pay (like charging you for a uniform or customers’ dine-and-dash, putting you below minimum wage), that can be claimed as an unlawful deduction – frame it as wages owed back to you. 

- **Employee misclassification (as contractor) disputes:** This usually comes up in context of overtime or benefits. If you were misclassified as an independent contractor when you were actually an employee, you might claim things like overtime or even lost benefits. However, small claims is not really equipped to formally rule on “misclassification” beyond deciding if you were owed money. For overtime, the court can implicitly decide you were a statutory employee and owed overtime. For benefits (like you would’ve had health insurance or workers’ comp), those are not given as damages in small claims. Small claims can’t, for example, make the company grant you stock options you missed or retroactively enroll you in insurance, since that’s not a money judgment. So usually the issue is unpaid overtime or tax withholdings. If you ended up paying self-employment tax because of misclassification, that’s not directly recoverable from the employer via small claims (that would be a tax issue). Focus on any wages shorted. In sum: you can raise it to justify why you are owed overtime or min wage (like “They called me a contractor but controlled my hours, so I should get overtime like an employee”). The court will consider the substance – but be prepared with evidence of the degree of control, etc. These can get legally complex, but for a small dollar figure the court may take a practical approach if it feels you were essentially an employee.

- **Better forum – Department of Labor or others:** The Alabama Department of Labor does not process individual wage claims for unpaid work (they handle things like unemployment). The U.S. Department of Labor (federal) is the avenue for minimum wage/overtime or FMLA issues, and the National Labor Relations Board for collective labor issues, etc. If your wage issue is part of a larger pattern affecting many employees, or if you feel uncomfortable confronting the employer in court, you might contact the federal Wage & Hour Division. But that can take a while and they may pursue only statutory wages, not things like commissions or expense reimbursements. **Small claims is often the quickest way for an individual to get their money** – you have control of the case and can get a judgment possibly within a couple of months. One caution: if your employer is **likely to retaliate** (and you still work there), filing a suit could strain things. But if you’re already gone, that’s less a concern. If the employer declares bankruptcy, that complicates collection (you’d become a creditor in bankruptcy court, which small claims can’t override – you’d have to file a claim in bankruptcy). Also note, if your claim is for **fringe benefits** like owed vacation pay, check the company policy – in Alabama, employers aren’t required by law to pay out unused vacation unless their policy or contract says so. If your employer promised to pay unused PTO in writing and hasn’t, you can claim it as contract. No statutory penalty if they don’t, though (again, unlike some states). 

### Personal Services & Professional Services Disputes

- **Hairdressers / Barbers / Nail Salons (botched services):** Yes – if a personal care service went awry and cost you money to fix or caused damage, you can sue for a refund and related costs. Example: a hair stylist chemically damaged your hair requiring $300 in treatments to repair – you might sue for the $150 you paid + $300 fix = $450. Or a nail salon cut you badly leading to a doctor visit for infection ($200). Normally these are negligence or breach of contract claims. Alabama doesn’t have a specific statute for “bad haircut” or anything – it’s handled as ordinary negligence. If you suffered a physical injury (like a burn or cut), you could also include pain and suffering in a higher court, but in small claims you’re limited to monetary losses (medical bills, etc.). It might be better to stick to tangible costs because evaluating pain in small claims is difficult. Provide pictures of the damage, receipts for corrective treatments, or doctor’s notes if applicable. Often, bringing such a claim gets the salon to offer a settlement to avoid bad publicity. Note: If the damage is purely dissatisfaction with style (not objectively “wrong”), you may not win – small claims won’t compensate for subjective unhappiness unless the result clearly violated what was agreed (like you asked for a trim and they shaved your head – that’s clearly not what you consented to). If an expensive procedure (like hair coloring or straightening) was done incorrectly and you had to pay someone else to redo it, that cost is recoverable. Make sure to give the original salon a chance to fix it or refund – if you march straight to court without at least requesting a refund, the judge might question that. But legally not required, just practical.

- **Tutors, instructors, coaches not delivering lessons:** Yes – if you paid for lessons or training sessions and the person didn’t provide them (or did far fewer than promised), you can sue for the unused portion of your money. Example: you paid a private tutor $500 in advance for 10 sessions, and they only did 4 sessions then quit – you could seek the remaining $300 value. Or if a music teacher significantly failed to deliver what was promised (like you paid for a certain curriculum but got something else), it’s a breach of contract. These often hinge on whether there was a written agreement on number of sessions or outcomes. Provide any emails or receipts that show what was expected. If it’s just dissatisfaction with quality (“I didn’t learn anything”), that’s harder – the service was rendered, even if not to your liking. But outright non-performance or cancellation by the instructor is straightforward. Alabama has no special licensing or consumer law on private tutors – it’s just a contract. If the provider is a business (like a tutoring center or swim school), check your enrollment agreement – it might have a no-refund policy or some cancellation terms. Small claims can still consider fairness, but a clear contract clause might limit you. In general, you’ll more likely recover if you didn’t receive lessons you paid for, versus wanting a refund because you didn’t improve as expected.

- **Pet boarding, grooming, or training disputes:** Yes – if you paid for pet services and something went wrong financially, you can sue. Example: a dog trainer charged $800 for a month-long training but only provided one week then disappeared – sue for the unused $600. Or a pet boarding kennel lost your pet’s expensive bed or returned your dog with injuries due to negligence (vet bills incurred). These involve contract and possibly negligence. If your pet was injured or died due to the service provider’s negligence, you can recover the **economic value of the pet or vet costs**. Unfortunately, emotional distress over pet injury is generally not compensable in Alabama (pets are property legally). But any vet bills to treat issues caused by the service are recoverable. If a groomer nicked your dog causing a $200 vet visit, that’s claimable. Ensure you have proof like vet records saying the injury likely occurred during grooming. If an actual contract or intake form was signed at the facility, see if it included liability waivers. Many kennels have you sign something like “we are not liable for injury or death of pet from inherent risks.” Such waivers can complicate things but may not cover clear negligence. Small claims judges will look at basic fairness. Provide receipts of payment for the service and any additional costs you incurred due to their failing. Also any communications (texts saying “sorry I only did one week but can’t continue” – which helps you prove lack of service). 

- **Medical or dental billing disputes (when not covered by insurance or outside surprise billing laws):** Possibly – small claims can handle straightforward billing disagreements with healthcare providers, but many such disputes are complicated by insurance and regulatory laws. However, scenarios like: you were **overcharged** beyond the quoted price for an elective procedure, or you *self-paid* for a treatment that was done incorrectly and you had to pay another doctor to fix it – these can be brought as contract or negligence claims. Example: a dentist charged you for 3 fillings but only filled 2 teeth – you can sue for the cost of the third filling not provided. Or you paid $1,000 for a cosmetic procedure that was not done per agreed plan, requiring a redo elsewhere for $1,000 – you could attempt to recover your loss. Be aware: if it involves **medical malpractice** (negligent treatment causing injury), those cases usually exceed small claims and require expert testimony – not suitable for small claims. But if it’s essentially a **fee dispute** or a **service not delivered** issue, it’s viable. Alabama has adopted the federal **No Surprises Act**, which protects patients from surprise out-of-network bills in emergencies and requires providers to give good faith estimates for self-pay patients. If you got a surprise bill that falls under that law, the resolution is through an independent dispute resolution system, not small claims. But small claims could be used for, say, enforcing a **good faith estimate** if the final bill wildly exceeds it without justification. For example, if a provider gave a $2,000 estimate and then billed $5,000, you might bring that up as a contract-type issue under the NSA’s spirit, but technically the NSA process is outside small claims. If you do try in small claims, present the written estimate. Also some hospital billing errors (double charges etc.) can be resolved by a small claims suit if they won’t listen otherwise. Be mindful: medical offices may send bills to collections, and sometimes patients file small claims to contest a debt and get clarity. The judge can indeed adjudicate whether a charge must be paid or not. Summon the provider or collections to court with your evidence of why the charge is incorrect (insurance EOB, etc.). There’s no special pre-suit step required (other than perhaps writing to the provider disputing the bill, which is recommended). Summaries: use small claims for clear-cut money disputes with healthcare providers under $6k, but not for complex malpractice injuries.

- **Daycare or preschool refunds:** Yes – if you paid a daycare, preschool, or summer camp and it closed early or you withdrew properly but didn’t get a due refund, you can sue for that money. Example: you paid $1,200 for three months of daycare, but after one month you moved out-of-state and daycare promised a partial refund that never came – you can claim the unused portion if their contract allowed for cancellation. Or if a daycare expelled your child unfairly and kept the fees. Look at your enrollment agreement: many have notice requirements or non-refundable clauses. If the daycare breached its own terms (e.g. they closed for COVID for a month and didn’t refund lunches you paid for), that could be argued. Alabama doesn’t have a unique statute for daycare refunds; it’s contract law and maybe consumer protection if there was deception. Ensure you gave any required notice of withdrawal. Bring proof of payment and the contract.
