If it was a *deposit* to hold a spot and it was supposed to be refundable (some require say a one-week deposit and will refund if you give 2 weeks’ notice), include that. Conversely, if the contract says “absolutely no refunds,” you might argue equity if the service wasn’t provided at all. Judges sometimes award refunds despite such clauses if it’s clearly unjust (like they never even started providing care). Also, if a daycare was shut down for licensing issues and you lost money, that’s a strong case – they couldn’t legally provide the service, so you should get your money back. 

### Loans, Debts, and Financial Disputes Among Individuals

- **Money lent to friends or family:** Yes – a personal loan to someone is one of the most typical small claims. If you lent someone money and they haven’t repaid as agreed, you can sue for the balance due (principal + any interest if you explicitly agreed on interest). Example: you loaned a friend $2,000 to help with rent, with a verbal promise “I’ll pay you back in 3 months,” and it’s been a year – sue for $2,000 plus maybe some nominal interest (Alabama’s legal interest rate is 6% if you choose to claim interest for a loan, but if no interest was discussed, you might just claim principal to keep it simple ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-8/chapter-8/section-8-8-1/#:~:text=Alabama%20Code%20%C2%A7%208,or%20for%20a%20longer%20or))). Provide any evidence: a casual IOU note, text messages “I’ll pay you next month,” or even testimony that you gave the money. If you withdrew cash to give them, a bank statement of that can help corroborate. In small claims, a lot comes down to credibility – if you have some documentation, it strengthens your case. Alabama’s statute of limitations on an unwritten loan is 6 years if it’s considered a contract (2 years if considered a quasi-contract unjust enrichment – but courts generally treat it as a debt contract). If it’s been more than 6 years, you might be out of luck. Lawsuits can strain relationships, but that’s beyond the legal realm – legally, you have every right if it was a loan, not a gift. Be prepared for the defendant possibly to claim “It was a gift” or come up with some excuse. Clear evidence like a note that says “I promise to repay” will sink them. In absence of writing, your testimony that you expected repayment and any partial payments made by them (even small or one time) is great proof it was a loan. If they made a payment or even promised again in writing within the last 6 years, the clock can renew.

- **Shared expenses among roommates or ex-partners:** Yes – disputes over splitting bills, living expenses, or jointly purchased items are common. Example: you and a roommate agreed to split utilities but they moved out and didn’t pay their $300 share of the last power bill – sue for $300. Or you and a significant other went on vacation and you covered the whole cost on the understanding they’d pay half ($1,000) but they never did – you can claim $500. These are essentially oral contracts (or implied contracts) to share costs. They can be a bit he-said/she-said, so evidence helps: any texts like “I’ll pay you back for my half” or Venmo requests that went unanswered, etc. If multiple expenses are involved, itemize them in court (e.g. “We agreed to split rent $700 each; I paid full $1,400 for May and June, so I want her $700 for May and $700 for June”). Judges see many roommate cases and typically enforce fair splits if an agreement can be shown. If no explicit agreement, the court might still apply an equitable sharing (common law calls it “quantum meruit” or unjust enrichment). For example, if one roommate just ghosted without paying and you had to pay everything to avoid eviction, a judge will likely make them reimburse their share for the time they lived there. Keep the claims limited to actual money out of pocket. Also, small claims might not handle complex property division (like one party kept the couch; see next bullet). But pure debt splitting is fine.

- **Disputes over jointly owned or jointly purchased property (after breakup or friendship ending):** These can be tricky because small claims can’t directly divide property or enforce transfer of title, but it can award money to compensate one party. If you and an ex bought a TV together for $1,000, and after breaking up one person kept the TV, the other might claim $500 (their half of value). Small claims can adjudicate that by essentially treating it as a **partition of personal property** issue: either ordering one to pay the other for their share. It’s cleaner if one party clearly took the item – then the other sues for the value of their share. For example, a couple splits up and one keeps the $5,000 car that was in both names – the co-owner could sue for $2,500 (half the equity) if that was the understanding. Alabama doesn’t have a specific statute for unmarried partners’ property, so it’s general equity and contract. If there was any written agreement (even texts like “you keep the couch, you owe me $300 for it”), bring that. Otherwise, present evidence of the joint purchase (receipts showing both contributed). The judge will try to reach a fair monetary resolution. Keep in mind the $6k limit – if the property is worth more, small claims might not fully resolve it. Also, small claims won’t change a title (for instance, if both names are on a car title, small claims can’t reassign it to one – you’d need to handle that through DMV once you agree who gets it). They’ll just deal in money. If the dispute is about **pets** acquired together, note that Alabama treats pets as property too – an ex could sue if you kept the dog they paid for, but usually courts prefer to not wade into “custody” of pets in small claims. They might value the pet and give one side money if appropriate. But often people want the pet, not money – small claims cannot order “give the dog to X.” Only money judgments. So those are usually not good small claims cases unless both just treat it as a monetary issue.

- **Wedding or engagement gifts and rings when engagement is canceled:** Yes – Alabama follows the general rule that an **engagement ring is a conditional gift**: if the marriage doesn’t occur, the ring should be returned to the giver (condition being marriage) *unless* the giver unjustifiably broke the engagement. If you were the giver of an engagement ring and your fiancée broke off the engagement, you can sue to get the ring back or its value. Small claims can handle the value if it’s $6,000 or less (some rings are more, which would require higher court). If the ring is still in their possession, you might sue for “return of specific property” (replevin) – Alabama small claims **does allow suits for specific personal property** (there’s even a form “Statement of Claim for Specific Property” (Form SM-02) ([russell.alacourt.gov](https://russell.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=To%20Download%20Small%20Claims%20forms%3A%C2%A0Click,Specific%20Property))). In that case, the court can order them to return the ring or pay its value. If you use the specific property route, be sure the item’s value is within $6k. Alternatively, sue for the value as money. Likewise, if an engagement was mutually broken and the ring not returned, legally it should go back to giver – small claims can enforce that. For **wedding expenses or gifts**: if the wedding was canceled and you, for example, had paid $2,000 towards a venue and the couple agreed to split the loss but the other didn’t pay, you can pursue that as a shared expense (as above). Or if you gave a **significant gift** (like $1,000 to help them buy a house) and it was premised on them getting married which didn’t happen, that’s murkier – gifts aren’t usually recoverable unless conditional. Engagement rings are specifically seen as conditional; other gifts likely not unless explicitly said “This is if you two marry.” Use caution. For **wedding rings after a short marriage**: If they actually married then split, the engagement ring is no longer conditional (the condition was met by marriage). It’s now typically the spouse’s property (a completed gift). So small claims wouldn’t override that. But if fighting over other property or money after a marriage, that becomes a divorce property issue – outside small claims. So we’re only talking pre-marriage breakups. Provide proof of cost or an appraisal for the ring if you sue for value. Alabama case law supports return to the donor if donor wasn’t at fault for breakup ([www.troymessenger.com](https://www.troymessenger.com/2015/08/05/small-claims-court-increases-claim-amount-limitations/#:~:text=Small%20business%20owners%20and%20others,3%2C000%20jurisdictional%20limitation%20any%20longer)).

- **Shared debts or credit card bills:** If you and someone else agreed to split a debt and one didn’t pay their share, yes you can sue. E.g. you co-signed a loan (discussed earlier under guarantors) or you jointly took out a credit card and you ended up paying it all – you can attempt to get the other’s share. These are similar to co-signer scenarios: you’d show the agreement or understanding that each was responsible for half. The complicator is many such arrangements aren’t in writing except the credit contract with the bank (which holds both liable for full amount). But between the two of you, maybe there was an understanding. If you have texts like “I’ll pay you back for my half of the Visa bill,” that seals it. Otherwise, you’ll need to convince the judge of the informal agreement’s existence. If it’s a large debt, consider that the other person might not have the means to pay anyway (a judgment is only as good as collection). But legally, yes you can bring it.

- **Recovery of deposits or earnest money in deals between individuals:** Yes – if you gave a cash deposit to someone for a purchase (car, boat, etc.) and the deal fell through, small claims can help you get it back if warranted. Example: you put down a $500 earnest money deposit to buy a used car from an individual, but then the sale didn’t happen (either you backed out or they did). Whether you can recover depends on the circumstances and any written terms. If *they* backed out or the sale failed through no fault of yours, typically you get your deposit back. If *you* backed out without a contingency, the seller might try to keep it as liquidated damages. Alabama allows reasonable liquidated damages if agreed. If nothing was written, the court will decide based on fairness – often, earnest money is returned if the seller can easily sell the item to someone else shortly after. In real estate, earnest money is often governed by a contract with specific terms (but real estate likely exceeds $6k). For small deals, present any bill of sale notes or communications. If you are the buyer wanting it back, stress that the other party didn’t incur losses and is unjustly enriched by keeping your money. If you are the seller who kept a deposit and you get sued, be prepared to show how you lost opportunities or took the item off market. That said, rarely do sellers sue in small claims to *keep* a deposit – they just keep it and wait to see if the buyer sues. Many times the threat of a small claim will prompt return of deposit. Legally, absent a clear contract, courts generally lean toward preventing a windfall to the seller, so they’d likely order the deposit returned minus any actual expenses incurred by the seller due to the failed sale (e.g. listing fees, etc.). 

- **Can banks or creditors sue consumers in small claims?** Yes – original creditors like banks, credit unions, hospitals, etc., can use small claims court to collect debts from consumers (and they often do for smaller debts). Alabama does not prohibit businesses from being plaintiffs in small claims; in fact, the law explicitly allows partnerships and corporations to appear and sue in small claims ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,time%20employee%20of%20the%20corporation)). The only restriction is if the debt has been **assigned** to someone else (like a third-party collection agency) – then that agency must have an attorney to file (they cannot have a non-lawyer agent do it) ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,original%20owner%20of%20the%20claim)). But they **still can file** in small claims, just via an attorney. There’s no outright bar on collection suits. There is also no limit on how many collection suits a company can file (some states restrict high-volume filers, Alabama doesn’t beyond the attorney rule). So yes, credit card companies, medical providers, landlords, etc. regularly sue in small claims for debts up to $6,000. If you are a consumer wanting to sue *them* (maybe for wrongful debt collection or a refund), you can as well – but that often falls under either contract or consumer protection law (as discussed above). It’s worth noting that Alabama law **requires debt buyers** (those who purchase defaulted accounts) to be represented by attorneys in small claims ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,original%20owner%20of%20the%20claim)) – so if you’re being sued by, say, Portfolio Recovery Associates in small claims, check if an attorney signed the complaint. If not, that may be improper. But from the plaintiff perspective (you suing someone), no issue. If you are an *out-of-state* business wanting to sue an Alabama resident in Alabama small claims, be aware of the **foreign entity registration requirement**: a foreign company “transacting business” in Alabama cannot maintain an action in Alabama courts until it registers with the Alabama Secretary of State ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-10a/chapter-1/article-7/division-c/section-10a-1-7-21/#:~:text=,has%20registered%20in%20this%20state)). So if, for example, you run a Georgia LLC and you want to sue an Alabama client for nonpayment, technically if you were “doing business” in Alabama without authority, your suit could be paused until you register. If it’s a one-off deal not regular business, you might not need to register (the law has exceptions for isolated transactions). But keep this in mind (see Section 13 on business plaintiffs). For individuals, no such issue.

### Vehicle-Specific Issues

- **Accident damage where insurance isn’t covering:** Yes – (addressed above under property damage) if you have a car accident and, for instance, the other driver was at fault but uninsured or their insurer low-balled you, you can sue the driver for the remainder of your damages within $6k. A common scenario: your car’s damage is, say, $7,500; the at-fault driver’s insurance only had minimum property limit of $25,000 so they don’t contest liability but they depreciated some parts and cut the check for $6,000, leaving $1,500 of the repair bill unpaid (or you had that much in deductible/uncovered items). You could sue the at-fault driver for that $1,500 difference in small claims (the insurance will likely pay it once you get judgment, because they owe it under their coverage up to their limit and they prefer to close the claim). Another scenario: You were hit, and their insurer denied the claim saying their driver wasn’t at fault – you can sue the driver directly and let the judge decide fault. If you win, their insurer should pay the judgment (up to policy limits) or the driver will have to if uninsured. Be sure to also claim things like towing or car rental if you paid out of pocket due to the accident. Note: personal injury from accidents is usually more than $6k, but if it was minor (like $1,000 medical bills and you’re fine now), you *could* include that. Usually better to separate property damage and injury claims – property damage is clear and fits small claims well, personal injury might deserve higher court because of contributory negligence rule and need for evidence on injuries. But if it’s truly small and you don’t want a lawyer, you can include it (just realize if any fault is assigned to you, you lose entirely). Also, if you do include an injury claim, Alabama law requires evidence to meet a “greater weight of evidence” (preponderance) for negligence and a **higher threshold (clear and convincing) for any punitive damages** ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2015/title-6/chapter-11/article-2/section-6-11-20/#:~:text=and%20convincing%20evidence%20proven%3B%20definitions,AL%20Code%20%C2%A7)). Likely irrelevant unless the accident was caused by wantonness like DUI (punitive potential). That’s beyond small claims scope in most cases.

- **Disputes with mechanics / body shops:** Yes – (addressed in consumer disputes) if a mechanic did poor work or overcharged, you can sue for the cost to fix their mistakes or the difference in fair price. For body shops: if insurance paid them to do repairs and they did shoddy work leaving your car unsafe or diminished in value, you can sue the shop for the diminished value or additional repairs (but note often insurance warranties the repair – check if your insurer will handle it with the shop first). If a shop kept your car an excessively long time causing you to incur rental fees beyond what insurance covered, you could sue the shop for those extra costs on a theory of breach of implied reasonable time. If a mechanic **damaged your car** (dent, scratch, or mechanical damage) while in their custody, that’s their responsibility too. Bring before/after photos or other evidence. Alabama’s **Motor Vehicle Repair Act** (if any) is minimal – mostly focusing on licensing of shops for certain work but not consumer rights like estimates. So it’s mainly contract and tort law at play.

- **Dealership disputes (sales and repairs):** Yes – many are covered above (defective used car, etc.). Additional examples: **Yo-yo sale** (dealership let you take the car then financing fell through and they demand the car back but you already gave a trade-in and maybe cash deposit) – you could sue for return of your trade-in or deposit if they can’t finalize the deal. Or **undisclosed fees** in a sales contract (maybe they snuck in a $500 service fee you didn’t notice) – small claims can potentially unwind that portion if you prove it was fraudulent. Or if a dealer agreed to pay off your old loan on a trade but didn’t, leaving you on the hook – that’s a breach you could sue for (the amount of the payoff they failed to make). Carefully read your purchase contract; dealerships have arbitration clauses in many cases. Check if your contract says arbitration is required – if so, the dealer might ask the court to dismiss in favor of arbitration. Some arbitration clauses have exceptions for small claims though. If it’s not too big an issue, they might ignore arbitration and fight in small claims. But be aware it exists. For **repairs at a dealership**, it’s same as any mechanic. 

- **Towing and impound disputes:** Yes – if your car was towed and you believe it was illegal or excessive fee, you can sue the towing company or property owner who ordered it. Alabama’s consumer protection on towing is not very strong (Alabama ranks low in towing protections ([www.towinglaws.com](https://www.towinglaws.com/alabama#:~:text=Alabama%20Towing%20Laws%20For%202026,in%20terms%20of%20consumer%20protection))). There’s no state-set fee cap or statutory damages for wrongful towing, but you can sue under general law for **conversion** or **trespass to chattels** if the tow was improper. For example, if a tow company didn’t follow required notice or signage laws and you had to pay $300, you can sue to get that $300 back. Or if they damaged your vehicle during the tow, you can sue for the damage repair cost. Check local ordinances too; some cities might have towing regs. The **Alabama state law** requires tow companies impounding vehicles for law enforcement to notify owners and lienholders (Ala. Code § 32-13-4, etc.), but for private property tows, it’s mostly contract between landowner and tower. If you argue the tow was not legally justified (maybe signage wasn’t posted as required by local law, or you actually had permission to park, etc.), bring evidence: photos of lot with no signage, etc. If the tow company did not provide an itemized bill or charged more than allowed (if any local cap), highlight that. You can also sue for any personal property lost (if items from your car disappeared while in impound). Alabama does criminalize **vehicle tampering** ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2022/title-32/chapter-8/article-4/section-32-8-83/#:~:text=Alabama%20Code%20%C2%A7%2032,2022)) – not directly helpful civilly, but shows a policy that unauthorized messing with a vehicle is wrong. In small claims, you’d likely sue the towing company, possibly also the property owner if they were the ones who directed it (but usually just the tower for fees). If your claim is the car was wrongfully auctioned off, that’s more complex (would involve title issues beyond small claims), but you could sue for the value of the car if they failed to notify you (if under $6k). The statute of limitations is 2 years for property damage or conversion, so act fast.

- **Parking lot damage (hit-and-run with identified culprit):** Yes – if your parked car was hit and you luckily identified the driver (maybe through surveillance or they left a note or witness got plate), you can sue that driver for your repair costs, just like any accident. Often these drivers will pay or their insurance will, but if not, small claims is appropriate. Provide any proof tying them to the incident (e.g. note they left admitting fault, or a witness statement or camera footage). If it’s a private parking lot and you don’t know who hit you, you unfortunately can’t use small claims to magically find them or make the lot owner pay (lot owners generally aren’t liable for random hit and runs). That becomes an insurance issue for collision coverage or uninsured motorist property damage if you have it. But if identified, treat as normal property damage claim.

### Personal Injury (Minor Cases)

- **Minor personal injury claims (within $6k):** Yes – you can sue for injuries like small medical bills, minor pain and suffering, etc., as long as you cap your claim at $6,000. Most personal injury cases (car accidents, slip-and-fall, etc.) involve damages higher than $6k, so they go to regular court (and often involve attorneys). However, there are situations where an injury is relatively small or the injured person chooses not to involve lawyers. Examples: you slipped at a grocery store and sprained your ankle, incurring $1,500 in urgent care and physical therapy – you could file a small claim against the store for those costs (and possibly some token for pain). Or a neighbor’s dog bit you causing a $800 ER visit – small claims can handle that (Alabama’s dog bite statute makes owners liable when bite happens on their property or victim was legally there ([www.animallaw.info](https://www.animallaw.info/statute/al-dog-bitedangerous-animal-liability-owners-dogs-biting-or-injuring-persons#:~:text=Injuring%20Persons.%20,property%20owned%20or%20controlled%20by)); if you were off property, you’d use common law negligence – either way, you can sue). **Be realistic with the $6k limit:** You might have $4k medical bills and want $2k additional for pain and lost wages, that fits $6k. But if you broke a bone or have ongoing issues, that’s usually more valuable than $6k – don’t undersell your claim; consider higher court or settlement. Also note, in small claims you won’t have a jury (Alabama small claims are always bench trials ([marion.alacourt.gov](https://marion.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=The%20Small%20Claims%20Court%20is,the%20judgment%20to%20Circuit%20Court))), and you won’t be able to easily present expert testimony (nor would you usually for a minor injury). The burden is on you to prove the other party was at fault (negligent) and that the accident caused your injuries. Bring any incident reports, photos of the hazard or accident scene, and your medical records and bills (the judge can admit those as evidence of your treatment and costs). Alabama’s strict **contributory negligence** rule applies: if the defendant shows you were even a little negligent, you lose entirely. For instance, if you slipped but a sign was there and you didn’t see it, the store will claim you were partly at fault for not paying attention – if the judge agrees even 1%, your claim is barred. This is a harsh rule, so be prepared to combat any suggestion of your fault. If you win, you can recover **medical costs, lost wages, and possibly a modest amount for pain and suffering**. There’s no formula; small claims judges often give only economic damages, but they can include some general damages if you credibly describe your pain (just remember the $6k max). Punitive damages (to punish) are *not* available in garden-variety negligence (only for wanton or intentional conduct with clear and convincing proof ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2015/title-6/chapter-11/article-2/section-6-11-20/#:~:text=and%20convincing%20evidence%20proven%3B%20definitions,AL%20Code%20%C2%A7)), like a drunk driver case). Even then, the $6k cap limits total recovery. So don’t expect a windfall – focus on actual losses. Statute of limitations for personal injury in Alabama is **2 years** ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/8577/11-47-23.html#:~:text=Section%2011,View%20Our%20Newest%20Version%20Here)) from the injury, so file before then.

### Specific Statutory Claims & State-Specific Causes of Action

*(These are claims created by statute that allow recovery of money, which can often be pursued in small claims if the amount is right.)*

- **Worthless check damages:** As noted earlier, Alabama law (Ala. Code § 6-5-285) allows the payee of a bad check to recover **punitive damages and attorney’s fees** in addition to the check amount ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-6/chapter-5/article-16/section-6-5-285/#:~:text=though%20there%20has%20been%20no,trying%20the%20case%20may%20assess)). While not a fixed multiplier like some states, it gives the court leeway to punish the check writer. In practice, small claims judges might award, say, double the amount if it was a fraud situation and you ask for it. Additionally, under Alabama’s criminal code, writing a bad check can be pursued criminally, but civilly §6-5-285 is your friend to cite. If you pursue a bad-check case in small claims, cite **“Ala. Code § 6-5-285”** in your Statement of Claim or at least bring a copy, showing you are entitled to “such damages, both punitive and compensatory, including a reasonable attorney fee, as the court or jury may assess” ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-6/chapter-5/article-16/section-6-5-285/#:~:text=though%20there%20has%20been%20no,trying%20the%20case%20may%20assess)). That phrase basically empowers the small claims judge to tack on extra. But remember the total still capped at $6k. If it was a huge bounced check above $6k, don’t split it – go to higher court or reduce claim. 

- **Consumer Protection (UDAP) claims:** The **Alabama Deceptive Trade Practices Act (DTPA)** is the main consumer statute (Ala. Code § 8-19-1 et seq.). It covers a wide range of unfair or deceptive acts (false advertising, bait-and-switch, phony sweepstakes, etc.). Under § 8-19-10, a consumer can recover **actual damages or $100, whichever is more, and up to 3× actual damages** for willful unlawful acts ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,100%2C%20whichever%20is%20greater%3B%20or)), plus **costs and reasonable attorney’s fee** if they win ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,reasonable%20attorney%27s%20fees%20and%20costs)). Many UDAP claims (like against big companies) might exceed $6k or be better pursued by an attorney, but small claims is viable for smaller incidents. For example, suppose a furniture store knowingly misrepresented a couch as leather which is actually vinyl, and you paid $2,000 – you could sue under DTPA for $2,000 actual plus potentially an extra $2,000 (making $4k) as punitive *if the court finds it was done intentionally* ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,practices%20were%20committed%20intentionally%3B%20and)), all within the small claims limit. Or a gym that kept charging your credit card after cancellation (deceptive practice) causing you $500 loss – that’s a UDAP claim. If you bring a DTPA claim: be aware of a couple of things – (1) You should send a **written demand** 15 days before filing, allowing the business to cure, to be eligible for attorneys fees under §8-19-8 (the statute encourages pre-suit resolution, although lack of demand doesn’t bar suit, it just affects attorney fee eligibility). Since you likely have no attorney in small claims, that’s moot, but it’s courteous and may resolve it. (2) As mentioned, the clerk will notify the Alabama Attorney General’s Consumer Protection Section about your suit ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,shall%20mail%20a%20copy%20of)) – this doesn’t affect your case except the AG could choose to intervene (rare for a small case). (3) Government entities and most regulated industries (like insurance companies, utilities, etc.) are exempt from the DTPA. And you cannot bring a class action in small claims. So ensure it’s within scope. If successful, you can get at least your actual damages. Getting the “up to triple” is discretionary – emphasize any evidence of the defendant’s intentional wrongdoing or pattern of doing this to others, as those factors are in the statute ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,practices%20were%20committed%20intentionally%3B%20and)). The judge may or may not grant multiples; often they stick to actual in a small case. But asking for it (say, in your complaint you might write “Violation of ADTPA – actual damages $___, request trebling under §8-19-10(a)(2)”) at least preserves the possibility. The **$100 minimum** means even if your actual loss was small, the court can award $100 plus maybe costs. This could apply to a small rip-off like a deceptive ad that cost you $50 – statute says you get $100. That’s still within small claims. Summing up: UDAP claims are allowed and sometimes a powerful tool in small claims, but you must still meet the burden of proof that the act was unlawful under the statute and caused you harm.

- **New car Lemon Law:** Alabama’s **Motor Vehicle Lemon Law** (Ala. Code § 8-20A-1 to 8-20A-6) gives consumers of new vehicles rights to refund or replacement if a serious defect can’t be fixed after a reasonable number of attempts (generally 3 tries or 30 days out of service within 1 year/12,000 miles) ([www.carlemon.com](https://www.carlemon.com/amp/alabama-lemon-law.html#:~:text=Alabama%20Lemon%20Law%20%288,with%20lemon%20vehicles%20may%20enjoy)). Typically, enforcing the lemon law involves going to circuit court or invoking an arbitration program, and the stakes (vehicle’s full price) are beyond small claims. However, small claims could come into play if, for example, your new car had a defect and the manufacturer agreed to repurchase but is quibbling over $500 of fees – you might handle that in small claims if it couldn’t be resolved. But generally, you **will not** use small claims to compel a manufacturer to take back a car or to get a full lemon refund, because that relief is equitable and often exceeds $6k. If you tried, the manufacturer’s lawyer would likely get it moved or dismissed. Instead, small claims could be used for something like: a dealership sold you what should have been a “lemon law buyback” with disclosure, but didn’t disclose – you could then sue under UDAP for that. Or if a “lemon” was fixed but you incurred expenses during the saga (like towing, etc.) that manufacturer didn’t reimburse as they should under the lemon law, and those are under $6k, you could sue for those reimbursements. Lemon law specifically requires the consumer to go through maybe an informal dispute settlement if one is available (many companies have BBB Auto Line arbitration) before suing. If you haven’t done that, the court might throw out a lemon claim. So bottom line: lemon law issues rarely go to small claims except for tangential money claims.

- **Identity theft and fraud losses:** If someone fraudulently used your identity or financial information to your detriment, you might attempt to recover the monetary loss in small claims – but often the perpetrator is unknown or judgment-proof. For instance, if a known individual (maybe an acquaintance or family member) opened a credit card in your name and racked $2,000 in charges, you could sue that person for the $2,000 identity theft damages. You’d have to show they did it (perhaps there was a criminal conviction or clear evidence tying them). Alabama has criminal laws for identity theft ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-13a/chapter-8/article-10/section-13a-8-192/#:~:text=%3A%3A%20U,192%20Identity%20Theft)) but no specific civil statute granting a fixed damage. You’d proceed under general fraud/tort theories. If someone stole your bank info and drained $1,000, and the bank won’t reimburse because it appears “friendly fraud,” you could sue the thief if identifiable. Sometimes people also sue for **credit damage** or similar, but small claims won’t entertain speculative damages like “my credit score dropped.” Stick to out-of-pocket losses. Also consider small claims for **fraudulent schemes**: e.g. you paid $500 to a scam contractor who vanished – that’s essentially theft by deception; you can sue for that money (and you might also report to law enforcement). If the person is caught, a criminal court might order restitution, but you don’t have to wait – a civil small claim can be faster to get a judgment (though collecting from a scammer may be tough). For **consumer identity protection**, Alabama law (Ala. Code § 8-19C) allows you to place a security freeze on credit, but that doesn’t give money – it’s preventative. Any money claim from identity fraud goes through normal civil channels. So yes, small claims is possible if you have someone to hold accountable by name.

- **Statutory wage penalties:** Alabama notably does *not* have things like a “waiting time penalty” for late paychecks (as California does), or a state overtime penalty. We addressed wages in Employment section – basically no extra damages beyond actual wages (except federal doubling for willful FLSA violations). Alabama also doesn’t have a separate state *minimum wage* (it defaults to the federal $7.25). So, there aren’t additional state statutory damage provisions for wages to cite in Alabama small claims, other than local laws if any (e.g., Birmingham attempted a local minimum wage once but state preempted it). So in small claims wage cases, you’re looking at contract and FLSA.

- **Other miscellaneous Alabama statutes:** Alabama has various specific statutes that could come up in small disputes. For instance:
    - **Home solicitation sales:** If you bought something from a door-to-door seller and they didn’t honor the 3-day cancellation rights under Alabama law, you could seek your refund (Ala. Code § 8-19-5 addresses door-to-door sales as an unlawful act under DTPA if not following rules).
    - **Veterans’ benefits payday loan protections:** Alabama limits interest on loans to veterans on benefits – if violated, maybe small claims can undo interest.
    - **Ticket sales:** Alabama forbids ticket scalping above $5 over face value (Ala. Code § 8-19E-3). If you were overcharged for a football ticket by a reseller, that’s technically illegal. Could you sue to recover the overcharge? Possibly, as a statutory illegal contract; though enforcement is usually by AG, not individuals. But maybe under DTPA as a deceptive practice.
    - **Tenant rights to utilities:** If a landlord willfully interrupts essential utilities to force you out (illegal self-eviction), Ala. Code § 35-9A-424 gives a tenant the right to sue for actual damages plus attorneys’ fees. In small claims, you could use that – say the landlord cut power for a week, you had to spend $300 living elsewhere, you sue for that $300. The statute doesn’t provide a *penalty* beyond actual damages and fees, and in small claims you likely have no attorney fee to recover. But the statute’s existence bolsters your claim that the landlord’s act was unlawful.
    - **Automobile “diminished value”**: Alabama allows claims for diminished value of a vehicle after an accident (if repaired but worth less). If an insurance company refuses to pay that, you could bring it as a claim (though often requires an expert appraisal, which is challenging in small claims).
    - **HOA fines or condo disputes:** If an HOA fines you improperly contrary to bylaws (e.g. without notice or for a rule that doesn’t exist) and puts it on your account, you might pay and then sue the HOA for a refund. Or if you as an HOA sue an owner for unpaid dues under $6k, that can be done in small claims (though HOAs typically go to district or circuit). If you’re the homeowner, you could also use small claims to challenge certain charges if you went ahead and paid but dispute them.
    - **Predatory lending / usury:** Alabama caps interest at 8% by contract (except for certain licensed lenders) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-8/chapter-8/section-8-8-1/#:~:text=2025%20Code%20of%20Alabama%20%3A%3A,or%20for%20a%20longer%20or)). If you fell victim to a predatory unlicensed loan with 200% interest and you paid, you might sue to recover the usurious interest as unlawful enrichment. This is complex but small claims could see it as voiding interest over 8% and order a refund of excess. If it’s a payday loan or title loan, those are regulated by separate statutes (which allow high interest but with licenses). If the lender was unlicensed, that’s illegal and you can claim under DTPA or general illegality.
    - **Surprise medical billing:** Federal law covers it, as mentioned, with an arbitration process (so small claims might not be proper for those if provider invokes that). 
    - **Telemarketing or debt collection abuse:** The federal Telephone Consumer Protection Act (TCPA) gives $500 per unwanted call/text statutory damages – interestingly, some people do file pro se small claims under TCPA. TCPA is a federal claim but can be in state court. If you got 10 spam texts from a known company, you could claim $5,000 (10×$500) in small claims. The company might remove it to federal court, but maybe not for such small sum. Another is the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act (FDCPA) – federal law gives up to $1,000 statutory damages for harassment by a debt collector, plus any actual damages. You *can* file FDCPA claims in state court. If you feel a debt collector violated your rights (e.g. called 50 times a day or cursed at you), you could sue for up to $1,000 under FDCPA. Many folks do this in small claims to avoid needing an attorney, though FDCPA allows attorney fees (which you give up if you go alone). Provided you have evidence of the violations (witnesses, call logs, letters), a small claims judge can award up to $1k statutory and perhaps less inclined to, but they could. No Alabama analog law exists (AL doesn’t have its own state FDCPA), so use the federal one. The collector may bring an attorney, but they might also choose to settle quick to avoid costs. This is one of those “not typical” uses of small claims but legally permissible.

The above are examples – basically if a state or federal statute creates a right to sue for money and your claim is within $6k, you can use small claims. Just ensure any required prelim steps (like demand letters or agency filings) are done if the statute requires.

### Other Real-World Disputes Suited for Alabama Small Claims

*(This is an open-ended catch-all for any type of dispute that doesn’t squarely fit above but is commonly seen in small claims.)*

- **Neighbor disputes (property damage aside):** If a neighbor owes you money for something like shared fence repairs (you both agreed to split a new fence cost and they never paid their half), that’s enforceable in small claims as a verbal contract. Or if a neighbor’s *kids* caused damage – technically you’d sue the parents (parents in Alabama can be liable for a minor child’s willful or malicious property damage up to $1,000 by statute, Ala. Code § 6-5-380). You could invoke that statute if, say, their 15-year-old keyed your car ($800 damage) – they are on the hook up to $1k ([www.troymessenger.com](https://www.troymessenger.com/2015/08/05/small-claims-court-increases-claim-amount-limitations/#:~:text=The%20fees%20for%20filing%20for,attorney%20fees%20in%20higher%20courts)). 

- **Household goods disputes:** If you sold something on Craigslist and the check bounced or buyer never paid in full, you can use small claims to get your money or item back (like a simpler breach of sale agreement). Or vice versa, if you bought a used item that was misrepresented (e.g. “antique table” that turned out to be a cheap replica), you might claim a partial refund for the difference in value as a misrepresentation.

- **Rideshare or gig economy issues:** If you are a driver for Uber/Lyft or delivery and the company failed to pay an earned bonus or fare adjustment, you could sue for that money (some drivers have used small claims for withheld pay or wrongful deactivations resulting in lost earnings – the latter is harder as it’s more an employment dispute). Most gig companies have arbitration clauses you agreed to, which they might enforce, but some carve out small claims. For example, Uber’s terms often allow individual small claims despite arbitration clause. If so, you could bring, e.g., “Uber promised a $500 sign-on bonus and never paid it.” Or a passenger sued a rideshare driver for property damage or vice versa – sure, that’s possible (though usually insurance covers accidents, but e.g. driver damaged passenger’s property by recklessness, passenger could sue driver for that minor claim). 

- **Airbnb or rental platform disputes:** If you rented a vacation house and it was a scam or conditions were grossly misrepresented, you could sue the host for a refund or damages under breach of contract or UDAP. Many online platforms have arbitration in their terms (Airbnb, VRBO etc. all have terms – often requiring arbitration *except* small claims is usually still allowed for individual claims). Check their user agreement. If allowed, you’d likely have to sue the host (who may be out-of-state – jurisdiction and serving them could be tricky unless they own property in AL). But if it occurred in Alabama and the host has assets here (like the property), you might proceed. Or if you *hosted* someone who caused damage not fully covered by the platform’s guarantee, you could sue the guest in small claims for the remainder (if you can find them). It’s challenging if they’re far away, but possible if they travel back or you can serve them via certified mail out of state (Alabama allows out-of-state service via certified mail for small claims). 

- **Event ticket issues:** If a reseller sold you fake tickets or overpriced illegally, you might attempt to recover your money (this could also be DTPA for a deceptive act). Or if an event was canceled and the organizer didn’t refund your ticket (contrary to purchase terms), small claims can handle that contractual refund.

- **Cell phone or cable billing disputes:** If your phone company or cable company incorrectly billed you fees and after exhausting their customer service you’re still out money, you can try small claims. Major telecoms often have arbitration clauses though (notably AT&T, Verizon, etc., typically do, but often they also say you can go to small claims for individual disputes – check your service agreement). If permitted, you could sue for that $300 wrongly charged roaming or equipment fee they refuse to reverse. Often just filing might prompt a settlement from their legal department.

- **Deposits with service providers:** Many services require deposits (renters of equipment, utility companies, contractors). If you never got your deposit back and it’s clearly due, small claims is the way. E.g., an **electric company** in Alabama may require a deposit and promise to refund after 12 months of good payment, but then never did – you can sue for it. Utilities are heavily regulated though; sometimes you might file a complaint with the Alabama Public Service Commission. But small claims is another path if it’s straightforward. Similarly, if you **rented equipment** (let’s say a rug shampoo machine with a $50 deposit) and returned it in good condition but never got deposit back, that’s small claims.

In summary, any scenario where someone owes you money or has property of yours up to $6,000 in value – and there’s no exclusive specialized court or immunity blocking it – is fair game for small claims.

### Counterclaims in Small Claims

If you are a **defendant in a small claims case**, you have the right to file a **counterclaim** against the plaintiff if *they* actually owe *you* money or caused *you* damages. Alabama’s small claims rules allow counterclaims basically to the same extent as claims (any civil counter-demand up to $6,000). For example, a contractor sues a homeowner for $5,000 unpaid bill; the homeowner could counterclaim that the work was defective and cost $3,000 to fix, seeking that $3,000. Counterclaims are filed using the **Defendant’s Counterclaim form (Form SM-06)** ([eforms.alacourt.gov](https://eforms.alacourt.gov/search/?q=SM-06#:~:text=E,11%2F16%2F2005)) ([shelby.alacourt.gov](https://shelby.alacourt.gov/small-claims/small-claims-faq/#:~:text=filed%20within%2014%20calendar%20days,you%20have%20against%20the%20plaintiff)), which is available from the clerk or online. You generally should file the counterclaim at the **same time you file your Answer or soon after (within the 14-day answer period)** ([shelby.alacourt.gov](https://shelby.alacourt.gov/small-claims/small-claims-faq/#:~:text=Mail%20or%20UPS%20Commercial%20Carrier,a%20conflict%20with%20the%20trial)) ([shelby.alacourt.gov](https://shelby.alacourt.gov/small-claims/small-claims-faq/#:~:text=you%20must%20answer%20the%20Complaint,you%20have%20against%20the%20plaintiff)), to ensure it’s considered. Failing to answer in time could result in a default against you, so don’t delay.

- **Counterclaim amount limit:** The counterclaim is subject to the **same $6,000 limit** in small claims court. If you (as a defendant) believe you have damages *exceeding* $6,000, you face a strategic choice: Alabama procedural rules likely allow the case to be **transferred to a higher court** (District Civil or Circuit) if a counterclaim over the jurisdictional limit is raised. In practice, what happens is the district judge would order the case moved to the general civil docket (District Court) where the limit is $20,000 ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-11-30/#:~:text=%281%29%20CIVIL,exclusive%20of%20interest%20and%20costs)) ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-11-30/#:~:text=,exclusive%20of%20interest%20and%20costs)), or even to Circuit Court if it exceeds $20k. *Alternatively*, you can **waive the amount above $6k and just claim $6,000** to keep it in small claims. Many times, defendants prefer to keep it in small claims for simplicity, so they will limit their counterclaim to $6,000 even if the actual damages could be higher. Consult an attorney if you’re unsure, but that’s the general approach. If a counterclaim *far* above $6k is filed, the plaintiff (or court on its own) might transfer the whole case out of the simplified small claims docket.

- **Filing fee for counterclaims:** In Alabama small claims, a defendant *usually does not have to pay a separate filing fee for a counterclaim* on the small claims docket – the initial filing fee covers the case. However, if your counterclaim takes the case out of small claims (e.g. over $1,500 such that it invokes regular district procedures), Ala. Code § 12-12-31(f) suggests the higher fee schedule applies ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-12/chapter-12/article-2/section-12-12-31/#:~:text=,are%20not%20small%20claims%20cases)). This effectively might mean you as counter-plaintiff have to pay the difference in filing fee (for example, if the plaintiff’s claim was $1,000 they paid $**35 base** filing fee ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-12/chapter-19/article-3/division-2/section-12-19-71/#:~:text=%281%29%20Thirty,in%20determining%20the%20jurisdictional%20amount)), but your counterclaim is $5,000, normally a $198 base fee applies to such a case ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-12/chapter-19/article-3/division-2/section-12-19-71/#:~:text=%283%29%20One%20hundred%20ninety,in%20determining%20the%20jurisdictional%20amount)) – the clerk might require you to pay that difference). In practice, many small claims courts keep it informal and won’t assess extra unless the whole case is formally transferred to the regular docket. Check with the clerk when filing the counterclaim – at worst, be prepared to pay a fee similar to a normal filing (possibly around $100+). It’s still cheaper than filing a new separate lawsuit.

- **What can you counterclaim for?** Anything arising out of or related to the plaintiff’s claim or transaction. Alabama allows even **unrelated counterclaims** in District Court (per Rule 13 of ARCP), but in small claims, typically it’s about the same contract or incident. E.g., if a landlord sues you for rent, you counterclaim for the security deposit and property repairs you paid for. If a credit card company sues you for $4,000, frankly you might not have a counterclaim against them except perhaps an FDCPA violation or accounting error – which you could raise. If an auto body shop sues you for unpaid repair bill, you counterclaim that the repair was bad and the car is still damaged. Filing a counterclaim ensures **both** disputes get resolved in one hearing.

- **Procedure:** To file a counterclaim, get the SM-06 form (for example, “Defendant’s Counterclaim – Form SM-6” ([eforms.alacourt.gov](https://eforms.alacourt.gov/search/?q=SM-06#:~:text=E,11%2F16%2F2005))). Fill it out stating who you are (defendant) and who you’re counter-suing (the plaintiff), for what amount and why. File it with the clerk; the clerk will serve it on the plaintiff (often by mail to their address of record or via their attorney). The plaintiff will need to **answer the counterclaim** – typically also within 14 days of being served with it (the Small Claims Rules mirror the answer timeline). In practice, if your counterclaim is filed close to the trial date, the court might reschedule the trial to give the plaintiff a chance to respond. Under Alabama Small Claims Rule F, the **deadline for answer** is 14 days ([shelby.alacourt.gov](https://shelby.alacourt.gov/small-claims/small-claims-faq/#:~:text=Mail%20or%20UPS%20Commercial%20Carrier,a%20conflict%20with%20the%20trial)) – that likely applies to counterclaims too.

- **If counterclaim exceeds $6k:** As discussed, likely the case can be elevated. Alabama law provides that District Court can hear cases up to $20k ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-11-30/#:~:text=%281%29%20CIVIL,exclusive%20of%20interest%20and%20costs)), but small claims *procedure* only up to $6k. So the case might continue in District Court under regular rules (which are more complex – e.g. discovery allowed, etc.). Often, the plaintiff (who initially chose small claims for simplicity) might choose to **amend and reduce** or even dismiss their claim to avoid going to the regular court if they see a big counterclaim. Or the defendant might waive the excess on the counterclaim to keep it in small claims. These decisions can be strategic. If your counterclaim is legitimately far larger, you might want a jury trial (only available in Circuit) – then intentionally busting the limit is in your interest. But that’s beyond the scope here. Small claims spirit is to keep things small.

- **Outcome of counterclaims:** If both a claim and counterclaim are heard, the judge might find for one side on both, or could find *each* side wins something. The court could then **offset judgments**. For example, contractor sues for $5,000, homeowner counters for $3,000 in defects; judge might decide homeowner owes $5,000 – $2,000 (because contractor’s work was 40% defective) = net $3,000 to contractor. In Alabama, the rules allow judgment to be entered on each claim. If both win, they offset and the net can be entered. If defendant’s counterclaim actually exceeds plaintiff’s claim and defendant proves it, the result could be plaintiff gets zero and defendant wins some positive amount against plaintiff. E.g., plaintiff’s claim dismissed and defendant wins $2,000 on counter – then plaintiff (as counter-defendant) owes $2,000. So yes, a counterclaim can leave the plaintiff owing money when the dust settles.

In summary, **defendants absolutely should consider counterclaims** in Alabama small claims if they have any plausible claim back at the plaintiff. The process is straightforward (a one-page form). As the Shelby County FAQ advises: “As the defendant, you may also choose to file a Counterclaim…which is a claim you have against the plaintiff.” ([shelby.alacourt.gov](https://shelby.alacourt.gov/small-claims/small-claims-faq/#:~:text=filed%20within%2014%20calendar%20days,you%20have%20against%20the%20plaintiff)). Just remember to keep it within $6,000 or be prepared for the case to possibly get more complicated.

### What **Cannot** Be Sued for in Alabama Small Claims

Small claims courts are limited in the type of relief they can grant. They generally handle *money judgments* (or return of specific personal property). You **cannot** ask for non-monetary orders like injunctions, nor can you bring cases that by law must be heard in another court or are beyond the small claims jurisdiction. Here are specific things you *cannot* pursue in Alabama small claims, including both generic exclusions and any Alabama-specific carve-outs:

- **Evictions and foreclosures:** You cannot evict a tenant or otherwise recover possession of real property in small claims. Alabama eviction actions (unlawful detainer) must be filed as a separate case in District or Circuit Court (often District Civil). Small claims deals only with money. So if you’re a landlord, file eviction separately; if you’re a tenant wrongly being evicted, small claims can’t stop it (you’d fight that in the eviction case or seek an injunction in a higher court). Similarly, foreclosure of a mortgage can’t be done in small claims.

- **Real estate title or boundary disputes:** Small claims has no jurisdiction to determine title to real property or decide exactly where a property line is. Those issues belong in Circuit Court. If your dispute is about *money* related to real estate (like someone didn’t pay you for work done on a house, etc.) that’s fine. But if it’s “I claim ownership of this strip of land and neighbor also claims it,” that’s an **ejectment** or quiet title action for Circuit Court. Likewise, if you’re trying to enforce a contract to buy real estate (specific performance), small claims can’t order the transfer of land. You could possibly sue for return of an earnest money deposit (money), but not for the house itself.

- **Family law matters:** Small claims cannot handle divorce, child support, child custody, alimony, adoption, or domestic relations issues. Those go to family/domestic court (Circuit). Even if your family matter is about money (like one spouse owes the other money per a divorce decree), you generally enforce that through the divorce case in family court, not a new small claim. If it’s truly independent (like you loaned your cousin money – that’s just a loan, not “family law”), that’s fine. But anything requiring determination of marital or parental rights is out. Also, suing for emotional distress or personal grievances within a family (like “my ex cheated, I want compensation”) is not a valid small claim – no cause of action for that in small claims.

- **Libel/slander (defamation) above the cap:** Technically, defamation is a tort where you could sue for damages, but valuing reputation harm is often >$6k and requires evidence of harm. A small claims court is not appropriate for most defamation cases. If someone defamed you and you can quantify a small actual loss (like it cost you $1,000 business), you *could* sue for that $1,000 perhaps. But punitive damages in defamation (which often make those cases large) cannot be sought in small claims beyond the cap, and an injunction (like retraction) is not available. So practically, defamation is not usually handled in small claims. If you attempt it for a small amount, be aware you must prove the elements (false statement, published to others, caused actual harm). It’s complex and might get kicked to a higher court or dismissed. Alabama also has a one-year statute of limitations on defamation – short timeline.

- **Professional malpractice:** Claims of **malpractice by a licensed professional** (doctors, lawyers, engineers, etc.) are generally too complex for small claims. They often require expert testimony to establish the standard of care and breach. For example, if you think your dentist committed malpractice causing you injury, that’s likely above $6k and needs an expert dentist to testify – not feasible in small claims. Also, Alabama law requires you to give a detailed complaint and possibly an affidavit from an expert in medical malpractice cases filed in regular court – small claims lacks that format. So these are heard in Circuit Court. If a professional simply owes you *money* (like you paid a lawyer $1,000 and they never did any work, effectively breaching contract), you could sue for the $1,000 as a contract matter without calling it malpractice. That approach can sometimes recover small amounts paid for services not delivered. But any claim of **negligence in the service causing harm** (like a botched surgery causing injury) must be filed as a lawsuit in Circuit with all formalities – not small claims.

- **Federal law claims outside small claims scope:** Some matters are exclusive to federal courts (e.g. bankruptcy, federal tax disputes, immigration). For example, if someone owes you money but they filed **bankruptcy**, once they filed, an automatic stay stops you from suing or continuing to sue them in any court (including small claims) without permission from the bankruptcy court. And ultimately, the debt might be discharged; small claims cannot override a bankruptcy discharge. So you cannot pursue someone in small claims if the debt was discharged in bankruptcy – it’s legally uncollectible. Also, you cannot sue a **federal agency or the U.S. government** in small claims (so no, you can’t sue the IRS in small claims for a refund – that must go through federal courts or admin processes). Similarly, certain federal civil rights claims (like suing police for constitutional violations under 42 U.S.C. §1983) are beyond small claims purview – those should go to District Court (and often require attorneys and significant evidence). If it’s a straightforward matter like a local government breach of contract, you might do it if small ($6k), but be cautious: many government bodies have **sovereign immunity** or conditions (see next point).

- **Suing the State of Alabama or its agencies:** Generally not allowed in small claims (or any state court) without the state’s consent because of **sovereign immunity** in the Alabama Constitution. For example, you cannot sue the State Department of Transportation in small claims for damage to your car from a pothole – the state is immune to suit in court. Instead, Alabama has a **Board of Adjustment** that hears certain claims against the state (like for property damage or contract claims up to a certain amount) as an administrative remedy. Cities and counties have some immunity for certain claims too, but they can be sued for contractual breaches or negligence under some conditions (with damage caps: e.g. claims against a city for negligence are capped at $100,000 by statute, but those typically in Circuit – a small claim would be below that anyway). If you do sue a city or county in small claims (say the city breached a small contract with you), you must have complied with any **claims notice requirement** first. Alabama law (Ala. Code § 11-47-23) says claims against municipalities for torts must be filed with the city clerk within **6 months** of the incident ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/8577/11-47-23.html#:~:text=municipalities,View%20Our%20Newest%20Version%20Here)). If you fail to do that, the city can motion to dismiss your claim. So e.g. if a city garbage truck dented your fence and you want $500, you needed to send the city a written notice of claim within 6 months. If you did, you can then sue in small claims after they deny or ignore it. Always check local law for notice rules: counties have similar notice requirements (often 1 year for counties). And the State itself – you can’t sue it in small claims at all; you’d file with the Board of Adjustment (for amounts under $5,000 typically), which is an administrative tribunal that can award claims (like a state-level small claims for state liability).

- **Workers’ compensation:** You cannot sue your employer in small claims (or any court) for a workplace injury – those fall under the exclusive remedy of the Alabama **Workers’ Compensation** system. If your employer did not carry comp insurance and you were an employee, you might have a civil cause, but that goes to Circuit, not small claims, and likely needs a lawyer. If it’s a simpler wage issue or expense as discussed, that’s different. But actual injury on job – small claims is not appropriate. Also, if you try to sue a co-worker for injuring you on the job, that’s usually barred by comp exclusivity too, except for willful conduct by a co-employee, which again is a complex claim for higher court.

- **Patent, trademark, or copyright issues:** Those are exclusively federal subject matter – you can’t sue someone in state court for patent infringement or copyright damages. So not in small claims either. (The U.S. has a new “Copyright small claims board” federally, but that’s separate from our discussion). If you have an IP dispute, small claims can’t handle it.

- **Class actions or joining multiple plaintiffs:** Small claims in Alabama is designed for individual claims. You cannot bring a class action on behalf of others or a general public. Each person must file their own case. The rules typically also limit **joinder of parties** – e.g. a couple of people can sue together if it’s the same transaction (like both cosigners sue someone), but you can’t band 50 consumers together in one small claim complaint. The DTPA explicitly forbids class actions by consumers in Alabama state court. So keep it to your individual claim.

- **Orders to do or not do something (injunctions):** Small claims judges cannot order someone to perform a contract (specific performance) or to stop doing something or to fulfill an obligation other than paying money or giving back specific personal property. For example, you can’t sue in small claims to force your neighbor to remove a fence encroachment – you’d need an injunction in Circuit. You also can’t ask small claims to order a mechanic to finish repairing your car; you can only ask for money so you can pay someone else. If you accidentally file for a type of relief not allowed, the court will likely dismiss it or convert it to a money claim if possible.

- **Anything exceeding $6,000:** This goes without saying – if your claim, even if valid, is for more than $6,000 (excluding allowable interest/costs), it’s outside small claims jurisdiction. You cannot, for instance, sue for $10,000 and “just see if the judge will give me $6,000” – you’d have to formally limit your claim to $6,000 (waive the rest) or file in a higher court. If you try to file for more, the clerk will reject it or the court will dismiss for lack of jurisdiction. If your claim is close to the limit, you might reduce some less important parts to bring it under $6k. 

- **Punitive damages beyond what’s incidental:** While Alabama small claims *can* award punitive damages or statutory multiples in some cases (as discussed, e.g. punitive for bad checks, UDAP trebling, etc.), you obviously cannot exceed $6k total. Also, a stand-alone claim purely for a civil penalty or fine that isn’t tied to actual damages wouldn’t really be recognized. (For example, you can’t just sue someone and say “they did wrong, I want $5,000 to punish them” if you had no actual loss – except where a statute like DTPA sets $100 minimum, which still presumes some violation of your rights). Generally, small claims judges are conservative with punitive awards – they focus on making the plaintiff whole in money lost. So if your aim is huge punitive punishment, small claims is not the venue.

- **Criminal penalties or criminal restitution:** If someone committed a crime against you causing loss, you can’t use small claims to impose a criminal penalty (that’s for criminal court). You *can* sue for the money loss from the crime (like theft, as discussed). But say you were assaulted and want the person to “pay for pain” – that’s a personal injury tort, possibly allowed if within cap, but any notion of criminal punishment or imprisonment is not for small claims. Also, if a criminal court already ordered restitution, you can’t get a double recovery in small claims; you’d instead enforce the restitution through the criminal case.

- **Suing the United States or a sovereign entity:** If, for example, a U.S. Postal truck hit your fence, your claim is actually under the Federal Tort Claims Act (which requires filing with the federal agency first, etc.), not small claims. Or if you want a refund of federal taxes, that’s U.S. Tax Court or federal district after a claim. If you have an issue with a state agency’s decision (like denial of a license or benefits), that often must go through an administrative appeal process, not a new small claim. 

- **Bankruptcy stay or discharge situations:** If the person you want to sue is in an active bankruptcy or has received a discharge of that debt, small claims can’t override that. Attempting to collect is actually a violation of federal law. So always check if the defendant is bankrupt. Similarly, if you owe a debt and filed bankruptcy, a creditor can’t avoid that by using small claims either.

- **Automobile accident with injuries above $6k:** Already covered – basically you shouldn’t file those in small claims. Only minor injuries should be considered in small claims.

- **Any matter exclusively assigned to another forum:** For completeness, things like **probate matters** (wills, estates, guardianships) must go to Probate or Circuit Court; small claims can’t, for instance, adjudicate a will dispute or order an executor to pay you (you’d file a claim in the estate). **Juvenile matters** (like suing on behalf of a minor’s delinquency or something) are handled in Juvenile Court usually. **Election disputes**, **municipal ordinance appeals**, **zoning appeals** – all have specific venues, not small claims.

- **Disputes that involve complicated equitable relief:** For instance, if two people co-own a car and disagree on its sale, a true resolution might involve ordering a sale and splitting proceeds – small claims won’t do that; they’d just award money to one and you sort out the car. Or if you want a **rescission** of a contract (undo the contract entirely and return both sides to pre-contract state), that’s an equitable remedy not typically granted in small claims; small claims would just give money damages. 

To highlight *Alabama-specific exclusions*: The big one is the **sovereign immunity** of the State (as noted, Alabama’s constitution gives the state absolute immunity from being sued in its own courts). So you cannot sue the State of Alabama or its agencies in small claims for anything – you must go to the Board of Adjustment or another administrative route. Another is **municipal notice** – not exactly an exclusion, but a procedural bar if not followed (the 6-month notice to city for tort claims). Also, **punitive damages in small claims** – while not explicitly barred, Alabama law (Ala. Code § 6-11-20) sets a high standard for awarding punitive damages (clear and convincing evidence of oppression, fraud, etc.) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2015/title-6/chapter-11/article-2/section-6-11-20/#:~:text=and%20convincing%20evidence%20proven%3B%20definitions,AL%20Code%20%C2%A7)). In a casual small claims hearing, you’re unlikely to clear that bar unless it’s very obvious. So functionally, don’t expect punitive damages except in those statutory scenarios (bad checks, UDAP) where they are specifically contemplated.

### Damages – What You Can and Can’t Recover

Small claims can award **compensatory damages** to make the plaintiff whole for their proven losses. Here’s what is and isn’t recoverable in Alabama small claims:

- **Compensatory (Actual) Damages:** Yes – this is the core of small claims. You can recover the amount of money that compensates your direct loss: the unpaid debt, the cost of repairs, the medical bills, the lost wages, the value of property that was damaged or not returned, etc. You need to substantiate the amount (with bills, receipts, estimates, market values). The judge will only award what you prove up to $6,000. They will not automatically add extra beyond what you claim and prove. So if you’re owed $5,000 and you prove $5,000, you get $5,000 (assuming you win on liability). If you ask for too much compared to evidence (like you claim $5k but only show $3k of bills), the judge at most will give $3k. Alabama also allows **incidental or consequential damages** if they naturally arise from the breach and are foreseeable. For example, if a contractor’s breach caused you to have to stay in a hotel for 2 nights ($200), you can claim that $200 in addition to, say, the cost to fix the work. Just make sure it’s directly tied and not too speculative.

- **Punitive/Exemplary Damages:** Available only in specific circumstances and even then limited by the $6k cap. Punitive damages (meant to punish the wrongdoer for egregious conduct) are *not* typical in small claims, but Alabama law does allow them where the conduct involves fraud, malice, or willful/wanton behavior and is proven by clear and convincing evidence ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2015/title-6/chapter-11/article-2/section-6-11-20/#:~:text=and%20convincing%20evidence%20proven%3B%20definitions,AL%20Code%20%C2%A7)). In practice, small claims cases seldom meet this evidentiary threshold, and many small claims judges are reluctant to award punitive sums. The small claims form doesn’t even have a place to explicitly ask for punitive damages, but you could mention it in your claim description if applicable (e.g. “seeking $500 for actual damage and $500 punitive for willful fraud”). If you have a case of intentional wrongdoing – e.g., a defendant **defrauded** you – you might get some punitive component. For instance, under the worthless check statute, the court may award punitive damages explicitly ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-6/chapter-5/article-16/section-6-5-285/#:~:text=date%20of%20the%20unlawful%20act,trying%20the%20case%20may%20assess)). Under the DTPA, up to triple damages serve a punitive purpose ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,practices%20were%20committed%20intentionally%3B%20and)). So punitive-type awards *can* be given via those statutes (within the $6k total). But you **cannot** expect punitive damages *in addition to* $6k compensatory that would exceed the cap. The total judgment can’t exceed $6,000. So effectively, punitive damages in small claims are constrained by that. Example: you proved $2,000 actual loss and extraordinary misconduct; maybe the judge gives you $4,000 total (which implies $2k punitive). You cannot get $6k actual + $6k punitive = $12k – impossible in small claims. Also note, Alabama has statutory caps on punitive damages in higher courts (3x compensatory or $500k in most cases, with exceptions for small amounts where triple might be less) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2015/title-6/chapter-11/article-2/section-6-11-20/#:~:text=and%20convincing%20evidence%20proven%3B%20definitions,AL%20Code%20%C2%A7)) – but those caps are way above small claims level, so not an issue here. So bottom line: focus on proving your actual damages. Punitive will only be gravy in clear cases of intentional wrongdoing, and always within the $6k limit.

- **Statutory multipliers or penalties:** Some statutes essentially allow more than actual damages, like **double security deposit** penalty ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=both%2C%20is%20sufficient%20compliance%20with,this%20chapter)) or **treble consumer damages** ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,100%2C%20whichever%20is%20greater%3B%20or)). These are considered a form of additional damages or penalty that the court can award. In small claims, these are allowable as long as the resultant sum is ≤ $6k. For instance, if a deposit was $1,000, double is $2,000 – fine. If deposit was $4,000, double is $8,000 – you’d be capped at $6,000, effectively meaning you could only get 1.5x in that scenario because of jurisdiction limit (unless you go to District Civil instead to seek the full $8k). Similarly for treble under DTPA: if actual $2,000, treble $6,000 exactly fits; if actual $3,000, treble $9,000 too high for small claims, so you’d either waive part or not seek full treble. Alabama law doesn’t explicitly forbid awarding a statutorily prescribed penalty that exceeds $6k, it simply says the court’s jurisdiction is up to $6k. So a small claims judge literally has no power to issue a judgment beyond $6,000. Therefore, consider the cap when asking for multiples – you might ask for “treble or $6,000, whichever is less.” Another example: FDCPA ($1,000 cap) fits within $6k so that’s fine per violation per defendant.

- **Attorney’s fees and court costs:** In Alabama small claims, **court costs** (filing fee, service fee, subpoena fees, etc.) are generally awarded to the **prevailing party** by default. The Shelby County FAQ notes that additional fees you paid “will be added to what the defendant owes if you win; otherwise, the plaintiff is responsible” ([chambers.alacourt.gov](https://chambers.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Filing%20Fee%20,%2412.00%20per%20subpoena)) ([shelby.alacourt.gov](https://shelby.alacourt.gov/small-claims/small-claims-faq/#:~:text=In%20some%20instances%2C%20it%20costs,is%20set%20for%20a%20hearing)). So if you win, the judgment typically says “$X plus court costs.” You don’t necessarily need to ask; it’s customary. If for some reason the judge doesn’t mention costs, you can respectfully remind them that you paid $** (the clerk usually auto-adds costs). These include the filing fee, any fees for serving the defendant (sheriff or certified mail fee), and subpoena service fees if you needed witness subpoenas. However, **attorney’s fees** are another matter. In small claims, **you generally cannot recover attorney fees unless a contract or statute entitles you to them AND you actually had an attorney** ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,represented%20by%20a%20licensed%20attorney)). Ala. Code § 12-12-31(c) explicitly says no party can recover attorney fees in a small claims case *unless* they were represented by a licensed attorney in the case ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,represented%20by%20a%20licensed%20attorney)). Since attorneys are optional and most plaintiffs are self-represented, this basically means pro se parties can’t get “attorney fees” for their own time. If you did hire a lawyer for your small claim (rare, but possible, especially for a business plaintiff or an assigned claim which requires one), and the contract or law allows fee-shifting, then the court can award those fees. For example, a loan contract might say “borrower will pay lender’s attorney fees if collected by an attorney” – if the lender used an attorney in small claims to sue, they could ask for fees (though often in small claims, amounts are small enough that fee awards are limited by reasonableness anyway). For consumers, the DTPA and FDCPA provide attorney fees to a prevailing plaintiff – but if you have no attorney, you get nothing for that (you can’t claim a “pro se fee”). So likely no attorney fees in 99% of small claims cases. Don’t expect compensation for your time off work or travel to court as “fees” – courts don’t award that.

- **Interest (pre-judgment and post-judgment):** Yes, you can recover interest in some cases:
    - **Pre-judgment interest:** If your claim is for a **liquidated amount** (a fixed sum that was due at a certain time, like a loan or unpaid invoice), Alabama law allows adding interest from the date due to the date of judgment. The default rate for such **contract debts** when no rate is specified is **6% per annum** ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-8/chapter-8/section-8-8-1/#:~:text=2025%20Code%20of%20Alabama%20%3A%3A,or%20for%20a%20longer%20or)) (Ala. Code § 8-8-1). If a higher interest rate was agreed in a written contract, you can claim that rate (up to 8% generally legal in AL for non-loan, and more for certain loans – but 6% is safe unless specified) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-8/chapter-8/section-8-8-1/#:~:text=2025%20Code%20of%20Alabama%20%3A%3A,or%20for%20a%20longer%20or)). For example, if someone owed you $1,000 on Jan 1, 2025, and judgment is on Jan 1, 2026, you could ask for one year’s interest (~$60) on top. However, in small claims, many plaintiffs don’t bother with pre-judgment interest because it’s often a small amount and can complicate things. But you are entitled to it if applicable – you should explicitly request it in your complaint or at trial. If your claim is unliquidated (like damages from an accident), pre-judgment interest is typically not awarded – those only accrue interest after judgment. Only “ascertainable” debts earn pre-judgment interest in Alabama ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-12/chapter-19/article-3/division-2/section-12-19-71/#:~:text=%281%29%20Thirty,in%20determining%20the%20jurisdictional%20amount)).
