    - **Post-judgment interest:** Once you obtain a judgment, it accrues interest until paid at the rate set by law. Alabama’s post-judgment interest rate is **7.5% per year** (for judgments entered on or after Sept 1, 2011) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-8/chapter-8/section-8-8-10/#:~:text=,from%20the%20day%20of%20entry)). This is set by Ala. Code § 8-8-10: judgments on contract claims bear contract rate, all others 7.5% ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-8/chapter-8/section-8-8-10/#:~:text=,from%20the%20day%20of%20entry)). Practically, nearly all small claims judgments will be at 7.5% APR (the “contract action” language means if the judgment is *specifically on a contract that had a stated interest rate*, it would use that – e.g., a loan at 10% per year, then judgment might carry 10%; but if silent or non-contract or any other case, 7.5%). The clerk will typically add “with interest accruing at 7.5% per annum from the date of judgment until paid” on the judgment form. You don’t need to ask; it’s by operation of law ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-8/chapter-8/section-8-8-10/#:~:text=,from%20the%20day%20of%20entry)). But it’s good to know you are entitled to it. For example, if the defendant drags out payment for a year, you can add 7.5% of the amount as interest. On $6,000, that’s $450 a year. Post-judgment interest in Alabama cannot be waived by the court; it’s mandatory. 
    - **Interest vs. Cap:** Note, the $6,000 cap applies to the “amount in controversy, exclusive of interest and costs” ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,promulgated%20by%20the%20Supreme%20Court)). That means you can get a $6,000 judgment plus court costs and post-judgment interest on top of that, and that’s permissible. Pre-judgment interest is also “exclusive of” the cap, arguably, since the statute says interest is excluded in determining jurisdictional amount ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,promulgated%20by%20the%20Supreme%20Court)). Indeed, § 12-12-31(a) says “exclusive of interest and costs” in the $6k calculation. So you could potentially get $6,000 + pre-judgment interest. However, to be safe, if your principal plus expected interest would overshoot a lot, it’s better to claim a total not over $6k. In most cases, interest is small enough it won’t push it far over. But know that legally the interest doesn’t count toward the $6k limit in terms of jurisdiction ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,promulgated%20by%20the%20Supreme%20Court)). For example, a $6,000 loan that’s 3 years overdue at 6% interest – interest ~ $1,080. Small claims should still take it because principal is $6k exclusive of interest. They could award $6,000 + interest. 
    - Always ensure interest is calculated correctly. The judge may or may not do it on the spot. Sometimes the judgment will just say “plus interest from date of judgment at 7.5%” and not mention pre-judgment. If you want those few hundred dollars of pre-judgment interest, bring a calculation and politely request it. It’s at the judge’s discretion whether to include it if you didn’t specifically plead it, but by law if it’s a liquidated sum due on a date, you have a right to it ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-12/chapter-19/article-3/division-2/section-12-19-71/#:~:text=%281%29%20Thirty,in%20determining%20the%20jurisdictional%20amount)). 
    - **Example:** You sue for an unpaid $5,000 promissory note due a year ago. You could ask judgment for $5,000 + “pre-judgment interest of $300 (at 6%)” + costs. The judge might simply give “$5,300 plus court costs” and then interest at 7.5% going forward. But some might just give $5,000 if you don’t mention it. So mention it.

- **Court costs:** As mentioned, court costs are generally awarded to the winner. In Alabama, court costs include the **filing fee**, fees for service of process (sheriff or certified mail costs), and any **subpoena issuance fees** you paid for witnesses. It may also include a small fee if a default judgment was entered ($50 in some counties for processing a default for claims $3k-$6k ([lee.alacourt.gov](https://lee.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Additional%20Defendant%20%20,%2420.00))). For example, in Lee County’s fee schedule it shows a $50 default fee for larger claims ([lee.alacourt.gov](https://lee.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Additional%20Defendant%20%20,%2420.00)). If you had to pay that to get a default, you can add it. The judgment will usually say “plus costs of court.” The clerk then computes the exact cost amount separately. If you’re the plaintiff and you lose, you could be responsible for *defendant’s* court costs, but since defendants in small claims often have $0 costs (they usually don’t pay an answer fee; only if they subpoenaed a witness or something), it’s often not much. So costs risk is low for a plaintiff aside from your own.

- **No pain & suffering unless tied to an injury claim:** Emotional distress, pain, suffering, etc., are considered general damages and can only be recovered if you have a valid tort claim that allows them (like personal injury). In contract cases or property cases, you *cannot* get money for stress or inconvenience – those are not recognized damages in such contexts. E.g., if a contractor’s breach inconvenienced you greatly, the court might sympathize but won’t award money for your “aggravation” – just for the financial loss. Only physical personal injury claims allow money for pain (and even then, small claims might be conservative with awarding it without medical corroboration). So don’t tack on, say, “and $1,000 for my mental anguish” in a typical small claim – the judge will likely ignore that. Alabama law does allow mental anguish damages in certain contract-like cases (e.g., mishandling of corpse, etc., which is exceptional; not typical small claims scenario). But run-of-the-mill cases, no.

- **No punitive damages for simple negligence:** We covered punitive – must show willful/wanton conduct. So if someone just was carelessly negligent (like a fender-bender accident), you get compensatory (car repairs, maybe medical), *not* punitive. Save the punitive ask for truly egregious facts.

- **No double recovery:** If you already received compensation from another source, the court might offset that. For instance, if an insurance already paid you part of a loss, you can’t recover that again from the defendant (you only go after the remainder). Or if an item is returned to you after filing (like defendant gave back property), you adjust the claim accordingly. The judgment will only cover what’s still owed.

- **Property recovery (Replevin):** Alabama small claims can order **specific personal property** to be returned (through an action of detinue/replevin) using the SM-02 form ([russell.alacourt.gov](https://russell.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=To%20Download%20Small%20Claims%20forms%3A%C2%A0Click,Specific%20Property)). This is not money damages per se, but a remedy to get the actual item back. It’s allowed if the item’s value is ≤ $6,000. If you file for specific property, the court’s judgment can be “Item X shall be returned to Plaintiff, or if not possible, Plaintiff is awarded $Y value of the property.” Often they give an alternate value in case the item isn’t recoverable. So that’s a form of relief you can get that’s not just money – but it’s still essentially about property worth up to $6k. *Exception:* If the item is unique to you and you really want it back, small claims can technically order it, but enforcement is by sheriff like a writ of execution to seize property. That can be tricky. Sometimes just the threat of judgment will cause return.

In summary, Alabama small claims judgments typically consist of: **principal damages** (what you proved), **plus court costs**, **plus any allowable interest**, and occasionally **plus a statutory or punitive enhancement** if justified. No speculative or indirect damages, no emotional damages (unless a verifiable part of an injury claim within limits), no attorneys’ fees unless provided by law/contract and you had one. The goal is to compensate your economic loss up to $6,000. 

Now that we’ve covered what you can sue for and what you can’t, the next sections will discuss **how and where** to file your Alabama small claim.

## 3. Where to File

**Alabama small claims cases are filed in the District Court of the county** where jurisdiction and venue are proper. Each of Alabama’s 67 counties has a District Court, and within it a designated **Small Claims docket** ([marion.alacourt.gov](https://marion.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=The%20Small%20Claims%20Court%20is,the%20judgment%20to%20Circuit%20Court)). You will file your case with the **Clerk of the District Court** (sometimes called the Circuit Clerk’s Office, since circuit clerks handle filings for all state courts in the county). Key points on choosing the right county (venue) and filing method:

- **Venue (Proper County):** In general, you should file in the county where the **defendant resides** or does business, or where the dispute arose. Alabama law on venue for individuals says *contract* cases must be filed in the county where the defendant (or one of them) resides ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2021/title-6/chapter-3/article-1/section-6-3-2/#:~:text=,the%20state%20a%20permanent%20residence)), and *all other personal actions* (like torts) can be in the defendant’s home county **or** the county “in which the act or omission complained of may have been done or occurred.” ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2021/title-6/chapter-3/article-1/section-6-3-2/#:~:text=,done%20or%20may%20have%20occurred)). For example:
  - If you’re suing an individual, usually file in their home county (if they live in Alabama) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2021/title-6/chapter-3/article-1/section-6-3-2/#:~:text=,the%20state%20a%20permanent%20residence)). 
  - If the dispute is over something that happened in another county, that county is also acceptable venue ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2021/title-6/chapter-3/article-1/section-6-3-2/#:~:text=,done%20or%20may%20have%20occurred)). E.g., a car accident happened in County X but the defendant lives in County Y; you may file in X (where accident occurred) *or* Y (where defendant lives) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2021/title-6/chapter-3/article-1/section-6-3-2/#:~:text=,done%20or%20may%20have%20occurred)). 
  - If it’s a **contract**, say you entered into an agreement in County A but the defendant lives in County B, venue is in County B under the contract rule ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2021/title-6/chapter-3/article-1/section-6-3-2/#:~:text=,the%20state%20a%20permanent%20residence)). (There’s a nuance: if the contract specifies a place of performance and breach happened elsewhere, but safe approach is defendant’s residence for contracts).
  - For a **business defendant (company)**: Alabama venue statute for corporations (Ala. Code § 6-3-7) basically says a domestic corporation can be sued in the county of its **principal office** or where the **cause of action arose** if the corporation does business there, or where the plaintiff resides if the corporation does business there ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2021/title-6/chapter-3/article-1/section-6-3-2/#:~:text=Alabama%20Code%20%C2%A7%206,Code%20of%20Alabama%20Title%206)) ([judicial.alabama.gov](https://judicial.alabama.gov/library/CivilProcedure#:~:text=,Justices%20and%20Judges%3A%20Bios)). For foreign corporations, similar plus where it does business. Simpler: If suing a business, often you can file where the wrong happened or where their main place of business or registered agent is located. 
  - **Multiple defendants:** If you have several defendants in different counties, venue is okay in a county where *any one* defendant resides ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2021/title-6/chapter-3/article-1/section-6-3-2/#:~:text=,the%20state%20a%20permanent%20residence)) (for contracts) or where any defendant resides or act occurred (for torts). 
  - **Out-of-state defendants:** If the person or company is **not in Alabama**, you can still file in Alabama if the dispute is connected to Alabama and the court has *personal jurisdiction* (e.g. they did business in Alabama or the incident happened in Alabama). Venue in such case could be in the county where the claim arose ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2021/title-6/chapter-3/article-1/section-6-3-2/#:~:text=in%20which%20the%20defendant%20or,a%20material%20defendant%20resides)) or where you (plaintiff) reside for equitable matters ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2021/title-6/chapter-3/article-1/section-6-3-2/#:~:text=be%20commenced%20in%20the%20county,are%20pending%20or%20judgment%20entered)). For a non-resident defendant in a law case, Alabama venue is basically the county where the incident happened or property is located ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2021/title-6/chapter-3/article-1/section-6-3-2/#:~:text=in%20which%20the%20defendant%20or,a%20material%20defendant%20resides)). So, if someone from Georgia wrecked your car in Birmingham, you can file in Jefferson County (Birmingham’s county). The court will have to serve them out of state (allowed by certified mail).
  - **Examples:** If you’re a Huntsville resident owed money by someone who lives in Mobile, you should file in Mobile County (defendant’s home) in general – unless the contract was made in Huntsville and some exception applies (but Alabama doesn’t have broad plaintiff venue for contracts beyond defendant’s home). If you sue a Montgomery business because of something that happened at their Montgomery store, file in Montgomery County (where it happened and they operate). Venue can sometimes overlap; when in doubt, filing in defendant’s county is safest.

  Filing in the wrong county can result in a **transfer or dismissal**. If the defendant raises improper venue, the court may **transfer** the case to the correct county (usually the filing fee you paid can be transferred, but you might have to pay a small transfer fee or at least another service fee). If venue is clearly wrong and contested, better to refile in correct county to avoid delays. It’s important because small claims forms don’t usually require you to state venue basis, but the defendant can object. If both parties show up and don’t object, sometimes the court will let it proceed even if technically wrong venue, but that’s risky.

- **Which court within the county:** All small claims are heard by the District Court of that county. You might see them referred to as “Small Claims Court of ___ County.” They are not separate courts but a division of the District Court. **Use the county’s Circuit Clerk’s office** to file – that office handles District Court filings as well (circuit clerks serve both circuit and district). In some smaller counties, the Circuit Clerk doubles as District Clerk. In larger counties, they may have a specific **small claims clerk window** or desk. For example, Jefferson County (Birmingham) has a special small claims division location. But generally, go to the main courthouse where the clerk is.

- **Electronic Filing (e-filing):** Alabama has an e-filing system called **AlaFile** for online filing of court documents ([www.troymessenger.com](https://www.troymessenger.com/2015/08/05/small-claims-court-increases-claim-amount-limitations/#:~:text=Scarbrough%20also%20said%20that%20business,those%20wishing%20to%20file%20claims)). E-filing is *mandatory for attorneys* statewide in civil cases, but **optional for self-represented individuals**. If you are a pro se (self-rep) plaintiff, you currently *cannot* just spontaneously file online without setting up an account and possibly getting clerk approval – however, Alabama encourages even pro se to use AlaFile if possible ([www.troymessenger.com](https://www.troymessenger.com/2015/08/05/small-claims-court-increases-claim-amount-limitations/#:~:text=Scarbrough%20also%20said%20that%20business,those%20wishing%20to%20file%20claims)). According to a 2015 update, pro se individuals can register for an AlaFile account (you have to fill a registration and appear at clerk’s office to verify identity) ([shelby.alacourt.gov](https://shelby.alacourt.gov/pro-se-alafile-registration/#:~:text=Welcome%20to%20the%20Shelby%20County,of%20the%20email%20you%20receive)). Once set, you could e-file your small claim from home. The advantage is convenience; the drawback is figuring out the system. Many people simply file by mail or in person. Some counties explicitly mention that businesses or individuals can use AlaFile and that it’s free except a **4% convenience fee** on any payments (filing fee payment) ([www.troymessenger.com](https://www.troymessenger.com/2015/08/05/small-claims-court-increases-claim-amount-limitations/#:~:text=Scarbrough%20also%20said%20that%20business,those%20wishing%20to%20file%20claims)). That 4% credit card processing fee **can be added to your judgment as a cost** ([www.troymessenger.com](https://www.troymessenger.com/2015/08/05/small-claims-court-increases-claim-amount-limitations/#:~:text=claims)) (as noted by the Pike County clerk, they allow it to be collected from defendant). If you want to try e-filing:
   - Go to the [AlaFile website](https://alafile.alacourt.gov) and register. You select user type as “Self-Represented Individual” or “Business,” and fill info. Then the system will instruct you to present a printed form to the clerk in person for approval ([shelby.alacourt.gov](https://shelby.alacourt.gov/pro-se-alafile-registration/#:~:text=Welcome%20to%20the%20Shelby%20County,of%20the%20email%20you%20receive)). After approval, you can file online.
   - Alternatively, some counties have their own guides. Shelby County’s site, for instance, encourages pro se use of AlaFile and describes the sign-up ([shelby.alacourt.gov](https://shelby.alacourt.gov/pro-se-alafile-registration/#:~:text=Welcome%20to%20the%20Shelby%20County,of%20the%20email%20you%20receive)).
   - If you don’t want to e-file, **in-person filing** at the courthouse clerk’s office is perfectly fine. Many small claims are filed by walking into the clerk’s office, asking for a “Statement of Claim” form, filling it out, and paying the fee. It’s straightforward and the clerks can ensure you fill everything needed (though they can’t give legal advice).
   - **Filing by mail:** You can also file by postal mail. You’d need to obtain the Statement of Claim form (from Alabama Administrative Office of Courts website or the local clerk’s site – many have PDFs), fill it out, sign it before a notary or court clerk (some forms need notarization if not signing in clerk’s presence), and mail it with the correct filing fee payment (money order or cashier’s check typically, to the Clerk of Court). The clerk will then file it and send you a case number or receipt. If you do this, call the clerk’s office to verify the mailing address and payee for the fee, and to see if they require any extra copies, etc. Some counties provide instructions for filing by mail on their websites. Expect some delay for processing by mail.
   - **E-portal for forms:** Even if not e-filing, you can find forms online. The Alabama AOC E-Forms site lists **Small Claims Statement of Claim (General)** and **for Specific Property** forms ready to download ([eforms.alacourt.gov](https://eforms.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=10%2F1%2F2023%20SM,11%2F16%2F2005)). Many counties also have local fillable PDFs or printed forms at the clerk’s.

- **Filing in wrong court:** If you mistakenly file in Circuit Court or District Civil when it should be small claims, the case might be reassigned to the small claims docket. Small claims is actually within District Court, so usually the clerk will just flag it as a small claim if it meets criteria. Conversely, if you file in small claims but it doesn’t belong there (like it’s actually an eviction or above limit), the clerk may reject it or the judge will dismiss or move it. It’s on you to choose correctly. Use “Small Claims” forms to be safe.

- **Service of process considerations by county:** When you file, you must also arrange **service of the summons and complaint** on the defendant. In Alabama small claims, you typically have these options: **Sheriff’s service** or **Certified Mail by clerk**, and in some cases **process server** or **publication** (if needed). We’ll detail this in Section 8. For now, know that when you file, the clerk will ask how you want to serve the defendant and collect fees accordingly. If the defendant is local, many choose sheriff or private process; if they are out-of-state or have a PO box, certified mail is often used (the clerk sends by certified mail with return receipt) ([shelby.alacourt.gov](https://shelby.alacourt.gov/small-claims/small-claims-faq/#:~:text=concerning%20your%20case%29,You%20should%20have)). Some counties, like Russell, note “Service by Sheriff on all civil filings: $15” ([russell.alacourt.gov](https://russell.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Filing%20Fee%20,%2417.00%C2%A0each)) – meaning if you file, they assume sheriff unless you specify otherwise or it’s out-of-state. Shelby’s FAQ states after processing, the case “is then sent to the Sheriff’s Department for service on the defendant(s), or issued by Certified Mail or UPS Commercial Carrier whichever process you stated” ([shelby.alacourt.gov](https://shelby.alacourt.gov/small-claims/small-claims-faq/#:~:text=concerning%20your%20case%29,You%20should%20have)). So be clear on your filing form which method (there’s a checkbox on the form). If e-filing, you’ll select service type electronically.

- **Court location:** The case will be heard in the courthouse or annex for that county’s District Court. Some counties have multiple courthouses (like Jefferson has a Birmingham and Bessemer division). Ensure you file in the correct division if that matters (Jefferson County is split by district – if the defendant resides in Bessemer Cutoff, you file there). Most counties just have one location. The clerk will assign a case number (format often “SM-XX-XXXXX” or “DV” for District Civil – sometimes small claims cases may have “SM” or just a CV number but flagged as small claims; e.g. “DC-2026-000123”).

- **Hearing assignment:** After filing, the clerk will usually set a **trial date** and issue the summons. In some counties, they set a date as soon as service is achieved or an answer is filed. Many Alabama counties automatically set a **first court date about 4–8 weeks out** when you file, and it’s included on the summons sent to defendant. For example, you file on June 1, they might set hearing July 15 at 9:00 AM small claims courtroom. Check with the clerk if and when you’ll be notified of the court date. Some counties may not schedule until the defendant answers or the 14-day answer period passes, but the **Alabama Small Claims Rules** encourage a quick setting (and Shelby’s info indicates trial usually 6–8 weeks from answer date ([shelby.alacourt.gov](https://shelby.alacourt.gov/small-claims/small-claims-faq/#:~:text=mail%20with%20a%20computer%20print,order%20to%20subpoena%20any%20witnesses))). You, as plaintiff, will usually get a notice via mail of the court date as well (if you e-file, you get e-notice). Make sure the clerk always has your current address.

- **If you file in the wrong county by mistake:** The defendant can file a motion to transfer or dismiss for improper venue. The small claims judge could choose to just transfer it to the correct county’s small claims (especially if the defendant doesn’t want to kill it, just move it). There is a $50 transfer fee mandated by law for transferring a case to another county (Ala. Code § 12-11-11). Usually the plaintiff would have to pay that up front (and maybe recover it later as costs if they win). To avoid that, double-check venue.

- **List of county clerk contacts:** You can find each county’s circuit clerk address and phone on the Alabama Judicial System website or the local circuit court site. For example, “Montgomery County Circuit Clerk – Small Claims Division, address, etc.” If you’re unsure of where to go, call the clerk and say “I need to file a small claim, could you direct me?”

In summary: **file your case in a proper Alabama county** (usually where defendant lives or problem happened). Go to the **District Court clerk’s office** (often on the same floor as circuit clerk) to file, or use Alabama’s AlaFile system if you are comfortable. Pay the filing fee (discussed next section) and provide the necessary information on the complaint form. The clerk will issue a case number and handle service of process per your chosen method. Then your job is to show up on the court date. 

*(Next, we’ll cover the fees you must pay to file and serve your case.)*

## 4. Filing Fees

There is a **filing fee (court cost deposit) required to start a small claims case**, which varies based on the amount you’re suing for. Alabama’s filing fees for small claims are set by statute and include a base amount plus various surcharges. In practice, the **total fee differs slightly by county** because some local law library or sheriff’s fees may be added. Below is the standard fee schedule and typical ranges (for *one* defendant) as of 2026:

- **Claim up to $1,500.00:** Base filing fee is **$35** by statute ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-12/chapter-19/article-3/division-2/section-12-19-71/#:~:text=,in%20civil%20cases%20shall%20be)). After surcharges, counties charge around **$50 to $102** total for one defendant. For example, Marion County charges $50 ([marion.alacourt.gov](https://marion.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=The%20Small%20Claims%20Court%20is,the%20judgment%20to%20Circuit%20Court)) ([marion.alacourt.gov](https://marion.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Fee%20Description%20%20,%24213.00)), while Lee/Chambers Counties charge $102 ([chambers.alacourt.gov](https://chambers.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Fee%20Description%20%20,%2430.00)). (The variation is due to local additions like a law library fee, Judicial Administration Fund fee, etc.)

- **Claim $1,500.01 to $3,000.00:** Base filing fee is **$109** by statute ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-12/chapter-19/article-3/division-2/section-12-19-71/#:~:text=,in%20determining%20the%20jurisdictional%20amount)). Counties typically charge around **$124 to $176** total. Marion shows $124 ([marion.alacourt.gov](https://marion.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Fee%20Description%20%20,%24213.00)), Lee shows $176 ([chambers.alacourt.gov](https://chambers.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Fee%20Description%20%20,%2430.00)). Others like Russell fall in between at $159 ([russell.alacourt.gov](https://russell.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Filing%20Fee%20,%2450.00%20%28%24500%20max)).

- **Claim $3,000.01 to $6,000.00:** Base filing fee is **$198** by statute (this is actually the same fee as a regular District Court case up to $20k) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-12/chapter-19/article-3/division-2/section-12-19-71/#:~:text=%283%29%20One%20hundred%20ninety,in%20determining%20the%20jurisdictional%20amount)). Counties charge roughly **$213 to $265** total for one defendant. Marion lists $213 ([marion.alacourt.gov](https://marion.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Fee%20Description%20%20,%24213.00)), Lee lists $265 ([chambers.alacourt.gov](https://chambers.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Filing%20Fee%20,%2430.00)), Russell $248 ([russell.alacourt.gov](https://russell.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Filing%20Fee%20,%2450.00%20%28%24500%20max)). These include that the law requires if claim > $1,500, the docket fee equals the general civil fee ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,are%20not%20small%20claims%20cases)), and then locals add some.

For convenience, here is a **table** summarizing typical filing fees (including surcharges) for one defendant, based on data from several counties:

| **Claim Amount**           | **Filing Fee (1 Defendant)**        | **Example Sources**                   |
|---------------------------|-------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| $0 up to $1,500           | about **$50 – $102** (varies)       | Marion Co. $50 ([marion.alacourt.gov](https://marion.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Fee%20Description%20%20,%24213.00)); Lee Co. $102 ([chambers.alacourt.gov](https://chambers.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Filing%20Fee%20,%2430.00)); Russell Co. $85 ([russell.alacourt.gov](https://russell.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Fee%20Description%20%20,%2450.00%20%28%24500%20max)) |
| $1,500.01 up to $3,000    | about **$124 – $176** (varies)      | Marion $124 ([marion.alacourt.gov](https://marion.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Fee%20Description%20%20,%24213.00)); Lee $176 ([chambers.alacourt.gov](https://chambers.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Fee%20Description%20%20,%2430.00)); Russell $159 ([russell.alacourt.gov](https://russell.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Fee%20Description%20%20,%2450.00%20%28%24500%20max))      |
| $3,000.01 up to $6,000    | about **$213 – $265** (varies)      | Marion $213 ([marion.alacourt.gov](https://marion.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Fee%20Description%20%20,%24213.00)); Lee $265 ([chambers.alacourt.gov](https://chambers.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Filing%20Fee%20,%2430.00)); Russell $248 ([russell.alacourt.gov](https://russell.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Filing%20Fee%20,%2450.00%20%28%24500%20max))      |

*(These amounts include the statutory base fee plus add-ons like Judicial and facility fees, etc. Counties update fees periodically, so check with the clerk for current exact fee.)*

- **Additional Defendants:** If you are suing more than one defendant, there is a small extra fee per additional defendant. Typically **$10 – $15** for each extra defendant beyond the first ([marion.alacourt.gov](https://marion.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Filing%20Fee%20,00%20per%20additional%20defendant)) ([russell.alacourt.gov](https://russell.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Filing%20Fee%20,%2450.00%20%28%24500%20max)). For instance, Marion and Lee show $10 per additional defendant ([marion.alacourt.gov](https://marion.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Filing%20Fee%20,00%20per%20additional%20defendant)) ([chambers.alacourt.gov](https://chambers.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Filing%20Fee%20,%2430.00)), Russell shows $15 ([russell.alacourt.gov](https://russell.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Filing%20Fee%20,%2450.00%20%28%24500%20max)). So if you sue 2 defendants, the filing fee might increase by that amount. This covers the cost of issuing an extra summons and service.

- **Service of Process Fees:** Service is a separate cost, but often collected at filing. In Alabama, service by **Sheriff** costs around **$20** in many counties for in-county service ([lee.alacourt.gov](https://lee.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Sheriff%20Service%20%20,%2420.00)) (Lee shows $20 ([lee.alacourt.gov](https://lee.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Sheriff%20Service%20%20,%2420.00)); other counties often $20–$30). Actually, our county data: Shelby lists Sheriff service $20 ([lee.alacourt.gov](https://lee.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Sheriff%20Service%20%20,%2420.00)), Russell lists $15 (maybe subsidized) ([russell.alacourt.gov](https://russell.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Additional%20Defendant%20%20,%2435.00)). Service by **Certified Mail** through the clerk typically costs around **$7–$10** (the postal certified mail fee plus return receipt). Some counties explicitly list a “Certified mail” fee if applicable (Russell implies certified is used for PO boxes/out-of-state but didn’t list cost – likely they include it in base or charge similarly to sheriff). If you’re unsure, ask the clerk “how much for service by certified mail” if you choose that. If the defendant resides out-of-county but in Alabama, sheriff of that county will charge a fee (the clerk usually coordinates that and includes the fee). Budget roughly $10–$20 per address for service. 
    - Many counties have a flat **“Service by Sheriff” fee** which you pay upfront per defendant address (like $20 each) ([lee.alacourt.gov](https://lee.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Sheriff%20Service%20%20,%2420.00)). If you want an out-of-state service, certified mail is usually the way because sending a sheriff out-of-state isn’t feasible – some states allow private process, but Alabama clerk can’t force an out-of-state sheriff (you’d have to arrange it).
    - If you decide to hire a **private process server**, you pay them separately, not through the court, and you’d just pay filing fee without sheriff fee and later file proof of service. But in small claims, using the sheriff or certified mail is common and simpler, and not too expensive.

- **Fee for Default Judgment Application:** If your case goes to default (defendant doesn’t answer), some counties (like Lee) have an additional $50 fee for processing a default in claims above $3,000 ([lee.alacourt.gov](https://lee.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Additional%20Plaintiffs%20%20,%2430.00)). Also Alabama law sets a fee for filing a default judgment application in district court in some situations. For small claims under $3k, it might be lower or none. But indeed Lee’s schedule shows: default judgment fee $50 for cases $3,001–$6,000 ([lee.alacourt.gov](https://lee.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Additional%20Defendant%20%20,%2430.00)), none listed for under $3k (so presumably default doesn’t cost extra under $3k). Marion did not list a default fee in the snippet. If a default fee is required, the clerk typically will notify you when you move for default. This fee could ultimately be taxed to defendant if you win (since costs follow judgment). 

- **Payment methods:** You can usually pay by cash (in person), money order or cashier’s check (especially if mailing), and often by credit/debit card (most clerk’s offices accept cards, sometimes with a small processing fee ~4% ([www.troymessenger.com](https://www.troymessenger.com/2015/08/05/small-claims-court-increases-claim-amount-limitations/#:~:text=claims))). For e-filing, you pay electronically (card) and the convenience fee ~4% is added but recoverable in judgment ([www.troymessenger.com](https://www.troymessenger.com/2015/08/05/small-claims-court-increases-claim-amount-limitations/#:~:text=claims)). Always make sure any check or money order is payable to “Clerk of Court” or similar (the clerk can tell you exact payee). If paying by card in person, bring ID that matches the card.

- **Fee waivers (In Forma Pauperis):** If you cannot afford the filing fee, you can apply for a **fee waiver** due to indigence. Alabama law allows indigent plaintiffs to file **“Affidavit of Substantial Hardship” (Form C-10)** and if approved by a judge, the fees can be postponed or waived ([eforms.alacourt.gov](https://eforms.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Form%20Number%20%20,6%2F10%2F2025)). To do this, you fill out the **Affidavit of Substantial Hardship and Order** form (AOC form C-10 for civil) detailing your income, expenses, and that you can’t pay the costs up front ([eforms.alacourt.gov](https://eforms.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Form%20Number%20%20,6%2F10%2F2025)). The clerk can provide this form or it’s online ([eforms.alacourt.gov](https://eforms.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Form%20Number%20%20,6%2F10%2F2025)). A judge will review it – often it’s approved if you’re low-income (e.g. on public benefits or below certain poverty guideline). If granted, your case is filed **without upfront payment** ([eforms.alacourt.gov](https://eforms.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Small%20Claims%20Form%20Number%20,Complaint)). However, the judge can order that costs be paid later or out of any judgment you win. If you lose, sometimes the state or opposing party might not enforce those costs; if you win, the costs just come from defendant as usual. If denied, you’d have to pay to proceed. 
    - Each county may have local thresholds. Provide documentation of income if possible. The form must be notarized or signed before a clerk. Some counties combine the affidavit with the petition in one doc C-10. 
    - Note: There’s also a specific form **C-10A Notice that Hardship granted** ([eforms.alacourt.gov](https://eforms.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Form%20Number%20%20,7%2F24%2F2012)) that clerks use to confirm it.
    - *Important:* A fee waiver covers **court costs and service by sheriff**; it does not necessarily cover out-of-pocket costs like hiring a private process server or subpoena witness fees (though you can request the sheriff serve subpoenas as part of cost). But typically if you have a hardship order, the sheriff will serve without prepayment and those costs get taxed at end.
    - If you are low-income, definitely pursue this – it should not deter you from filing. Alabama’s constitution ensures the right to sue is not denied due to poverty.

- **Recovery of fees:** If you win your case, the **filing fee and service fees are added to the judgment** against the defendant ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,the%20court%20shall%20award%20to)). As mentioned, Alabama DTPA even explicitly lists costs as recoverable ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,the%20court%20shall%20award%20to)), but even outside that, it’s standard that the losing party pays costs. The judgment order will usually say "plus court costs" ([shelby.alacourt.gov](https://shelby.alacourt.gov/small-claims/small-claims-faq/#:~:text=If%20the%20defendant%20does%20not,available%20in%20the%20clerk%27s%20office)). So you should get reimbursed. If the defendant pays before trial (settles), they often will include filing fees if you insist (since they know they’d owe it in judgment). If you lose the case, you lose the fee (and could be responsible for defendant’s costs, though that’s usually just maybe their service fee if any). But there’s no refund of filing fee from the court if you lose; it’s spent. The only times fee might be refunded is if you filed in wrong court and it’s dismissed possibly without prejudice – rarely the court might refund, but generally filing fees are non-refundable once case commenced (as echoed by Marion’s note "Filing Fees are Nonrefundable" ([marion.alacourt.gov](https://marion.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=))).

- **Other fees that might arise:**
   - If you need to **subpoena a witness**, there is a fee for subpoena issuance (around $12 in some counties) ([chambers.alacourt.gov](https://chambers.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Garnishment%20%20,%2412.00%20per%20subpoena)) ([russell.alacourt.gov](https://russell.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Dispositive%20Motion%20Fee%20%20,%2417.00%C2%A0each)) plus a service fee if sheriff serves it. E.g., Russell: $17 each subpoena ([russell.alacourt.gov](https://russell.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Dispositive%20Motion%20Fee%20%20,%2417.00%C2%A0each)) and $15 to serve. That can also be recovered if you win (if you remember to ask to tax it as cost). Keep receipts or proof of payment for any subpoenas served.
   - If you later need to **garnish wages or bank accounts** to collect a judgment, there are filing fees for those post-judgment writs (often ~$30) ([russell.alacourt.gov](https://russell.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Additional%20Plaintiffs%20%20,%2417.00%C2%A0each)). For example, Russell lists **Garnishment $35**, **Execution $35** ([russell.alacourt.gov](https://russell.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Additional%20Plaintiffs%20%20,%2417.00%C2%A0each)). Those can be added to the debt you collect from defendant as well. We will discuss this in the Post-judgment section.
   - If you file a **Notice of Appeal** because you lost and want to appeal to Circuit, there is a hefty filing fee for appeals (currently $302 for non-jury appeal in circuit) ([shelby.alacourt.gov](https://shelby.alacourt.gov/small-claims/small-claims-faq/#:~:text=available%20here%3A%20Notice%20of%20Appeal,Office%20also%20has%20this%20form)). We’ll cover that in Appeals. But know it’s much more than small claims fee because you’re essentially paying the circuit court fee at that point.

**To summarize fees:**

- The filing fee is roughly $85 for small claims up to $1,500, around $159 for claims to $3k, and around $248 for claims to $6k (these are mid-range estimates), inclusive of surcharges ([russell.alacourt.gov](https://russell.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Fee%20Description%20%20,%2450.00%20%28%24500%20max)). Each county may differ by $10

content.text:
## 1. Jurisdictional limits — MAXIMUM AMOUNT YOU CAN SUE FOR

- **Monetary limit:** Alabama small claims courts can handle claims up to **$6,000 (not including interest or court costs)** ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,promulgated%20by%20the%20Supreme%20Court)). This $6,000 cap is the **maximum amount of money** you can ask for in your lawsuit (sometimes called the “amount in controversy”). If your claim is even one cent over $6,000, the case **cannot** be filed as a small claim – it would have to go to the regular District or Circuit Court. The $6,000 limit is set by law in Ala. Code § 12-12-31(a) ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,promulgated%20by%20the%20Supreme%20Court)). It has been $6,000 since 2015 (raised from the previous $3,000 limit) ([www.troymessenger.com](https://www.troymessenger.com/2015/08/05/small-claims-court-increases-claim-amount-limitations/#:~:text=Small%20business%20owners%20and%20others,3%2C000%20jurisdictional%20limitation%20any%20longer)). *Practical tip:* If your total loss is slightly above $6,000, you **cannot split it** into two small claims cases – you must either **reduce/“waive”** the amount above $6,000 or use a higher court. (Intentionally splitting one claim into multiple suits is generally not allowed and could get your case dismissed as an abuse of process.)

- **No different limits by plaintiff type:** The $6,000 cap applies to **all plaintiffs**, whether you are an individual, a small business, a corporation, an LLC, etc. There is no lower cap for businesses – everyone gets the same $6,000 maximum in Alabama small claims ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,promulgated%20by%20the%20Supreme%20Court)). Likewise, Alabama doesn’t impose an aggregate yearly limit on the number of small claims filings or total recovery. (In some states like California, a business can only file a certain number of claims per year, or individuals can only sue for over a certain amount twice a year – but **Alabama has no such restrictions**. You could theoretically file many small claims, as long as each claim itself is within $6,000.)

- **Exclusive jurisdiction:** If your claim is for $6,000 or less, the District Court’s small claims division has *exclusive* jurisdiction – meaning larger courts won’t hear it ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,promulgated%20by%20the%20Supreme%20Court)). Conversely, if it’s more than $6,000, the small claims court *cannot* hear it. Alabama District Courts (outside the small claims docket) can hear cases up to $20,000, and Circuit Courts handle over $20,000 ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-11-30/#:~:text=%281%29%20CIVIL,exclusive%20of%20interest%20and%20costs)). But **$6,000 is the hard cut-off** for the simplified small claims process. (If a counterclaim from a defendant pushes the amount over $6,000, see the Counterclaims section below on how that is handled.)

- **Interest and costs not counted:** When checking the $6,000 limit, **do not count** any interest, court costs, or attorney fees you might also request – the $6,000 is measured on the base claim amount. Ala. Code § 12-12-31 says “exclusive of interest and costs” ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,promulgated%20by%20the%20Supreme%20Court)). For example, if you’re demanding $5,800 in principal and also ask for ~$500 in accrued interest and a $200 filing fee, that’s okay – it doesn’t push you over the limit. But if the *principal* of your claim is $6,500, you’re over the limit and cannot use small claims (even if you’re willing to drop interest or costs). You’d need to either waive $500 of the principal (suing for $6,000 max) or file in a higher court.

- **Cannot divide a claim to fit:** You **must not split** one larger claim into two or more smaller claims to evade the limit. Courts consider that improper. For instance, if a defendant owes you $10,000 on one contract, you cannot file two separate suits for $5,000 each – that’s called claim splitting and is not allowed. The court would likely dismiss or merge such cases. The proper course is either suing for the full amount in District/Circuit Court or **agreeing to only pursue $6,000 of it** in small claims (essentially forgiving the rest). If you try to split a single cause of action across multiple small claims, the judge may dismiss the later case(s) as duplicative.

- **No yearly filing limits:** Some states limit how many claims over a certain amount a person can file per year (to prevent abuse by frequent filers). Alabama **does not** have any statute or rule limiting the number of small claims cases you can file in a year or the total amount you can sue for per year. As long as each individual claim is within the $6,000 jurisdictional limit, you can file as many legitimate small claims as needed. The court might scrutinize volume filers for proper purposes (especially companies suing on assigned debts – see below), but there is no formal numeric cap.

- **Assigned debt and collection claims:** Alabama does have a special rule that **collection agencies or other assignees** of a claim *must be represented by a licensed attorney* to file in small claims ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,original%20owner%20of%20the%20claim)). This doesn’t change the $6,000 limit, but it means if, say, a debt buyer bought a $2,000 credit card debt, the debt buyer cannot have a non-lawyer agent file the small claim – they’d need an attorney. This rule, in Ala. Code § 12-12-31(d), is intended to prevent mass filings by unlicensed collection agents. It doesn’t stop the suit; it just requires they hire an attorney (who could instead file in District Civil if they wanted, but often they still use small claims for speed). If an assignee plaintiff files without an attorney, the case can be dismissed. **Original creditors** (the actual business owed money) and individuals can file on their own. There’s also a rule barring anyone whose law license is suspended/revoked from filing on behalf of someone else ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=assignee%20of%20the%20claim%20which,original%20owner%20of%20the%20claim)) (to prevent disbarred lawyers from acting as “agents”). 

- **Parties and representation:** The types of parties who can use small claims include **individuals, sole proprietors, partnerships, and corporations** – basically any legal entity – as either plaintiff or defendant ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,time%20employee%20of%20the%20corporation)). Alabama allows businesses to appear **without an attorney** in small claims (which is not allowed in some states). If a **partnership** comes to court without a lawyer, a partner or employee can represent it. If a **corporation** comes without a lawyer, an officer or full-time employee of the corporation must be the representative ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=cases%20on%20the%20small%20claims,time%20employee%20of%20the%20corporation)). (That person should have authority to negotiate and settle on behalf of the company.) So, for example, a small LLC can have its owner or manager file the case and appear in court – hiring an attorney is optional in small claims. The same goes for a plaintiff that is a county or city agency (they often send a staff employee). One exception: as noted, an assignee of a claim must use an attorney to prosecute the claim ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,original%20owner%20of%20the%20claim)). Also, if you want to **recover attorney’s fees** as part of judgment (because a contract or statute allows it), you actually have to have an attorney – a self-represented person can’t claim “attorney fees” for their own time ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,represented%20by%20a%20licensed%20attorney)) (more on that in the Damages section). 

In summary: **$6,000 is the ceiling** for Alabama small claims cases. It’s a firm limit per case (excluding add-on costs/interest). The limit is the same regardless of who is suing. You can’t circumvent it by chopping up a claim or by filing in another county. If you are just over the limit, you might choose to **waive the excess** to stay in small claims – many people do this because small claims is simpler and cheaper than higher courts. Just understand that if you win, you’re forfeiting any amount above $6,000. If you need every dollar, you’ll have to file in District or Circuit Court with more formal procedure. Small claims exists for relatively small disputes where speed and low cost are more important than full legal process.

## 2. What types of cases qualify — EVERYTHING YOU CAN SUE FOR

Alabama small claims courts can handle a broad range of **civil disputes involving money or personal property** (not real estate) as long as they’re within the $6,000 limit. The key question is: are you seeking a **monetary judgment** or return of property worth ≤ $6,000? If yes, it probably fits – unless some law carves it out (see the next section for exclusions). Below is an exhaustive list of case types commonly brought in small claims, organized by category. For each, we explain if it’s allowed in Alabama, give real-life examples, cite any special Alabama statutes or requirements, and note quirks.

### Contract & money-owed disputes

These are among the most typical small claims cases. **If someone breached an agreement or owes you money**, small claims is usually an appropriate forum.

- **Breach of written contract:** *Eligible.* If you and someone signed a contract (or you have another written agreement, like a signed estimate or invoice) and they didn’t honor it, you can sue for the financial loss up to $6,000. Examples: a contractor signed a written home repair contract but never did the work; a caterer took your deposit under a written agreement and then failed to show up; or a customer refuses to pay a bill for services rendered under a written contract. You’ll want to bring a **copy of the signed contract** to court. Alabama follows ordinary contract law – there’s no requirement to send a demand letter or do anything special before suing (though it’s generally wise to *request* performance or payment one last time). If the contract has a clause about attorneys’ fees or specific damages, note that in small claims those can only be awarded if you had an attorney or if they are a fixed liquidated sum – otherwise, you’ll likely just get your actual loss ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,represented%20by%20a%20licensed%20attorney)). Alabama has a 6-year statute of limitations for written contracts (and 6 years for most unwritten contracts as well), so make sure you file within 6 years of the breach (sooner is better) to avoid any limitations defense. No special Alabama statute governs garden-variety breach of contract suits in small claims – it’s basic common law enforcement of agreements.

- **Breach of oral contract (handshake agreements):** *Eligible.* Verbal agreements are generally enforceable in Alabama (except certain types that by law must be written, like real estate sales or agreements that can’t be performed within a year – those are governed by the Statute of Frauds). If you had an **oral agreement** and the other party broke their promise, you can sue for up to $6,000. Examples: you agreed to sell your used bike to a friend for $300 and they took the bike but never paid; or you hired someone verbally to paint your fence for $500 and you paid them, but they never did the work. The challenge with oral contracts is *proof* – you’ll need evidence of the terms and the breach. This can be witness testimony, text/email confirmations (“I’ll pay you back next month”), partial payments made (implying a loan), etc. Alabama’s limitation period for unwritten contracts or quasi-contracts is generally 6 years as well (for claims accruing after 1984, Alabama has mostly a universal 6-year limit for contract claims ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2021/title-12/chapter-19/article-3/division-2/section-12-19-71/#:~:text=%281%29%20Thirty,in%20determining%20the%20jurisdictional%20amount)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2021/title-12/chapter-19/article-3/division-2/section-12-19-71/#:~:text=%283%29%20One%20hundred%20ninety,in%20determining%20the%20jurisdictional%20amount))). One caution: if the deal was for the sale of **goods over $500**, under the UCC (Uniform Commercial Code, adopted in Alabama) there ideally should be some writing, but there are exceptions (like if the goods have been delivered and accepted, the oral contract is enforced). In small claims, judges often focus on fairness and evidence of an agreement – if you can convince the judge a deal existed and was broken, you can win even an oral contract case. There’s no requirement of a demand letter or any state agency involvement beforehand.

- **Unpaid loans or promissory notes:** *Eligible.* If you **lent someone money** (with or without a formal promissory note) and they haven’t paid you back, you absolutely can sue in small claims for the balance owed (plus any interest if it was agreed, or legal interest if not). Common scenario: personal loans between friends or family. For example, you loaned your cousin $2,000 to fix their car, they promised orally to repay in 2 months, and now it’s a year later with no payment – this is perfect for small claims. If you have a **written IOU or promissory note**, bring it – a signed note is strong evidence. Even without a note, you can prove a loan by showing you transferred the money (bank withdrawal or Venmo record) and any admissions by them that it was a loan (texts like “I will pay you back”). Alabama law doesn’t require any particular notice before suing on a debt. Just be sure the amount is $6,000 or less. If you charged interest or the note had interest terms, you can include interest up to the date of judgment (see “Damages” section for interest rates). By default, a loan that doesn’t specify interest may accrue **6% annual interest** in Alabama ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-12/chapter-19/article-3/division-2/section-12-19-71/#:~:text=%281%29%20Thirty,in%20determining%20the%20jurisdictional%20amount)), but you need to ask for it. Many people just sue for principal to keep things simple. Also, Alabama’s small claims code provides that no **attorney fees** can be awarded unless an attorney represented you ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,represented%20by%20a%20licensed%20attorney)) – so even if your note says “attorney’s fees on default,” if you’re pro se you won’t get a separate fee award. Stick to the actual loan amount and any contractual interest. (If the person who owes you filed bankruptcy, that’s another story – you generally can’t sue due to the bankruptcy stay/discharge. Consult a lawyer in that case.)

- **Unpaid invoices or accounts (for goods or services):** *Eligible.* If you’re a business owner or individual who provided goods or services and billed the customer, but they didn’t pay, you can sue for the unpaid amount. This could be an **open account** (like a running tab or line of credit) or a specific invoice. Examples: a freelance web designer completed a project and the client never paid the $1,200 invoice; a supplier sold $4,000 of materials to a contractor on account and wasn’t paid; a medical office is owed a patient’s deductible portion that the patient never paid. These fall under contract law (implied or explicit contract to pay for goods/services). Alabama allows such suits – often titled “Account Stated” or “Open Account” in legalese – and you should bring documentation: the invoices, any delivery receipts or work orders showing the job was done or goods delivered, and any communications from the client. There’s no special pre-suit requirement (for instance, some states require a 30-day demand for certain debts – Alabama doesn’t, except for the bad check scenario below). If finance charges or late fees were part of the agreement, you can claim those as well, but ensure the total stays ≤ $6k. One statutory quirk: Alabama law on **assigned accounts** requires a lawyer if a debt was assigned ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,original%20owner%20of%20the%20claim)) (as mentioned, a collection agency suing on a purchased debt must use an attorney, even in small claims). So if you’re the original business owed, you can self-represent; if you’re a collection agency employee, technically you need a lawyer to sign the paperwork. Many small claims courts enforce this rule strictly – a non-lawyer debt collector filing could get the case dismissed. So businesses should be mindful of that representation rule.

- **Unpaid rent or lease agreements:** *Eligible.* Small claims court is commonly used by landlords to sue former tenants for **unpaid rent** or by tenants to sue landlords for **refunded rent or deposit** (deposit issues are covered in a separate category below). Important: **Eviction (possession of the property) is not handled in small claims**, but **money claims** arising from rental agreements can be. So, a landlord can’t get an order kicking a tenant out via small claims, but can sue for past-due rent or property damage after the tenant has moved. For example, if a tenant moved out owing 2 months’ rent at $800/month, the landlord can file a small claim for $1,600. Or if a commercial tenant defaulted on a short-term lease and you’re seeking $5,000 in unpaid rent and late fees, that fits under $6k. Landlords should be aware of the **Uniform Residential Landlord-Tenant Act (URLTA)**, which Alabama has adopted (for counties with over 75,000 population) as Ala. Code Title 35, Chapter 9A. Under that law, you generally must give a **7-day notice** for nonpayment of rent and opportunity to cure before termination, etc. By the time you’re suing in small claims, presumably the tenancy is over or rent is clearly owed, but ensure you followed any required notice if you later also did an eviction. (If it’s simply a past tenant who left owing money, you don’t need to send any special notice to file suit for the debt – the lease itself and the ledger are your evidence.) Tenants can likewise sue landlords in small claims for **rent refunds** in some cases – e.g., if you prepaid last month’s rent and the lease ended early and the landlord said they’d refund but didn’t. That would be a breach of contract claim by the tenant. Another example: if a landlord **illegally locked a tenant out** or shut off utilities forcing the tenant to vacate, the tenant could sue for monetary damages (like the cost of a motel or the value of personal items seized). Those scenarios fall under landlord-tenant financial disputes and are allowed in small claims (with the understanding that you’re only getting money, not an injunction or return to the property). The **statute of limitations** for rent (written lease) is 6 years. Also note: if a *tenant* is suing a *landlord* for money, certain notice requirements might apply for specific claims (e.g. a tenant claiming unlawful utility cutoff has to show the landlord willfully did it – Alabama law allows recovery of actual damages in that case and possibly attorney’s fees ([shelby.alacourt.gov](https://shelby.alacourt.gov/small-claims/small-claims-faq/#:~:text=%2A%20Garnishment%20of%20Wages%20,10%20Writ%20of)) ([shelby.alacourt.gov](https://shelby.alacourt.gov/small-claims/small-claims-faq/#:~:text=It%20is%20important%20to%20mention%2C,cannot%20give%20you%20legal%20advice))). But in general, straightforward rent disputes are resolved on basic contract principles.

- **Bounced or bad checks (NSF checks):** *Eligible.* If someone paid you with a check that **bounced** (due to non-sufficient funds or a closed account), you have a cause of action to recover the amount of the check and possibly additional damages. Alabama law provides a specific civil remedy for **dishonored checks**: Ala. Code § 6-5-285 allows the holder of a worthless check to sue the check writer for **all actual damages, plus a possible punitive award and attorney’s fee** ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-6/chapter-5/article-16/section-6-5-285/#:~:text=though%20there%20has%20been%20no,trying%20the%20case%20may%20assess)). In small claims, typically you’d ask for the face amount of the check, any bank fees you incurred, maybe an inconvenience fee or other consequential loss, and the statute says the court “may” award **punitive damages** as it deems appropriate ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-6/chapter-5/article-16/section-6-5-285/#:~:text=though%20there%20has%20been%20no,trying%20the%20case%20may%20assess)). It also requires that the lawsuit be filed within **one year** of the date of the bad check ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-6/chapter-5/article-16/section-6-5-285/#:~:text=have%20a%20right%20of%20action,trying%20the%20case%20may%20assess)) (so act quickly – the statute of limitations for the civil action is 1 year, shorter than usual). To use this statute, the check must have been **unlawfully** dishonored – basically a bad check not honored by the bank. Note: Alabama does **not** require a formal 10-day demand letter for civil recovery (as some states do) – the civil action is allowed “though there has been no prosecution” ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-6/chapter-5/article-16/section-6-5-285/#:~:text=have%20a%20right%20of%20action,trying%20the%20case%20may%20assess)). However, it’s wise to notify the check writer and give them a chance to make it good; sometimes they will upon threat of a lawsuit or criminal action. Alabama’s criminal law (if you wanted to pursue it) requires a 10-day demand letter for bad checks under $500 before prosecution, but that’s separate from this civil route. In small claims court, you can simply show the **bounced check** (bring the original if you have it, or the bank’s returned-check image) and proof you weren’t paid. The judge can award the amount of the check plus perhaps a **penalty** – the law doesn’t fix a multiple (some states say double or triple the check amount automatically; Alabama’s law leaves it to the court as punitive damages) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-6/chapter-5/article-16/section-6-5-285/#:~:text=date%20of%20the%20unlawful%20act,trying%20the%20case%20may%20assess)). In practice, for small claims, judges might award the check amount plus any bank fee (like a $30 NSF fee) and maybe a small additional sum for the trouble, especially if the person never responded. If the check was for something like a purchase that you never got back (e.g. you sold an item and the check bounced, so you lost the item and didn’t get paid), make that clear. Remember that **punitive damages** require “unlawful” intent – bouncing a check is often considered fraudulent intent if not rectified, so the statute allows punitive. But in small claims, since the total is capped at $6,000, you’re not going to get, say, a $5,000 punishment on a $500 check – it would likely be something proportionate. Also note: if the person later **paid the check** after it bounced (e.g. you redeposited or they gave cash), you of course cannot still sue – the issue is resolved (though you could still sue for bank fees or costs caused by the bounce if they didn’t cover those). Often just the filing of the case prompts immediate payment from the check writer to avoid a judgment. If you win, you can also recover court costs and any attorney’s fee (if you hired one) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-6/chapter-5/article-16/section-6-5-285/#:~:text=date%20of%20the%20unlawful%20act,trying%20the%20case%20may%20assess)). Keep an eye on that one-year filing deadline – it’s short.

- **Open credit accounts (store credit, revolving accounts):** *Eligible.* This is similar to unpaid invoices, but worth noting: if someone had an **open account** with you (for example, a customer account at your business where they make charges and you bill monthly), you can sue for the unpaid balance. Alabama law treats an open account as a form of contract. Evidence might include a **statement of account** or ledger showing debits/credits and the final balance. If the customer made partial payments and then defaulted, bring records of that. The **limitation period** for open accounts in Alabama is 3 years *if* it’s styled as an open account (Ala. Code § 6-2-37), but many attorneys take the position the general 6-year applies (there’s some complexity in Alabama law about open account vs stated account). To be safe, file within 3 years of the last charge or payment. There’s no special pre-suit notice required by Alabama law for open accounts (unlike some states that require a “notice of intent to sue on an open account” – Alabama doesn’t). So you can proceed directly to small claims. Businesses using small claims for debt collection should recall the **attorney representation rule** for assigned or third-party debt collectors (discussed above) – but if you’re collecting your own accounts, you can self-represent. Also, Alabama’s Deceptive Trade Practices Act (UDAP) specifically does *not* apply to the collection of debts by an original creditor (it exempts most credit transactions), so you wouldn’t typically counter-sue under that if you’re the business. In short, if someone owes on an account, small claims is a proper venue to recover the money.

- **Co-signer or guarantor claims:** *Eligible.* If you co-signed a loan or guaranteed someone’s obligation and ended up having to pay the debt, you can sue the primary debtor for what **you** paid on their behalf (this is called a claim for reimbursement or “indemnity”). Example: you co-signed your friend’s $5,000 car loan, your friend defaulted, and you paid the bank $3,000 to settle – you can sue your friend for that $3,000 you had to shell out. Alabama law recognizes the co-signer’s right to recover from the principal debtor. This is essentially an **equitable contract** enforced by law: the expectation the primary would reimburse the guarantor. No special statute is needed, it’s common law. Just bring proof you paid (receipts, bank statements, letter from lender showing you settled the debt) and the original loan agreement if available to show you were co-liable. It also helps to have any communications where the friend acknowledged you covered it. The small claims limit might restrict you if the amount is high – e.g., if you had to pay $10k as a co-signer, you can only sue for up to $6k here (or go to a higher court). Co-signer cases are straightforward in small claims as long as liability and payment can be shown. Note: ensure the underlying obligation isn’t something like a student loan or other special contract that forbids assignment – but generally, the law allows the guarantor’s claim. No pre-suit notice required beyond maybe asking them to pay you back (which they likely failed to do, hence the suit).

*(Many other contract/money-owed scenarios exist – generally, **if you are out money because someone didn’t do what they agreed**, it can be a small claim. Below we cover consumer-specific contracts and statutory remedies separately.)*

### Consumer disputes

Small claims is user-friendly for consumers seeking refunds or compensation from businesses for poor goods or services. Alabama doesn’t have a separate small claims court for consumer issues – it’s all the same court – but certain laws give consumers extra rights (like the Deceptive Trade Practices Act). Here are common consumer claim types:

- **Defective product or appliance (refund or repair costs):** *Eligible.* If you bought a product that turned out to be defective or not as advertised, and the seller/manufacturer hasn’t made it right, you can sue for the cost of the product and any related damages (as long as within $6k). Example: you purchased a new washer/dryer for $1,200 and it has never worked properly, and the store refuses to refund or replace it – you could sue for a refund (and possibly any laundry costs incurred due to the defect). Another example: you bought a used car “as is” from a small dealer for $5,000, and the engine blew within a week due to a known issue the dealer lied about – you might sue for the repair cost or diminution in value (though used car cases often invoke **fraud** or lemon law issues – see separate bullet). Generally, when a product is defective, you have potential claims for **breach of warranty**. Alabama’s version of the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) implies a **warranty of merchantability** that goods from a merchant are fit for ordinary purposes (Ala. Code § 7-2-314). If the item fails, you can claim breach of that implied warranty. If the seller gave a **written warranty**, you can enforce that too – e.g., a 90-day guarantee and it broke on day 30 and they won’t fix. Under federal law (Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act), you could even get attorney fees for a warranty breach, but in small claims you likely have no attorney, so you just get actual damages. No special pre-suit requirement in Alabama beyond perhaps notifying the seller of the issue and giving them a chance to cure (which is often required by the warranty terms). Some manufacturers require you to go to an authorized service center first – do that if applicable. If they still won’t comply, small claims can award you a refund or the cost of repair. Keep evidence: receipts, warranty docs, photos of the defect, repair estimates. If the product caused property damage (like a defective toaster caught fire and caused $1,000 kitchen damage), you can also sue for those consequential damages – as long as it all stays within $6,000. Alabama’s Deceptive Trade Practices Act (DTPA) might also apply if the defect was coupled with misrepresentations (see UDAP bullet), but any DTPA claims have to be within the $6k cap and filed within **one year** of the cause of action ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=unlawful%20under%20this%20chapter%20and,consumer%20or%20other%20person%20for)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,14)). Often, simply suing for breach of warranty or contract is sufficient in small claims; if you prevail, the judge can also award court costs and possibly a modest punitive amount if there was fraud. *(Important:* If the product is a car or large item, see separate bullets on Lemon Law and auto issues.)

- **Services not performed or performed poorly (refunds):** *Eligible.* If you paid for a service that was never delivered, or was done in a shoddy manner, you can sue to get your money back or to cover the cost of fixing the poor work. Examples: you paid a wedding photographer a $1,000 deposit and they never showed up on the wedding day – you’d sue for that $1,000 (and any additional expenses, like hiring a last-minute photographer, up to $6k). Or you hired a tree service to remove a tree, paid $500 upfront, and they never did the work – small claims. Or a home contractor did the work but very poorly, causing damage – you might sue for the cost to repair their mistakes (or the difference in value). Alabama does not have a special “Home Repair Act” requiring pre-suit notice or anything; you proceed as a breach of contract or negligence claim. One caveat: if the service provider was **unlicensed** (say, an unlicensed electrician caused damage), you still can sue them – the fact they illegally did work doesn’t prevent you from recovering losses (indeed, it helps show negligence per se). Conversely, if you’re suing for return of payment, the unlicensed status can be argued as part of why the contract is void and you deserve your money back. Always gather evidence: contracts or estimates, photos of bad work, perhaps an independent expert statement of what’s wrong (in small claims, a written estimate from another professional to redo the work can serve as evidence of the cost to fix). No need to involve the Alabama Home Builders Licensure Board or others unless you choose to complain to them – it’s not a prerequisite to suing. If the business made specific promises about quality or timing that were broken, that could also fall under Alabama’s DTPA as a deceptive act. The DTPA (Ala. Code § 8-19-5) covers false promises in service contracts; it gives you up to 3× damages and attorney fees in a successful case ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,100%2C%20whichever%20is%20greater%3B%20or)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,reasonable%20attorney%27s%20fees%20and%20costs)). In small claims, again, attorney fees won’t apply for a pro se, but you might mention the DTPA to potentially get up to triple damages (within the $6k cap). For instance, if you paid $2,000 for a service never done, DTPA allows the court to award up to $6,000 (triple) as punishment ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,practices%20were%20committed%20intentionally%3B%20and)). You must prove the act was **unlawful or fraudulent** under that law (e.g., the contractor never intended to do the work and just took your money). Also, a 15-day prior written demand is recommended under DTPA (it’s required to claim attorneys’ fees, but you have none, still it’s good practice) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=unlawful%20under%20this%20chapter%20and,consumer%20or%20other%20person%20for)). Many small claims judges are comfortable just awarding the actual loss plus perhaps some court costs, unless there’s egregious fraud. So manage expectations – you’ll likely get your out-of-pocket loss, which is the goal.

- **Auto repairs and mechanics (overcharge or poor work):** *Eligible.* Disputes with auto repair shops are frequently heard in small claims. If a mechanic **overcharged** you or performed substandard repairs that you had to re-do, you can sue for the difference in value or cost to fix. Example: you paid a transmission shop $1,500 to rebuild your transmission, and it failed again two weeks later because they did it wrong, but they refuse to honor a warranty – you could sue for the $1,500 plus any additional damage caused. Or a body shop charged you for new parts but actually patched the old parts, resulting in shoddy repair – you could sue for the cost to properly fix the car. Alabama doesn’t have a specific “Automotive Repair Act” that gives additional consumer rights (like mandatory written estimates or treble damages for overcharging – some states do, Alabama mainly relies on general fraud/contract law). So your case will hinge on proving either a) the agreed price vs. what they charged (if it’s an overbilling dispute) or b) the quality of work performed was negligent or not as promised. It helps to have **before/after photos**, the **invoice** (check if it guarantees labor or parts for a certain time – many shops give a short warranty; if within that and they refuse to fix, that’s strong evidence of breach), and perhaps the **opinion of another mechanic**. In small claims, you can submit a written statement from another certified mechanic describing the problems with the work – while technically hearsay, many small claims judges will consider it if the other side doesn’t object, or you can bring the other mechanic as a witness (subpoena them if needed, see “Witnesses” in Hearing section). If a repair shop performed unauthorized repairs or exceeded the estimate substantially without consent, that’s another angle – you could argue you shouldn’t have to pay the excess. Alabama’s consumer law (ADTPA) lists as an unlawful act any service provider “**charging for repairs not actually performed**” or “misrepresenting that repairs are needed when they are not” ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=unlawful%20under%20this%20chapter%20and,consumer%20or%20other%20person%20for)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,practices%20were%20committed%20intentionally%3B%20and)). So if you suspect you were charged for work that wasn’t done or unnecessary work, you can sue under ADTPA. Again, ADTPA allows up to triple damages ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,100%2C%20whichever%20is%20greater%3B%20or)), but you must prove the deceit. For example, if they charged you for a new alternator and you later discover they never installed one, that’s clear fraud – you’d sue for that charge, and the judge might add a penalty. If it’s just poor quality, then it’s breach of implied duty to perform workmanlike – you get compensatory damages (cost to fix). Alabama requires auto shops to have a license, but lack thereof doesn’t preclude your suit (though you might report them to Alabama’s Consumer Protection Division if they’re defrauding customers). No special pre-suit notice is required by law – you might simply demand a refund or proper repair and then file suit if refused. Keep detailed records: receipts, any *used parts* returned to you or lack thereof (some laws require shops to offer to return old parts – if they didn’t and you suspect they didn’t change parts, mention that).
