Note: If your dispute is with a **car dealership’s service department**, the same principles apply; just note dealerships often have arbitration clauses in purchase contracts, but those usually don’t cover separate service transactions – so small claims is fine for the service issue. If it’s a warranty repair on a car and they didn’t fix it properly multiple times, that might escalate to lemon law territory (discussed later). But for out-of-warranty paid repairs, small claims is your remedy for poor service.

- **Home repairs & contractors (defective or incomplete work):** *Eligible.* This category overlaps with “services not performed,” but to elaborate for home improvement contexts: Alabama allows you to sue a contractor or handyman in small claims for unsatisfactory work or not finishing the job. Example: you hired a plumber to replumb your bathroom for $5,000, you paid in stages up to $4,000, and they abandoned the job – you can sue to recover whatever amount you paid for work not done or the cost to hire someone to finish within the original budget. Or if the contractor’s work was full of defects (roof leaks after a new roof installation), you can sue for the cost of repairs. Alabama’s **Home Builder Licensure Board** requires certain contractors to be licensed for jobs over $10,000 – but if your claim is under $6k, likely the job was small or the licensure threshold might not apply; either way, licensed or not, you can sue for the money. If the contractor was unlicensed (and should have been), mention it – under Ala. law, an unlicensed contractor may even be barred from counter-suing for payment, and it underscores the wrongdoing. There’s no required mediation or anything before suing (some states have a “Contractors Recovery Fund” or require arbitration – Alabama does not for residential jobs, aside from whatever your contract says). If your contract has an *arbitration clause*, there’s a chance the contractor could move to compel arbitration instead of small claims – but many small contractors don’t have such clauses or won’t bother to enforce them for a small sum. If they do, the small claims judge might remove the case. But absent that, proceed in small claims. Document everything: the contract or proposal, photos of incomplete/defective work, any texts where the contractor admits issues or promised to refund, etc. Also get an estimate from another contractor to *fix or complete* the work – this serves as evidence of your damages. Alabama’s ADTPA could apply if the contractor engaged in deceptive acts (e.g., took money knowing they wouldn’t perform, or lied about being licensed/bonded). ADTPA specifically lists misrepresenting or failing to deliver services as unlawful ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=unlawful%20under%20this%20chapter%20and,consumer%20or%20other%20person%20for)). If you prove that (perhaps pattern of him taking deposits and not doing work), you potentially get up to 3× damages and attorney fees ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,100%2C%20whichever%20is%20greater%3B%20or)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,the%20court%20shall%20award%20to)). Use that as leverage – often just mentioning a **fraud** or **UDAP** angle might encourage a settlement. But in court, realistically, judges typically award actual losses. Note: If the *homeowner* is suing, you can also file a complaint with the Licensure Board, but that’s separate and won’t get you money (it could revoke his license). Small claims is where you get the money judgment.

- **Canceled events or services (deposits not refunded):** *Eligible.* If you put money down for an event or service (like venue rental, catering, DJ, travel package, etc.) and the service was canceled or not provided and you didn’t get your deposit or prepayment back, you can sue for it. Example: you reserved a banquet hall for a family reunion, paying a $800 deposit, and they double-booked and canceled your reservation but haven’t refunded you – yes, sue for the $800. Or you hired a band for a party, paid $300 up front, and they no-showed – sue for the $300 (and any other related loss, like if you had to scramble for last-minute entertainment at extra cost). Such arrangements often have contracts – check the cancellation terms. If *you* canceled the event, sometimes deposits are non-refundable by contract (e.g., “50% deposit non-refundable for cancellation within 30 days of event”). If that’s the case, and the vendor was ready and lost other business, the court might side with the contract and you wouldn’t recover. But if the vendor is the one who failed or canceled, usually you should get a full refund. Alabama doesn’t have special event-cancellation statutes – it’s contract law. If the contract had a **force majeure** that they improperly invoked or something, that could be litigated. But these are usually straightforward – either they breached or they didn’t. Small claims can handle the breach. If the vendor gave some excuse, that’s their defense; the judge will weigh fairness. For instance, if a caterer canceled because their lead chef had an emergency, they might argue impossibility, but that usually doesn’t relieve payment unless truly unforeseeable and contractually allowed. Bring the contract, any receipts, and evidence you requested the refund. If the vendor’s actions were willful or fraudulent (e.g., they took multiple deposits knowing they couldn’t handle all events), ADTPA again could apply (false representation or suppression of a material fact) – that could allow enhanced damages ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,practices%20were%20committed%20intentionally%3B%20and)). Within small claims, likely just aim for your money back. 

- **Property damage by a business (dry cleaner ruined clothes, storage lost items, etc.):** *Eligible.* If you entrusted your property to a business and it was damaged or lost, you can sue for the value (up to $6k). Common examples: a dry cleaner **lost or destroyed** your dress or suit; a **storage facility** had a leak or rodent infestation that ruined your stored furniture; a **kennel** or pet groomer lost or injured your pet (pets are technically property in law – though see personal injury if it’s vet bills); a **hotel valet** crashed your car. These cases involve a “**bailment**” – the business had a duty to take reasonable care of your property. If they failed, they owe you the value of the item or cost of repair. Small claims regularly handles suits like “XYZ Dry Cleaners v. John Doe – seeking $200 for lost shirts.” There may be special statutes for certain situations – for instance, Alabama law requires a **storage facility** to exercise reasonable care and gives them a lien on stored goods for unpaid rent, etc., but doesn’t prevent you from suing for negligence. Always check if you signed a contract with a liability limitation: e.g., many dry cleaning tickets limit liability to 10x the cost of service or such. Alabama generally enforces reasonable liability limitations if you agreed, but if the business was grossly negligent, those limits might not protect them. Nonetheless, be aware the business might point to fine print “Not responsible for items left over 30 days” or “max liability $50 for loss.” Judges in small claims often attempt fairness over technicalities – if the loss was clearly their fault (e.g., they admit they lost the item or have no explanation), judges may award you the fair value of the item even if a small print says otherwise (especially if you weren’t aware of it). Bring evidence of the item’s **value**: original receipts if available, or photos and testimony of its quality/brand, or even Amazon/eBay printouts of similar item value. If the item had **depreciated**, expect the judge to award current value (e.g., your 5-year-old TV’s used value, not original price). If an item had **sentimental value**, unfortunately you cannot recover extra for that – it’s limited to market value (that heirloom quilt your grandma made might be priceless to you, but a court will only award maybe material value or cost of a similar blanket). If the business did something intentional (like an employee stole your item), you could also file a police report for theft, but civilly you just seek the value. Alabama’s ADTPA covers certain “services” and “repair or alteration of property” under consumer transactions – arguably losing/damaging a consumer’s property could be an unfair practice, but it’s a stretch. You likely don’t need ADTPA here; straightforward negligence claim suffices. The statute of limitations for damage to personal property is 2 years (a tort claim). So file within 2 years of when you knew of the loss/damage. If the business offered some compensation (say they offered $100 store credit for your $500 item and you refused), you can still sue for the full $500 and let the court decide the proper amount. The court costs can be added to your claim if you win.

- **Fraud, scams, and deceptive sales:** *Eligible (if under $6k).* If you fell victim to a scam or deceptive sales practice that cost you money, you can use small claims to get your money back. Example: a door-to-door alarm system salesman lied about the terms and got you to pay $500 you now want refunded; or a travel agent sold you a bogus vacation package for $1,200. These fall under **fraudulent misrepresentation** or the ADTPA (Alabama Deceptive Trade Practices Act). ADTPA is a powerful statute for consumers: it prohibits a long list of unfair or deceptive acts (false advertising, bait-and-switch, etc.) and allows a private suit for **actual damages or $100 (whichever greater), plus up to 3× actual damages as punitive, plus attorney fees** ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,100%2C%20whichever%20is%20greater%3B%20or)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,the%20court%20shall%20award%20to)). However, ADTPA specifically excludes certain entities (like banks, utilities, insurance – those have separate regulators). And it requires the suit to be filed **within 1 year** of the offense ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,14)) (or within 1 year of discovery if it couldn’t have been discovered earlier). Also, one must send a **15-day demand letter** before suing for ADTPA damages in most cases (to give the business a chance to cure) – it’s not explicitly in the statute text, but case law encourages it, and it’s required to recover attorney fees. Even if not mandatory, sending a written demand and waiting a couple weeks is wise; if they offer a fair refund, you’ve saved time. In small claims, if you prove a willful deception, the judge can indeed award up to triple your losses ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,practices%20were%20committed%20intentionally%3B%20and)), but note the overall $6k cap. For instance, if you lost $2,000 in a scam, triple would be $6,000 – which is at the limit, so it could be possible. If you lost $3,000, triple is $9,000 which small claims can’t give – you’d either sue for $6k (double) under ADTPA or go to circuit. Many ADTPA small claims end up with just actual damages awarded unless the conduct was egregious. Demonstrating fraud might require showing intentional misrepresentation. Use any evidence: ads, brochures with false claims, witness statements, etc. ADTPA also provides for **court costs and reasonable attorney fees** to a winning consumer ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,reasonable%20attorney%27s%20fees%20and%20costs)) – since you likely have no attorney, fees aren’t an issue, but costs you’ll get. As an example case: a consumer buys a “lemon” used car after a dealer assured “clean title, never wrecked” when it was in a major crash – that’s fraudulent inducement plus an unlawful act under ADTPA (false representation of vehicle condition). The consumer could rescind the sale and sue for a refund or for the diminution in value. That overlaps with auto issues, but it shows how ADTPA might be invoked. Small claims is an acceptable venue for ADTPA claims as long as they seek ≤ $6,000 ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-11-30/#:~:text=%281%29%20CIVIL,exclusive%20of%20interest%20and%20costs)) ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-11-30/#:~:text=,exclusive%20of%20interest%20and%20costs)) (the law mentions filing in circuit court, but because $6k is exclusive small claims territory, district small claims court can hear a properly valued ADTPA case). Just remember the 1-year deadline from the date of the deceptive act (or its discovery) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,14)). 

*(In general, Alabama’s small claims courts can address most consumer purchase or service disputes: if you paid money and didn’t get what was promised, or you were harmed by unfair practices, you have a case. The main exceptions are if your claim needs a non-monetary fix – small claims only gives money, it won’t force a business to do something, though the threat of money judgment often motivates compliance.)*

### Property damage

If someone damages your personal property (or a business’s property), you can sue for the cost of repair or replacement, as long as it’s within the small claims limit. The defendant could be an individual or a business. Common scenarios:

- **Vehicle accidents (minor car crashes):** *Eligible.* If another driver hit your car and caused damage within the $6k range, you can sue that driver for the cost of repairs, rental car, towing, etc. Example: a fender-bender resulted in $3,500 of body damage and the at-fault driver’s insurance is refusing to pay or is offering too little – you can sue the driver in small claims for the full amount of damages. Ensure you have evidence of fault (police report, witness statement) and proof of costs (repair estimates or invoices). Note that Alabama is a **“contributory negligence”** state – if you were even 1% at fault, you recover nothing. So if the other driver can prove you contributed to the accident, you lose the claim. This is a harsh rule (most states use comparative negligence). Be prepared to counter any claim that you were partially to blame. Also, Alabama requires drivers to have liability insurance; you can still sue an insured driver, though usually their insurance will defend them (and might settle with you before court). If the driver was uninsured, you might have a claim on your own uninsured motorist policy – but that’s separate (and often requires arbitration). Small claims is useful if insurance (theirs or yours) isn’t fully covering your loss – e.g., suing for your deductible if the other side won’t pay it. Also, note that **injuries** from car accidents are often more than $6k; you cannot split an injury and property claim – they’re part of one accident. Usually, car accident injury cases go to higher court or are settled. However, if your personal injuries were minor and your medical bills are, say, $2,000 and you want to handle it yourself, you could sue for those (see “Personal injury” section). But keep in mind the contributory negligence rule and that the value of pain and suffering can quickly exceed $6k, so small claims is mainly suitable for **property damage only** or very small injury cases. Alabama’s statute of limitations for property damage from a car accident is 2 years (same as injury) – so file within 2 years of the wreck. If the wreck involved a **government vehicle**, special notice rules apply (see “Cannot sue for” in Section 15 – you have to file a claim notice with the city or county within 6 months) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/8577/11-47-23.html#:~:text=Section%2011,View%20Our%20Newest%20Version%20Here)). Assuming it’s another private party, no such requirement. Summon the defendant to a court in the county where the accident happened or where they live (venue rule). Small claims judges regularly decide liability in accidents based on police reports, diagrams, and testimony. There is no jury – the judge will be the fact-finder. Bring any photos of the scene or damage. If the other driver has since moved out of state, you can still sue them in Alabama (for an accident that occurred in Alabama), but you’ll need to serve them out of state (certified mail or process server). The judgment would be enforceable if they have assets here or you domesticate it in their state. Often, suing prompts their insurance to step in and pay to avoid the hassle.

- **Damage to personal property (non-vehicle):** *Eligible.* If someone **damaged or destroyed your personal belongings**, either through negligence or intentional acts, you can sue for the monetary value. Examples: a neighbor’s kid threw a baseball through your $300 window and the parents refuse to pay; a friend borrowing your laptop dropped it and it broke; a passerby’s dog ran into your bicycle and bent the wheel; or someone at a party knocked over your antique vase. These are simple tort claims for property damage. Alabama allows recovery of the **cost of repair** or, if it’s not repairable, the **fair market value** of the item. You generally cannot recover sentimental value or *new* replacement cost for an old item (the measure is typically value at time of loss). If the act was **intentional or reckless**, you also can ask for punitive damages (though as discussed, small claims punitive awards are rare and limited by the cap). Alabama Code § 6-5-380 makes parents liable for up to $1,000 for the willful or malicious property damage caused by their minor children under 18 – so if a minor intentionally vandalized your property, you can alternatively sue the parents (limit $1k) under that statute. In small claims for say $500 damages, you might sue both the minor (through their guardian) and parent to cover all angles. Provide evidence of the damage (photos, repair estimates, receipts for original cost if relevant). If the defendant had **homeowner’s or renter’s insurance**, sometimes they’ll just pay you – but you’re free to sue them; they may turn it over to their insurer to handle. The limitation period for conversion or destruction of personal property is 6 years for conversion and 2 years for damage (negligence) – but to be safe, treat it as 2 years for property damage. No special demand needed, though it’s courteous to request payment first. If an incident also caused you personal injury (e.g., the baseball broke your window and cut you, causing $200 in medical bills), you *could* combine that in your suit (property + injury), but again if the total > $6k or the issues get complex, consider separating or dropping the minor injury to focus on property. Most property damage cases in small claims are straightforward on liability – usually the person admits it happened but balks at the amount, or claims it wasn’t entirely their fault. The judge will decide and likely award the proven repair or value amount. If **multiple people** were responsible (e.g., two friends roughhousing broke your TV), you can sue both jointly in one case (the court can hold them jointly liable for the full amount, then they sort out contribution).

- **Damage by tenants to rental property:** *Eligible.* If you’re a landlord and a former tenant caused damage beyond normal wear-and-tear, you can sue for the cost of repairs (often, this is covered by taking their security deposit – but if damage exceeds the deposit or you didn’t collect one, you can sue for the rest). For example, a tenant moved out and left $2,000 in damages (holes in walls, broken windows) and the deposit was $1,000 – you can file a claim for the remaining $1,000. Alabama’s landlord-tenant law (URLTA) says a landlord must itemize damages and refund the deposit balance within 60 days of tenancy termination ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=,tenancy%20and%20delivery%20of%20possession)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=35,tenancy%20and%20delivery%20of%20possession)). If the landlord fails to do that, the tenant can actually sue for **double the deposit** as a penalty ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=both%2C%20is%20sufficient%20compliance%20with,this%20chapter)) (discussed under deposits). So as a landlord plaintiff, ensure you complied with the 60-day notice/refund rule, or you risk the tenant having a counterclaim for double deposit. Assuming you did, you can proceed to claim any actual damages. Bring photos from before (if you have move-in condition pics) and after, copies of the lease showing tenant’s responsibilities, and receipts or estimates for the repairs. Note: if damage was caused by **normal wear** (e.g., carpet simply aged), you cannot charge that to tenant – small claims judges know the difference. But if it’s clearly negligence or abuse (giant holes, missing appliances, etc.), it’s tenant’s liability. Suing in small claims also might trigger the tenant to counter-sue you for things like wrongful deposit withholding or unperformed repairs, so be prepared for any such counterclaims (see Landlord-Tenant section and Deposits). If the amount is above $6k, you’d use District/Circuit Court (and possibly get a lawyer involved). But typical apartment damages suits are within small claims range. Statute of limitations is 6 years for breach of lease or 2 years for injury to property – since this arises from contract (lease), arguably 6 years, but best not to wait too long (memories fade).

- **Damage to a tenant’s personal property by landlord or others:** *Eligible.* If you’re a tenant and your landlord’s action (or inaction) caused damage to *your* belongings, you can sue for that loss. Example: the landlord negligently failed to fix a known leaky roof, and your furniture valued $2,500 got ruined by rain; or the landlord’s exterminator sprayed something that destroyed your $500 houseplants; or the landlord wrongfully seized or threw out your possessions after an eviction. These claims can be against the landlord (for negligence or wrongful conversion of property). Alabama’s URLTA requires landlords to keep premises habitable ([tenant-rights.com](https://tenant-rights.com/alabama/applying-rent-deduction-for-repairs-in-alabama-renter-s-guide#:~:text=Understanding%20Alabama%E2%80%99s%20Law%20on%20Rental,Repairs)), so not fixing a leak that leads to property damage can make them liable for those damages (and possibly a rent abatement). If the landlord *willfully* disposed of your belongings without following proper procedure, the tenant can recover the value and possibly show conversion. Alabama law doesn’t have a specific penalty for unlawful disposal of tenant’s property, but if done without the required 14-day holding period (landlords must hold onto abandoned property for 14 days or mail notice before disposing, per Ala. Code § 35-9A-423 & 425), you have a strong case for the value. In small claims, the judge won’t order the landlord to return items (maybe they’re gone), but will award money equal to value. Provide evidence of the condition and value of items (photos, receipts, testimony). If landlord’s negligence is clear (like many requests to fix a leak on record), that helps. The landlord might defend by saying you didn’t mitigate damages or it wasn’t their fault (e.g., “it was an extreme storm, not my negligence”). The judge will decide fairness. If *another tenant* or third party caused the damage (like neighbor’s fire spread to your unit), you could technically sue that person as well in small claims. But often it goes through insurance. Note: the **security deposit** cannot be used by landlord to cover other tenants’ property – deposit covers damage to premises, not your personal goods. So you still have your separate claim. Landlords often have insurance that may cover tenant’s personal property loss (though tenant’s own renter’s insurance is primary for their goods), but if neither pays, small claims is an option. Statute of limitations for property damage is 2 years – so don’t delay.

- **Neighbor disputes (trees, fences, animals):** *Eligible.* Disputes with neighbors over property damage are common. If your neighbor’s **tree limb fell** and damaged your shed, or their **dog tore up your garden**, or they **built a fence on your property** damaging your yard – these can be brought in small claims for the monetary loss. Alabama law on fallen trees generally says if the tree was healthy and fell due to an act of God (storm), the neighbor isn’t liable (each landowner handles their own cleanup) – but if the tree was **dead or hazardous** and the owner knew and did nothing, then that owner can be liable for resulting damage (negligence). So if you had warned your neighbor about their dead overhanging tree and it fell, you have a case for damages to your property. Or if they were actively cutting a tree and dropped it on your fence, obviously they’re liable. Boundary line issues sometimes involve **trespass** – e.g., neighbor’s contractor drove equipment over your lawn and cracked your driveway, you’d sue for repair costs. Alabama allows *double damages* for **willful trespass cutting of timber/trees** on someone’s land (Ala. Code § 9-13-62 gives double or triple stumpage value in some cases of timber theft). In small claims, you could invoke that if a neighbor maliciously cut down your trees. But more typically, say they accidentally cut a boundary tree – you’d claim its value (small ornamental tree value can be shown by nursery estimate). For **fence damage or boundary structures**: if a jointly owned fence, tricky – but if they clearly caused damage to your property, sue for repairs. If it’s more about exactly where the line is, that’s a title issue (not small claims), but any physical damage as a result (like they tore down *your* fence thinking it was theirs) can be claimed as money. If neighbor’s **animals** cause damage: under Alabama law, a pet owner is liable for damage their pet causes (strict for some animals like cows/horses breaking fences under stock laws, or common law negligence for dogs). If their dog chewed up your $200 hose or killed your chickens, absolutely you can claim value. Alabama has a dog bite statute for injuries to *persons* on owner’s property ([www.animallaw.info](https://www.animallaw.info/statute/al-dog-bitedangerous-animal-liability-owners-dogs-biting-or-injuring-persons#:~:text=Injuring%20Persons.%20,property%20owned%20or%20controlled%20by)), but for property damage by animals it’s general negligence. Show evidence (photos of damage, maybe an admission or eyewitness). Usually, neighbor disputes are resolved by small claims if modest; if it’s large (like major structural damage or serious encroachment), sometimes it goes to higher court. Also, often the mere act of filing might spur the neighbor or their **homeowners insurance** to pay you (yes, if your neighbor’s tree crushes your fence, sometimes *your* insurance covers and subrogates, but you can also directly pursue neighbor if negligence). Contributory negligence typically isn’t an issue in these – unless maybe you also did something to cause the damage. Statute of limitations is 2 years for property damage. 

- **Pet injury or death (to your pet):** *Eligible.* If someone’s negligence or intentional act injured or killed your pet, you can sue for the **vet bills** and/or the **market value of the pet**. Alabama law treats pets as property, so emotional value is not compensable. But you recover the economic loss. Example: a neighbor intentionally poisoned your dog – you can sue for the vet bills or the dog’s value if it died. Or another dog attacked yours resulting in $800 vet costs – you can sue the other dog’s owner for those expenses. Alabama’s dog bite statute covers bites to *people* ([www.animallaw.info](https://www.animallaw.info/statute/al-dog-bitedangerous-animal-liability-owners-dogs-biting-or-injuring-persons#:~:text=Injuring%20Persons.%20,property%20owned%20or%20controlled%20by)), but for dog-on-dog or dog-on-cat attacks, it’s a property damage issue. Proving it might require witness testimony – if it happened on your property, note that Alabama’s dog bite law (strict liability if bite on victim’s property) doesn’t directly apply dog-on-dog, but it shows owner negligence if their dog was roaming. *Be aware:* Small claims judges (many of whom are dog/pet lovers) will sympathize but are bound to award only measurable costs: vet bills, medicine, etc. They won’t pay for your emotional distress of losing a beloved pet. The value of a pet is typically its market or replacement value (adoption fee or purchase price), or stud value if breeding, etc. Sometimes punitive damages can be sought if someone **maliciously** harmed your pet (as an intentional tort). Alabama law would require clear evidence of malice for punitive – e.g., a neighbor confessed to poisoning out of spite. In small claims, you could ask for some punitive but remember the cap. For instance, if your actual vet bills were $1,000 and you ask $5,000 total for the malicious act, the judge could possibly give something more than $1k if they find it was willful and want to punish (within $6k). But absent explicit malice, you’ll get actual costs. If an animal was involved in **killing livestock or pets**, Alabama allows certain actions (farmers can kill dogs harassing livestock, etc.), but that’s not small claims issue – small claims only does money. So if your sheep worth $500 were killed by neighbor’s dog, you sue for $500. Provide any evidence like photos of injuries, vet reports connecting cause (like “these injuries consistent with dog bites,” or witness saw the attack). Alabama’s contributory negligence doesn’t really apply to pets (your dog can’t be negligent). So it’s straightforward: either strict liability if it falls under stock-killing laws, or negligence – either way, owners are usually held liable if their animal trespassed and caused harm. 

### Landlord–tenant (financial only)

*Important:* Small claims can resolve **money disputes** between landlords and tenants, but it **cannot evict** a tenant or otherwise award possession of real property. Also, it won’t issue injunctive relief (like ordering a landlord to make a repair). The cases here are about dollars.

- **Unreturned security deposits:** *Eligible and very common.* If you’re a **tenant** who moved out and your landlord unjustifiably kept your security deposit, you can sue to get it back. Likewise, if you’re a **landlord** who had to use the deposit for damages and they were more than the deposit, you could sue the tenant for the excess (as covered above in property damage). Alabama’s security deposit law is tenant-friendly in cases of wrongful withholding: A landlord must either return the deposit or send an itemized list of deductions within **60 days** after termination of tenancy and delivery of possession ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=,tenancy%20and%20delivery%20of%20possession)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=35,tenancy%20and%20delivery%20of%20possession)). If the landlord fails to do **either** (i.e., no refund and no itemized statement by 60 days), the tenant is entitled to **double the original deposit amount** as a penalty ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=both%2C%20is%20sufficient%20compliance%20with,this%20chapter)). This is stated in Ala. Code § 35-9A-201(f). Small claims courts regularly enforce this double-damages rule. For example, if your deposit was $800 and your landlord just ignored you after you moved, on day 61 you have a claim for $1,600. Even if the landlord *eventually* returns the $800 after you sue, the law says they “shall pay double” if they missed that 60-day window ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=,sufficient%20compliance%20with%20this%20chapter)) – so you’d argue you still deserve $800 more as statutory penalty. However, note: if they did send an itemized list, even if you think it’s bogus, the double penalty doesn’t automatically apply – then it becomes a question of whether those deductions were lawful. You can still sue for the deposit (or part of it) that you believe was wrongfully withheld, but you’d get *actual* damages, not double. The double only kicks in for **complete failure** to provide accounting or refund in 60 days ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=,sufficient%20compliance%20with%20this%20chapter)). For tenants: common claims are “landlord kept my deposit for normal wear and tear” or “landlord falsely claimed excessive cleaning costs,” etc. The tenant can sue for the portion withheld that they deem unfair. Judges will examine the lease, any move-out condition report, and the reasonableness of deductions. Alabama law allows deposit deductions for unpaid rent and for damages beyond ordinary wear (and possibly unpaid utilities if lease says). It specifically says a deposit cannot exceed one month’s rent (except for pet deposit or modifications) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=,obligations%20under%20a%20rental%20agreement)). If the landlord took an illegal deposit (too high) or a non-refundable deposit disguised as deposit, mention that (though remedy in that case is return of any excess). Tenants should always *provide a forwarding address* – Alabama law requires the tenant to give a valid address in writing; if not, the landlord is to mail the accounting/refund to the last known address (which could be the rental) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=,a%20period%20of%2090%20days)). If the tenant gave no address and mail is returned, the deposit escheats after 90 days, and landlord is safe from double penalty ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=the%20landlord%20shall%20mail%2C%20by,a%20period%20of%2090%20days)). So if you’re a tenant plaintiff, be ready to show you did supply a forwarding address and still got nothing by 60 days. Bring a copy of your notice letter/email if possible. For landlords defending deposit suits: if you did mail an itemized letter/refund, bring a copy and proof of mailing (the statute doesn’t explicitly require certified mail, but having proof is good). If you missed the deadline, expect the double penalty. Landlords sometimes argue “tenant left no forwarding address, so I mailed to rental” – if you did that within 60 days and it wasn’t returned, that might suffice as compliance ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=,a%20period%20of%2090%20days)). Judges often strictly enforce the law in favor of tenants if timelines were ignored. The statute of limitations for a tenant to sue for deposit would likely be 6 years (written or unwritten contract), but effectively you’d know by 60 days and should sue within a year or two. Court costs can be added if tenant wins. One more note: if the landlord’s withholding was **in bad faith** (e.g., trumped up damages), that’s essentially what the double deposit is meant to punish. No additional punitive beyond that is typical. The double covers the punishment. So a tenant’s claim will be either “return my $X deposit” or “you missed deadline – pay me $2X.” Alabama small claims judges are well aware of this law and will apply it if facts support it.

- **Last month’s rent or prepaid rent refund:** *Eligible.* Sometimes tenants pay **last month’s rent in advance** or other pre-paid rent. If the tenancy ends early or properly and that last month wasn’t actually used, the tenant may be entitled to a refund. Example: you paid first and last months’ rent when you moved in, you gave 30-day notice and moved out after 10 months due to a job relocation with landlord’s agreement – the landlord should refund the “last month” rent you had on deposit since you didn’t stay the full last month. If they don’t, you can sue for it. Unlike a security deposit, this is simply rent you paid that wasn’t applied. There’s no double penalty on rent – the double is specific to deposits ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=both%2C%20is%20sufficient%20compliance%20with,this%20chapter)). But you can get the unused portion back. Another scenario: you *overpaid* rent by mistake or landlord miscalculated pro-rata move-out rent and owes you a refund – that can be claimed. Always request it first in writing. Landlords sometimes treat “last month’s rent prepayment” like a deposit and apply it to damages or rent owed – but legally it was rent, not a damage deposit (unless lease allowed it). If they converted it to cover damages without your consent, you might argue that was improper (damage should come from security deposit). However, some leases explicitly allow using last month’s rent for any charges – read your lease. The small claims judge will honestly just seek to avoid double recovery: e.g., if you already had a security deposit that was returned, and also last month’s rent that landlord kept for damages, the judge might look at total paid vs actual damages. As a tenant, frame it as: last month rent was to be applied to last month of occupancy, which didn’t occur, so it should be returned. As a landlord, you’d defend that it was part of lease consideration and maybe tenant left early not per lease (if that’s the case). If the tenant broke the lease early, sometimes landlords try to keep last month as liquidated damages – but unless lease says it’s non-refundable in that case, the landlord should mitigate losses and return any unused. Small claims can weigh fairness. Usually, if tenant followed lease terms on termination, they get unused rent back. The amount is often straightforward (one month’s rent or part thereof). Document the payment (canceled check or lease showing prepay) and the move-out date. The general 6-year contract limit applies. Prepaid **pet fees** or **parking fees** that were supposed to be refunded can similarly be claimed if not returned (though many pet fees are explicitly non-refundable – the lease wording matters: “pet deposit” vs “pet fee”). A non-refundable fee cannot be reclaimed unless you argue failure of consideration or fraud. But if it was labeled a deposit and conditions met for refund, then it’s like security deposit.

- **Tenant’s claims for repair costs (“repair and deduct” situations):** *Eligible, with caution.* Alabama law (URLTA) technically does **not** give tenants a broad right to “repair and deduct” from rent (unlike some states). It does allow a tenant to notify the landlord of a needed repair affecting health/safety, and if not fixed within 14 days, the tenant can terminate the lease ([generisonline.com](https://generisonline.com/repair-and-deduct-and-rent-withholding-in-alabama-a-comprehensive-guide/#:~:text=Prerequisites%20for%20Repair,Withholding)) or possibly make minor repairs in some cases of essential services ([generisonline.com](https://generisonline.com/repair-and-deduct-and-rent-withholding-in-alabama-a-comprehensive-guide/#:~:text=obligations%20regarding%20property%20maintenance%20and,adverse%20effects%20of%20landlord%20neglect)). But there’s no explicit statutory allowance to deduct repair costs from rent. That said, if a situation forced you as a tenant to pay for a repair that was clearly the landlord’s responsibility (e.g., you paid an electrician $200 to restore power after landlord failed to fix unsafe wiring promptly, and it was an emergency), you can try to **recover that expense** in small claims. You’d argue the landlord breached the duty to maintain essential services (implied warranty of habitability) and you incurred reasonable costs as a result – therefore landlord should reimburse. Alabama courts might not have a clear precedent, but in equity and under contract principles, you could get compensated for *necessary* repairs that were the landlord’s obligation when they failed to act. Or you might frame it as **rent abatement**: e.g., no heat for a month, you had to buy space heaters for $100 and spent $300 on a hotel during a cold snap – you could sue for those costs or withhold and then if landlord sues for rent, counterclaim. Because Alabama doesn’t have a specific statute, these claims are a bit unconventional but not forbidden. Small claims is a way to have a judge decide fairness. Example: your landlord repeatedly failed to fix an AC unit in August, you spent $500 on window AC units – ask for reimbursement. Or your landlord refused to address a rodent infestation, so you paid $150 for an exterminator – claim that. Provide evidence you gave the landlord **notice and an opportunity** to fix (e.g., texts, letters requesting the repair) because a judge will want to see you didn’t just do it unannounced. If the issue was urgent (burst pipe at 2am, you couldn’t reach landlord, so you paid a plumber $300 to stop the leak), that’s very reasonable to claim. The landlord might counter-argue that you had no authority to incur those costs – but a judge likely finds it was their responsibility originally. The lease may have a clause “tenant shall not make repairs and landlord not liable for cost if they do” – if such clause exists, it complicates things, but a judge could override for serious issues. Alternatively, tenants sometimes **withhold rent** because of uninhabitability; if the landlord sues for rent, the tenant can counterclaim that the value of the premise was diminished (the “repair and deduct” in effect, by withholding). That would be resolved by giving the tenant an abatement of rent for that period or excusing withheld rent. But that’s a defensive use in an eviction case (which is in district civil, not small claims). In small claims, you as tenant proactively suing the landlord for money spent is cleaner than withholding. Be aware: no explicit 2x or 3x damages here – just actual costs. Alabama’s ADTPA could theoretically apply if landlord engaged in a deceptive practice (e.g., renting a place they knew had no heat and lying about it) – but many landlord-tenant disputes are exempt from ADTPA because the Act doesn’t apply to transactions regulated elsewhere (and leases are somewhat regulated by URLTA). So likely, you won’t use ADTPA except in egregious landlord fraud situations. Stick to breach of contract/warranty arguments. Summing up: while Alabama doesn’t codify “repair-and-deduct,” small claims has been used by tenants to recover expenditures that should have been the landlord’s – it boils down to persuading the judge it was necessary and fair.

- **Past landlord suing tenant for unpaid rent or damages:** *Eligible.* We touched on this under contract and property damage: a landlord can sue a former tenant in small claims for **unpaid rent** (e.g., tenant skipped last month) or **excess damages** beyond deposit. Those are routine small claims (just ensure any required notices for terminating lease or itemizing damages were done, to avoid tenant defenses). We mention it again here to stress that eviction for possession is separate – if you only need money, small claims is fine. 

- **Wrongful eviction or lockout (tenant’s claim):** *Eligible for money damages only.* If a landlord illegally evicted you – e.g., changed locks without court order, shut off utilities to force you out, removed your belongings – you can sue for whatever losses that caused (and possibly statutory damages under specific laws). Alabama’s URLTA makes “self-help” evictions (lock-outs, utility shut-offs) unlawful and allows the tenant to recover actual damages plus reasonable attorney’s fees ([shelby.alacourt.gov](https://shelby.alacourt.gov/small-claims/small-claims-faq/#:~:text=%2A%20Garnishment%20of%20Wages%20,10%20Writ%20of)) ([shelby.alacourt.gov](https://shelby.alacourt.gov/small-claims/small-claims-faq/#:~:text=It%20is%20important%20to%20mention%2C,cannot%20give%20you%20legal%20advice)). In small claims, again, no attorney for you, but you can get your actual damages. What damages could those be? Perhaps cost of temporary housing (like you had to stay in a motel for a week at $500), value of any property the landlord withheld or threw out (say $1,000 worth of furniture), maybe lost wages if the lockout caused you to miss work (less common). Also, the law would allow you to recover any prepaid rent for the period you were unlawfully kept out (basically a refund of rent for days locked out). And court costs. While the statute (§ 35-9A-426) doesn’t specify a penalty multiple, the act of willful unlawful eviction might persuade a judge to award something for inconvenience (though they may stick strictly to pecuniary losses). Document everything: police report of lockout, photos of padlocked door, receipts for alternate housing, list of possessions taken, etc. The limitation for this would be 2 years if seen as a trespass to property or some intentional tort. It’s good to act quickly for credibility. Keep in mind, if the landlord did **file a legal eviction** and got a court order, then locked you out pursuant to that, you don’t have a claim (that’s lawful). This is only for without-court evictions. If they had court eviction but mis-handled your property storage, that part you can claim (as covered in personal property damage above). 

*(In sum, small claims is a forum where both landlords and tenants can assert **financial** claims against each other: landlords for rent/damages, tenants for deposits, wrongful acts, etc. It won’t resolve who gets the apartment or order repairs, but it will swap money for any harm done.)*

### Employment & wages

If you’re an employee (or former employee) owed money by an employer, small claims can be a quick way to recover relatively small wage amounts or expenses. Alabama doesn’t have a robust state labor agency for wage claims (the Alabama Department of Labor refers most wage issues to the U.S. Department of Labor) ([adol.alabama.gov](https://adol.alabama.gov/faq/i-have-questions-about-pay-unpaid-wages-breaks-vacation-pay-fmla-etc/#:~:text=I%20have%20questions%20about%20pay%2C,7450%20or%20www.dol.gov%2Fagencies%2Fwhd)), so often your recourse is either federal DOL or suing directly. For amounts under $6,000, many workers choose small claims court because it’s faster and doesn’t require a lawyer.

- **Unpaid wages or final paycheck:** *Eligible.* If your employer did not pay you for work you’ve done – whether it’s your last paycheck, overtime hours, or any regular wages – you can sue the employer (or former employer) in small claims for the amount owed. Example: your job laid you off but never gave you your final 2 weeks’ pay of $1,200; or your employer consistently didn’t pay you for all hours (maybe forcing off-the-clock work) and you can quantify, say, $500 of unpaid time. Alabama has **no state wage payment law** requiring immediate final paycheck (unlike some states). It defaults to federal law (Fair Labor Standards Act) and general contract law. Under federal law, all wages earned must be paid – final paycheck usually by next regular pay cycle. Alabama doesn’t impose penalties for late payment like some states (“waiting time” penalties in CA, etc., don’t exist here). So, you’ll typically sue for the exact wages owed and possibly any interest. Under the federal **FLSA**, if it’s minimum wage or overtime that was not paid, you are actually entitled to “liquidated damages” of an equal amount (double) unless the employer shows a good faith reason. You could therefore ask the court for **2× the unpaid wages** under 29 U.S.C. § 216(b) (FLSA). Small claims courts can and do enforce federal law – they have concurrent jurisdiction for FLSA claims. Keep in mind, doubling under FLSA plus original wages counts as part of the “amount in controversy” in state court. If that total > $6k, you’d have to limit it. For example, if you’re owed $4,000 in overtime, double is $8,000 which is above small claims – you might waive liquidated damages to stay in small claims or just sue for the $4k actual (or sue for $6k out of $8k). It’s your choice. If you don’t explicitly seek liquidated damages, judge may just award actual. Also, FLSA provides attorney fees – but in small claims you likely have none, so skip that. Common wage suits in small claims: *last paycheck not delivered* – Alabama law says you can go straight to court or contact U.S. DOL. Many employees file small claims because the mere lawsuit might push the employer to pay rather than fight in front of a judge. *Unpaid overtime or minimum wage:* If you regularly worked over 40 hours and weren’t paid time-and-a-half by a non-exempt employer, you can claim the overtime pay difference. Or if you were paid below minimum wage (currently $7.25), you can claim the shortfall. Those are FLSA claims – mention that law in your complaint to justify entitlement. The statute of limitations for FLSA is 2 years (3 if willful). Alabama’s general for breach of oral employment contract is 6 years, but wages fall under FLSA mostly. Best practice: file within 2 years of when wages were due. Provide evidence: time sheets, pay stubs, personal logs of hours, co-worker statements, etc., and of course proof of what was paid vs. what should’ve been. *Commissions or bonuses:* If you earned a commission or bonus under a plan and it wasn’t paid, that’s also basically wages. It might not be covered by FLSA (which covers min wage/overtime), but it’s enforceable as a contract. E.g., you sold a deal entitling you to a $500 commission per a written policy, but after you left the company they refused to pay – you can sue for $500. Alabama has no specific commission payment statute, so it’s contract law (some states have 30-day commission laws; Alabama doesn’t). Just prove the promise (policy document, emails) and the performance (you made the sale). Similarly for a **promised bonus** – if it was guaranteed upon hitting targets and you did, it’s enforceable. If it was discretionary (“maybe a holiday bonus”), you usually can’t force it unless a contract was formed. *Unreimbursed business expenses:* Alabama doesn’t have a labor code requiring reimbursement, but if your employer agreed (explicitly or implicitly by policy) to repay expenses and didn’t, you can sue for those costs. Example: you traveled for work, paid $300 travel costs expecting reimbursement per company policy, but they never repaid – sue for $300. Provide receipts and the policy/email saying they’d reimburse. Note: If your *employment was governed by a written contract or union collective agreement*, check if any dispute must go to arbitration or a grievance process first. Absent that, small claims is fine. Also, if the employer is a **government entity**, you might have to file a claim with the Board of Adjustments (state agencies) or a claim with the city (for city/county) within a time frame (like 6 months for city) – see Section 15. But if it’s private sector, no such requirement – you just sue the company or proprietor. Also consider: if many employees are unpaid, sometimes a class action or DOL complaint is done – but you still have the right to your individual claim. If the employer has filed bankruptcy, that’s a complication (your claim would go to bankruptcy court, not small claims due to the automatic stay).

- **Wrongful deductions or paycheck errors:** *Eligible.* If an employer illegally deducted money from your paycheck (and it’s not returned), you can seek that money. For instance, they deducted the cost of a broken tool from your pay without your agreement, which made your pay fall below minimum wage – that’s unlawful under FLSA. Or they charged you for cash register shortages or uniforms improperly. Alabama law (Ala. Code § 6-10-7) protects a portion of wages from garnishment (75% is exempt) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-6/chapter-10/article-1/division-1/section-6-10-7/#:~:text=writs%20of%20garnishment%20or%20other,The)), but that’s about creditors. On employer deductions: Federal law says if a deduction drops you under minimum wage (except for specific things like taxes), it’s not allowed. So if that happened, you can claim that amount as unpaid wages. If not a minimum wage issue, but a contract issue (say they promised to cover training costs then deducted them when you quit contrary to agreement), that’s contract – you can claim it back. Alabama doesn’t have a statute forbidding specific deductions, but many such cases are handled as FLSA (if it affects overtime or min wage) or just contract. Show the before/after of your paystub or a written company policy. If it’s a dispute like “they said I owed them for a damaged company phone, so they docked $200,” small claims can decide if that was legitimately your debt or not. Many times, employers don’t have a right to dock pay for damage unless employee agreed in writing. Without that, you likely get it back. The amount is usually small, so small claims is apt. 

- **Misclassification (employee vs. contractor) and related claims:** This is complex, but if you were treated as an independent contractor and not paid overtime or benefits you think you’re entitled to as an employee, small claims can’t reclassify you (that’s a legal determination often for bigger courts or labor department). But you can still sue for the money as if you were an employee – though you’d have to first establish you really were one under law. That could be complicated to prove in a brief hearing. Possibly not ideal for small claims if it hinges on nuanced criteria. However, simpler cases occur: e.g., the employer labeled you “contractor” and didn’t pay overtime – you can claim overtime; if the judge finds you met the definition of employee (control, schedule, etc.), they’ll award it. Or if you were a salaried employee misclassified as exempt but you were non-exempt, you claim overtime – again, you have to educate the judge on why the law says you should get overtime (maybe bring a printout of FLSA regulation for your role). It’s doable but technical. The Alabama small claims judge may or may not want to dive into FLSA classification rules. If the amount is big, these often go to federal court or through DOL. But for a moderate sum, you can try. Bear in mind, if your case requires detailed FLSA interpretation, the employer might involve attorneys or remove it to a higher court (they have the right to *remove to federal court* an FLSA claim, potentially, but rarely would for such a small amount). In short, yes, you can attempt to recover wages by arguing you were misclassified as exempt (so you should get overtime) or as contractor (so you should have had wages or tax withholdings or expense reimbursements). But success may vary. It might be easier to stick to straightforward wage counts (hours times rate). If you prevail, you get wages plus possibly liquidated damages as said. If the employer acted in bad faith, the judge may be sympathetic. There’s no extra state penalty beyond FLSA.

- **Better forum – Dept. of Labor vs. court:** Alabama, as noted, doesn’t have a state-run wage claim process. The U.S. Department of Labor’s Wage and Hour Division (WHD) will take complaints for minimum wage/overtime or certain unpaid final pay issues for *interstate commerce* employers. That route is free and can pressure employers, but it can take a while and they might choose not to pursue small claims. They also cannot order **penalties** beyond double wages. For pure breaches like commissions, DOL won’t handle that – that’s outside their scope. So small claims is often the practical route. If your claim also involves something like discrimination or a retaliatory firing issue, those typically go to EEOC or a different legal channel – small claims only addresses the money part, not reinstatement or discrimination damages. So keep the case narrowly about the unpaid sums. Also note, if the amount is large or there are multiple workers, sometimes an attorney could do a collective action. But for one person and ≤ $6k, small claims is effective. Employers often don’t want a judgment against them (public record), so they might settle quickly once served. If you do get a judgment and the employer still doesn’t pay, you can garnish their bank account or seize assets (see Post-judgment section). 

*(Bottom line: small claims is a valuable tool for workers owed relatively small amounts – it levels the field since formal discovery isn’t required. As an employee, gather as much documentation of hours and pay as you can before/when you leave the job, because after suing you might not have access to records except what you kept.)*

### Personal services / professional disputes

This covers various service providers outside of construction, auto, etc., many of which operate on informal agreements. If you paid someone for a personal service and got poor results or no results, you can sue in small claims for the money back or damages up to $6k.

- **Hairdressers, barbers, salons (botched services):** *Eligible.* If a hair salon or barber seriously messed up your hair (wrong treatment that caused damage, etc.) and you had to pay to fix it, you can sue for the cost of the service and any additional treatment costs. Example: a chemical relaxer burned your scalp and you needed medical treatment and corrective products costing $500 – you could sue to get those costs and a refund of what you paid. Admittedly, it’s hard to put a dollar on a bad haircut itself (courts won’t pay for your embarrassment), but tangible costs (like having to go to another salon to correct color) are recoverable. If an expensive wig or extension was ruined, you claim its value. These cases are essentially negligence by the service provider. Bring photos (before/after) and receipts for what you had to spend to correct the problem. Many salons will offer a free fix or refund if truly botched – always request that first. If they refuse or made it worse, then consider court. Alabama does license cosmetologists, but there’s no special consumer fund for hair mistakes. So small claims is the venue. If an injury occurred (like scalp scarring), that crosses into personal injury – if minor, you could claim under $6k (medical bills, etc.), but if major, you’d need higher court. So for small claims, stick to the economic loss. The statute of limitations for negligence like this is 2 years from incident (or from discovery if not immediately obvious). For lesser dissatisfaction (didn’t like the style), you don’t have a legal claim – it must be objectively substandard or contrary to what was requested. If they gave you green hair instead of blonde as promised, that’s a breach (and arguably falls under ADTPA as a service not provided as agreed). You could use ADTPA if, say, a salon guaranteed a treatment would do X and instead it fried your hair – that might be a misrepresentation of a product’s quality. ADTPA could allow up to 3× if willful ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,100%2C%20whichever%20is%20greater%3B%20or)), but realistically, judges likely give you your money back and costs to fix. 

- **Caterers, DJs, photographers (no-show or poor performance):** *Eligible.* If you hired someone for an event (wedding, party) and they failed to perform or did an awful job, you can seek a refund or damages in small claims. Example: you paid a photographer $800 for an event and they lost all the photos or took unusable shots – you might sue for the $800 plus maybe any amount you paid for re-staging some photos, etc. Or a DJ showed up extremely late and ruined the vibe – if you paid them, you could ask for a refund due to breach. These are usually contract claims. Often a written contract exists – check if it has a **limitation of liability** (“If we mess up, max liability = amount paid”). Even if it does, you can claim at least what you paid. If you had incidental losses (event started late, etc.), those are harder to quantify and often not awarded unless you incurred extra costs (like renting a backup speaker system last-minute for $200 – that you could claim). If the provider simply didn’t show at all, it’s a straightforward breach – you should get your deposit or prepayment back. Additionally, if that left you scrambling and you had to hire a replacement at higher cost, you can claim the difference as consequential damages. Provide evidence of the no-show or failure (emails, witness statements from attendees, the contract, receipts from replacement vendor). If there’s a **force majeure** clause (say the DJ had a medical emergency), the contract might excuse them – but absent that, they breach by not delivering promised service. ADTPA might cover “failure to provide services paid for” as a deceptive act ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=unlawful%20under%20this%20chapter%20and,consumer%20or%20other%20person%20for)); in egregious cases (like a pattern of taking deposits and not showing up), you could mention ADTPA and potentially get attorney fees or multiples, but in one-off event breaches, judges usually stick to actual loss in small claims. Because these often involve emotional events (weddings), it’s worth noting you can’t recover for emotional distress or lost memories – only costs. E.g., you can’t put a dollar on missed wedding photos beyond maybe a refund and cost of trying to recoup some images. So temper expectations – small claims can compensate the money you wasted, not the intangible loss. 

- **Tutors, coaches, trainers (sessions paid but not delivered):** *Eligible.* If you paid in advance for lessons or sessions (academic tutor, music teacher, personal trainer, sports coach) and the person didn’t provide the sessions or cut them short without refund, you can sue for the unused portion of what you paid. Example: you bought a package of 10 piano lessons for $500, but after 3 lessons the teacher quit and never refunded the $350 for the remaining 7 – you can claim that $350. Or a personal trainer who was paid monthly $200 but then stopped showing up after 2 weeks – claim $100 back. Provide proof of payment and how many sessions were missed. If the quality was poor rather than not delivered (say you feel the tutor didn’t teach effectively), that’s harder – small claims isn’t going to judge educational outcomes, only whether the service was delivered. So focus on no-shows, cancellations, or clear failure to perform. Many of these arrangements might not be in writing – but your testimony and maybe text/email scheduling can show the agreement. Alabama has no special regulation on tutors or such – it’s plain contract. If the person had a legitimate reason (illness) but kept your money, legally they still have to refund for services not rendered. If they refuse, small claims. There’s no need to complain to any authority (there is none specific), just handle it in court. 

- **Pet services (groomers, boarding, trainers):** *Eligible.* If you paid for pet-related services and something went wrong financially, you can use small claims. Example: you paid a dog trainer $1,000 for a multi-week program and they only did 1 week then disappeared – sue for the unused $800. Or a pet boarding facility promised individual play sessions for an extra fee but clearly didn’t provide them (and your pet was neglected), you could at least get the extra fee back. Or a groomer seriously injured your pet (like cut its ear requiring vet care) – you could sue for the vet bill and maybe a refund of the grooming cost (overlap with property/pet injury damages described above). Many of these overlap with earlier categories: if a pet is injured, that’s property damage; here we focus on the service not being done as promised. Provide the contract or brochure that shows what was promised vs. what occurred. If a kennel lost your pet or it died due to negligence, that’s a much bigger matter – you could still claim the “value” of the pet (which often won’t exceed $6k for most animals). For small claims, keep it to monetary things: e.g., “I paid for 5 training sessions, got 2.” Or “I paid for deluxe boarding with playtime, but from evidence (maybe video or condition of pet) they did not do what I paid for.” Pet service contracts sometimes have disclaimers (“not responsible for injury or death of pet due to…”) which might limit liability for certain things, but not for failing to provide paid add-ons. Alabama does not have a specific statute on, say, kennel responsibilities beyond general animal welfare laws. So this is basic contract. If your pet was harmed due to their negligence, see the property damage/pet injury discussion – you’d sue for vet costs, etc., which is usually within $6k. You might combine a refund demand with vet costs in one case if it’s the same incident (e.g., groomer injures dog: ask for vet bill + refund of grooming fee). That’s fine as long as total ≤ $6k. These suits often encourage the business to settle to avoid bad publicity. Always try asking first – many grooming salons will pay the vet bill if it was clearly their fault. If not, small claims is your leverage. 

*(Essentially, any scenario where you paid for a professional or personal service and didn’t get what you paid for, or it caused you additional monetary loss, can be addressed in small claims. Alabama doesn’t have specialized consumer courts or panels for these – it all funnels into the general small claims docket.)*

### Loans, debts, and financial disputes (between individuals or informal groups)

Not all money disputes involve businesses – many are between friends, family, roommates, or acquaintances. Alabama small claims is perfectly suited for these interpersonal debt issues, which might otherwise go unresolved. We covered formal loans in Contract disputes; here are more informal or unique scenarios:

- **Loans between friends/family:** *Eligible.* (Covered above under unpaid personal loans.) If you lent money to a friend, relative, or significant other expecting repayment, and they haven’t paid, you can absolutely sue for it in small claims (with the caution that it may affect the relationship permanently). The court will treat it like any other contract debt. You don’t need a written note, though it helps. Provide evidence of the transfer (Venmo record, check copy, bank withdrawal) and any messages where they acknowledge it was a loan. Often in such cases the defendant will claim “it was a gift” – be prepared to show circumstances implying a loan (e.g., they asked to borrow, or you have texts about payback). The judge will weigh credibility. Alabama doesn’t enforce “usury” on personal loans unless you charged interest above 8% annually ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-8/chapter-8/section-8-8-1/#:~:text=Alabama%20Code%20%C2%A7%208,or%20for%20a%20longer%20or)), which usually isn’t the case among friends (usually it’s interest-free or very low interest). If you did agree on interest, you can claim the agreed rate through judgment. If none mentioned, you could claim the 6% legal interest from date of loan due ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-12/chapter-19/article-3/division-2/section-12-19-71/#:~:text=%281%29%20Thirty,in%20determining%20the%20jurisdictional%20amount)), but in small claims you might just focus on principal to keep it simple. The limitation is 6 years since it’s an unwritten contract (or written if you had some IOU letter). As lender, consider that suing a relative could cause drama – but legally you have the right. The court won’t consider “they’re family” as a defense; a loan is a loan. They might raise equitable defenses like “he forgave the loan” or “I did work equal to that amount” – those will be factual issues the judge decides. Absent evidence of forgiveness, a loan stands. It’s wise to send one last demand (“I really need this back by X or I will consider legal action”) – not legally required, but it documents that you tried and it might spur payment to avoid court.

- **Roommate expense splitting:** *Eligible.* If you had an agreement with a roommate or ex-roommate to share certain expenses (rent, utilities, groceries, etc.) and they didn’t pay their share, you can sue for the amount they owe you. E.g., both names on the lease but you paid the full rent for several months after they moved out – you can sue them for their half of those rent payments. Or you fronted a $500 utility deposit on the understanding you’d split, and they never paid you their $250 – claim that. Provide any proof of the agreement (text: “we’ll split electric 50/50,” or witnesses who knew the arrangement). Even without writing, the court can infer an agreement to share essential living costs if both benefited. If one roommate simply flaked on obligations, it’s unjust enrichment for them. Alabama recognizes claims like “implied contract” or “quantum meruit” for such situations. The statute is 6 years for an unwritten contract (some argue 2 for open account – but likely 6). Common roommate cases: one roommate moved out early and didn’t pay last month’s rent or share of bills, leaving the other to cover – that other person sues for reimbursement. The defendant might argue they gave proper notice or the plaintiff agreed to take on the amount – so be prepared to explain why the debt is clear-cut (e.g., lease was joint so they owed rent until replacement found, etc.). If however you voluntarily covered their part as a gift or to avoid eviction, and expected nothing, then it’s not recoverable. But usually that’s not the case. Judges will apply fairness: if two people signed a lease, they’re each liable for rent – if one paid it all, they can seek contribution from the other. Same for jointly used utilities. If any formal agreement was made when they left (“I’ll Venmo you each month” – and they didn’t), show that. These personal arrangements might not be documented, so often it’s he-said/she-said. The judge will evaluate credibility. Where possible, a simple witness or printout can tip the scale (maybe a third roommate can testify you all agreed to split costs, etc.). These disputes are very suitable for small claims – no need for lawyers, it’s just dividing bills. Another example: ex-romantic partners who shared expenses and one didn’t reimburse as promised – that too. (Be wary if it’s in the gray area of relationship “I paid for everything when we dated” – gifts vs. loans must be distinguished. The court leans that money spent during a relationship is a gift unless clearly articulated as a loan or shared debt. So if you pursue an ex for half of vacations or dinners, you’ll likely lose. But if you two explicitly bought a car together and one kept it and you kept paying the loan, that’s more clear – see next bullet on jointly owned property.)

- **Division of costs after break-up (joint purchases or bills):** *Eligible.* Unmarried couples or friends who jointly purchased property and then split up might use small claims to sort out reimbursement. For instance, you and your then-partner jointly bought a $4,000 TV (each contributing $2k) but it’s in their possession now and they refuse to compensate you – you can’t force return of the TV in small claims (that would be a replevin case – though note Alabama small claims **does allow** a claim “for specific personal property” with Form SM-02 ([russell.alacourt.gov](https://russell.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=To%20Download%20Small%20Claims%20forms%3A%C2%A0Click,Specific%20Property)), which is essentially replevin if you want the item itself; see Counterclaims section for that form). But you *can* sue for the $2,000 you effectively lost if they keep the TV. The court will effectively enforce an equitable division: either they pay you your share or, if they show some contrary agreement (like you gave it as a gift), then not. The challenge is proving it wasn’t a gift. If both names were on receipt or you have any proof of your contribution, that helps. Another scenario: an ex took an item that belonged to you entirely – that’s conversion/theft, and small claims can award its value if under $6k. Or if one person kept an engagement ring after breaking off the engagement – legally, in Alabama, an engagement ring is considered a **conditional gift** that should be returned if the marriage doesn’t occur (assuming the giver wasn’t at fault) ([www.justanswer.com](https://www.justanswer.com/law/62s4b-law-alabama-regarding-returning-engagement.html#:~:text=What%20is%20the%20law%20in,of%20an%20engagement%20ring%3F%20I)). So a fiancé who gave a ring can sue for its return or value if the engagement ended (most likely in District Court if value > $6k, but if modest ring under $6k, small claims works). Actually, Alabama case law tends to follow the rule: ring goes back to donor if donor wasn’t the one who broke the engagement (if donor broke it, they don’t get it back – a conditional gift with fault consideration). If you’re in that scenario and ring value is <= $6k, you can sue for the ring or its appraised value. The small claims court can technically order return of specific personal property (the form SM-02 covers “Complaint for Specific Property”) ([russell.alacourt.gov](https://russell.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=To%20Download%20Small%20Claims%20forms%3A%C2%A0Click,Specific%20Property)). That means the judgment could say “Plaintiff is entitled to the ring or $X value.” Service must be proper to retrieve the ring. This is a bit beyond normal money claims but allowed. If you go that route, treat it like a claim for property (replevin). If you just want money equivalently, sue for its value – but know emotional value is not counted. Provide proof of the ring’s cost or appraisal. The limitation for such gift return is unclear – presumably 6 years as it’s property/contract matter. For other jointly owned things: maybe you and a friend co-owned a used car, you paid off the loan and friend didn’t contribute as agreed – sue them for their share of payments. Or a group of friends rented a beach house and one didn’t pay their promised share – yes, small claims it. Provided there was a clear mutual understanding of splitting costs, you can enforce that. The default law if multiple parties agree to share an expense is that each is liable for their portion. Just compile the math (who paid what, who owes what). Judges often draw up a quick tally chart during hearing to confirm numbers. 

- **Informal group expenses (trip costs, etc.):** *Eligible.* Similar to roommates, if you fronted costs for a **group vacation** or event and people promised to pay you back their portion, but someone didn’t, you can pursue that person for their share. Example: you paid for a $1,200 Airbnb for a group of 6 college friends, each promised $200 but two never paid – you can sue each of those two for $200 (or sue them together, but usually you’d do separate cases or one case with two defendants – either is allowed since it’s same transaction). Show the communication where they agreed (text: “Yes, count me in, I’ll pay you later”). Or if not written, the judge may infer from context and the fact the others paid that everyone was supposed to. People might claim “I didn’t go on the trip so I shouldn’t have to pay” – if they cancelled after you booked based on their commitment, that’s on them unless you agreed to excuse. Or “the trip was unsatisfactory” – not relevant unless you organized it as a business (then they might counterclaim like customers, but if it’s just friends, that’s not a defense to not paying their share of non-refundable costs). Another example: group tickets to a concert – you bought 5 tickets at $100 each for friends, one friend never reimbursed – small claims for $100. Straightforward. Or bachelor party costs, etc. All the same principles: an agreement (explicit or implied by everyone else paying) to split, and one reneged. This is often resolved with peer pressure, but if not, legally you have a solid case. 

- **Wedding expenses/gifts disputes:** *Eligible.* Occasionally, ex-fiancés or ex-spouses (not married long enough for divorce court to handle property) may squabble over who pays for wedding costs or who keeps the gifts/ring after a canceled wedding. We mentioned engagement rings (conditional gift typically returned if marriage off, in Alabama) ([www.troymessenger.com](https://www.troymessenger.com/2015/08/05/small-claims-court-increases-claim-amount-limitations/#:~:text=Pike%20County%20Circuit%20Clerk%20Jamie,still%20paying%20smaller%20court%20costs)). What about wedding deposits paid? If both contributed and wedding got canceled, splitting those lost deposits fairly could be subject of a claim if one person bore more of the loss and the cancellation was the other’s fault. E.g., bride’s family paid $5k venue deposit, groom jilted, venue nonrefundable – the bride’s family could potentially sue the ex-groom for that loss, arguing he caused the loss by breaching the promise to marry (that’s essentially a “breach of promise to marry” action – historically allowed or not allowed depending on state; Alabama has abolished **heart-balm** actions like alienation of affection, but **breach of promise to marry** might still be actionable in some form. Actually, I should double-check – many states abolished breach of promise, but I’m not sure about Alabama. Probably safer to avoid that claim). It may be beyond small claims scope because it’s tied to personal relationship obligations, and lots of states don’t allow suing for breaking an engagement except ring returns. So maybe skip that scenario unless it’s just straightforward “we each agreed to split costs if wedding called off.” Honestly, small claims probably won’t entertain emotional blame for canceled wedding; they’ll stick to ring and tangible gifts. Wedding gifts given and then wedding canceled: legally, gifts to the couple for a marriage that didn’t happen should be returned to givers typically. But that’s between you and the gift-givers. If one of you didn’t return, a gift-giver could sue for it (rare). Not your fight. Items gifted between the couple pre-marriage are treated as conditional (like ring). If it’s unclear (like he gifted her a car during engagement and they break up – hard to claim that back unless clearly conditioned on marriage). Possibly out of scope here. 

*(These interpersonal financial disputes can be messy emotionally, but the court will try to cut to who promised what and what’s fair. Alabama’s small claims judges often encourage parties to settle these before trial if possible – but if not, they’ll make a decision as best as the evidence allows.)*

### Vehicle-specific disputes
