Beyond accidents (already covered under property damage and personal injury) and mechanic issues (covered under consumer disputes), there are a few other car-related matters that crop up in small claims:

- **Towing and impound fees disputes:** *Eligible.* If your car was **improperly towed or impounded** and you had to pay to get it back, you might sue the towing company or property owner to recover those fees. Alabama doesn’t have a super-strong consumer protection for predatory towing, but if, for example, a towing company violated a local ordinance (like no signage in a private lot but towed you anyway) or damaged your car during tow, you can sue for the fees and/or damage. Provide evidence the tow was wrongful (photos showing lack of “No Parking” sign, receipts, maybe witness who saw the lot half empty meaning it wasn’t a hazard, etc.). Alabama law requires certain notice and procedures for law enforcement impounds ([www.justanswer.com](https://www.justanswer.com/business-law/tirtw-towing-company-impounded-vehicle-lawsuit.html#:~:text=Alabama%20Towing%20Lawsuits%3A%20Impounded%20Vehicle,registered%20owner%20and%20lienholders%20must)), but for private property tows, it’s largely unregulated except by local city ordinances. You’d likely claim either **conversion** (“they unlawfully took my car”) or **breach of peace** or simply demand restitution of the money you paid under protest to get your car back. Some judges might be hesitant if you technically were parked illegally – check if the technical requirements for the tow were met. If the sign was not properly posted or the tow truck didn’t notify police as required, you have a stronger case. Also, if the tow company charged more than allowed (some cities cap fees, e.g., Birmingham’s code might have limits), show that. Another angle: if your car was **wrongly repossessed** by a lender (like they took the wrong vehicle or took it despite not being in default), you can sue for statutory damages under UCC Article 9 and conversion. That might exceed small claims, though – but if it’s just cost of getting it back, maybe. But typically wrongful repo cases go to bigger courts. For small claims, likely scenario: you had to pay $300 to the towing yard for something you feel was unjust – you sue for that $300 and possibly any damage to car from tow. If the tow was authorized (you did park in violation), you might not win. But if tow company didn’t follow law (like didn’t notify owner within required time, causing higher storage fees), you could argue for refund of excess fees. *Impingement of vehicles by city for tickets or by police if you think it was unlawful might require going to the city’s appeals process (some cities have hearing officers for impounds). But you could also pay then sue for a refund, forcing them to justify it in court. That could be tricky due to sovereign immunity (some impound fees are set by ordinance, and you may have to appeal administratively within short time rather than sue). Check local procedure. Often, if the city had an appeals process and you didn’t use it, a small claims judge may not overrule. But if a private tow, or if you did appeal and got nowhere, small claims is next. 
   - Also note: Alabama law specifically says if a vehicle is wrongfully *damaged* or converted by a towing service, you can sue like any property damage. The tow company might have a limitation sign (“not responsible for damage”), but if they were negligent hooking your car, they pay. Show repair estimates for any dents or alignment issues from the tow. That can be included along with fee reimbursement or separate, as long as total ≤ $6k.
- **Car dealer disputes (post-sale issues):** *Eligible (for monetary part).* We covered defective used cars under consumer disputes. To add: if a used car sale involved **odometer fraud** or **title issues**, you can sue the dealer under federal Odometer Act or state fraud. Federal odometer law (49 U.S.C. § 32710) provides either $10,000 or 3× actual damages, whichever is greater, for odometer tampering. $10k is beyond small claims, so you’d just sue for 3× actual if that’s ≤ $6k. For example, you paid $4,000 for a car that with true mileage was worth $2,000, your actual damage $2k, triple is $6k – fits. Provide evidence (CarFax, etc.). Often just threatening this gets a dealer to settle, as odometer fraud is also criminal. Title issues (like they sold you a car without clear title) – you could sue for rescission (return car for money back) in small claims if under $6k or for the diminishment in value if you keep it. Or if they owed you a **tag refund** or warranty refund and never paid, those small sums can be claimed. 
   - Also, if a dealer failed to pay off your trade-in loan as promised and you incurred fees, you can sue for those fees (though the main remedy is often regulatory complaint or larger breach). If the amount is small (like $500 extra interest because they delayed payoff), small claims can handle that. 
   - Another example: **extended warranty refund** – you canceled an extended warranty and dealer/manufacturer owes you prorated refund of $800 and hasn’t sent it – small claim for that is fine. 
   - **Financing scams** (like dealer misrepresenting interest rate or adding unauthorized charges) – those might fall under ADTPA (treble possible). But if the outcome is you paid $1,000 extra, you can sue for that $1,000 (maybe $3k by ADTPA if willful) – as long as within $6k. Provide the contract and evidence of deception.
   - **“Yo-yo” sales (spot delivery taken back)**: If a dealer let you take the car, then financing fell through and they demanded the car back but maybe kept a fee or sold your trade etc., that’s complicated. Many states treat that as allowed if contract had a rescission clause. But if they didn’t return your down payment or trade equity promptly, you can sue for that money. E.g., down payment $2,000 and they didn’t refund after deal canceled – definitely claim it. Or if they sold your trade-in and then deal fell, they owe you either the trade car back or its value – small claims can award the value (like $5k trade value). Actually, if they sold it, you might get more formal because conversion of trade is serious – but if amount is small, small claims might still address it. Provide evidence of trade value (KBB etc.). [This is rare scenario but it happens.]  
   - **Note:** Many dealer sales contracts have an **arbitration clause**. If you sue a dealer in small claims and they have one, they might file a motion to compel arbitration. Under Alabama law and FAA, even small claims cases can be forced to arbitration if a valid clause exists (though some arbitration clauses carve out small claims – check yours). If they compel, the small claims case will be stayed/dismissed. If the dealer doesn’t respond or raise it, you might win a default or judgment. It’s a gamble – sometimes for small amounts they don’t bother arbitrating. So you can file and see. They might either ignore (then you win) or move to arbitrate (then you’d have to decide to pursue arbitration or drop it). 
- **Car repair after insurance denial:** *Eligible.* If you were in an accident and your insurance or the other person’s insurance refused certain repairs or only partially paid, you might go after the other driver (as mentioned) or if it’s your own insurer not paying a claim, that is more of a contract with your insurer – that typically exceeds small claims and involves bad-faith insurance law, which small claims can’t handle beyond the policy amount. Usually, you can’t directly sue the other *insurance company* in small claims (no direct cause of action as third party in AL), you sue the at-fault person (their insurer will defend/pay). If it’s a dispute with your own insurer under $6k (like they short-paid $3k on a claim), technically you could sue them for breach of contract in small claims for that $3k. But insurance companies often move it to a higher court or compel appraisal processes. Many policies have appraisal or arbitration for disputes. Still, small claims is a venue for straightforward disputes like “insurer says scratch was pre-existing and won’t cover $500 repaint; I contend it was from covered incident.” Judges do handle those on occasion. But be mindful of contract terms. If it’s minor and you feel the insurer is clearly wrong, small claims pressure might make them settle. If they have a broad arbitration clause, they might invoke it. 
   - Note: If you sue your own *insurance company*, they will certainly send an attorney – they won’t default. Usually the legal muscle they bring might make it challenging for a pro se. But if it’s a very clear issue and documentation supports you, don’t be deterred – you might win or they may settle to avoid costs. 
   - If it’s about a **title dispute** (like you never got a proper title from a sale), small claims can’t issue a title, but can compensate you (like cost of bonded title, etc.). In summary, small claims is primarily for retrieving money or compensation – anything requiring forcing someone to give or do something (like issue a clean title) is outside its immediate power except as incidental to a money judgment (though, again, Alabama small claims can order specific property return in some cases).

*(Vehicle issues are often entangled with insurance and regulatory rules, but when it boils down to someone owes you money due to a vehicle transaction or incident and it’s not huge, small claims is a good platform to get that money.)*

### Personal injury (small / minor)

Major personal injury cases (e.g., serious car accidents, medical malpractice) usually far exceed $6,000 and require complex evidence – those go to higher courts. However, **minor injuries** can be resolved in small claims if the damages (medical bills, lost wages, etc.) fit under the limit. Keep in mind the following:

- **Small claims has no jurisdiction to award more than $6k**, even if your actual injury damages are higher. So if you file there, you’re effectively capping your recovery. For some minor injuries, that’s fine (like a trip to ER and a few follow-ups might be under $6k). For anything involving long-term treatment or pain, $6k might be too low. Plaintiffs sometimes use small claims for injuries when they want a quick result without lawyer fees, even if they surrender some value – that’s a personal choice. Just know $6k is the max you’ll get.

- **Examples of cases appropriate:** a dog bite that required a tetanus shot and a few doctor visits costing $1,200, plus maybe a scar and you’re content to ask for $5,000 (some for pain) – small claims could handle that (though technically pain and suffering aren’t easily quantified, you can include some amount, hoping the judge awards it within reason). Or a slip-and-fall bruised knee with $500 urgent care bill – you might sue for $500 +, say, $1,000 for pain (some judges might award some nominal pain amount, others stick strictly to economic loss because pain is subjective and they may feel uncomfortable awarding it without expert evidence – it varies by judge). Or a minor fender-bender where you had $2,500 chiropractic bills and no lasting injury – you could sue the at-fault driver for, say, $3,000 (bills + small additional). Keep your expectations realistic in small claims; many judges will compensate **out-of-pocket medical costs and lost wages** readily if liability is clear but are conservative about assigning a dollar figure to pain without more formal evidence. That said, nothing prohibits you from asking for it – just be prepared to substantiate as much as possible (photos of injury, medical records indicating you were in pain, how it impacted you). With no jury, it’s the judge’s discretion.

- **Contributory negligence (again):** Alabama is one of four pure contributory negligence states. If the defendant can prove you were even slightly negligent in causing your own injury, you get $0. This applies to personal injury claims. For example, if you slipped in a store, and the store argues you weren’t watching where you walked or you ignored a warning cone, the judge might find you partially at fault and thus bar recovery. This is a very harsh doctrine – basically, if you have any fault, you lose, no comparative weighing. So as a plaintiff in small claims for personal injury, anticipate that argument and have a response (like “there was no sign at all” or “I couldn’t have known”). If it’s clearly the other’s fault (they ran a red light and hit you), contributory negligence won’t be an issue unless they cook up something like “you were speeding” – but if no evidence of that, you’re okay.

- **No pain & suffering unless you prove actual injury:** You can’t claim arbitrary pain and suffering without showing some actual injury occurred. If you had **no medical treatment** at all, just discomfort, many judges will not award anything beyond maybe token $100 for nuisance – often they will say “if you were hurt, you’d have gotten medical attention.” So if you want to claim pain, at least have seen a doctor or have some documentation. For **emotional distress** on its own (like someone’s negligence endangered you but you weren’t physically hurt), Alabama typically requires actual physical injury for a negligence claim to recover emotional distress. Only some exceptions (like if you were in the “zone of danger” of a loved one’s injury – not typical small claims scenario). So focus on physical injury claims with concrete effects.

- **Statute of limitations:** For personal injury, it’s **2 years** from the injury (Ala. Code § 6-2-38). For example, if you got hurt on May 1, 2024, you must file by May 1, 2026. If you discovered an injury later (like an illness from toxic exposure), the discovery rule might extend it, but small claims judges may not entertain nuanced discovery arguments – best to file within 2 years of the incident.

- **Defenses like “Assumption of risk” or “Open and obvious hazard”:** Defendants might argue these to avoid liability (especially in slip-and-fall or recreational injury cases). Alabama honors *assumption of risk* (if you knowingly undertook a dangerous activity) – that completely bars recovery too. E.g., you got hurt in a voluntary boxing match – you assumed risk of being hit. Or *open and obvious* – if the hazard was obvious and you failed to avoid it, in Alabama that often just folds into contributory negligence or no duty by defendant. So be prepared to counter those with why you didn’t see hazard or had no meaningful choice. 

- **Attorneys in small claims PI cases:** Typically, you wouldn’t have one (by rule they’re generally not allowed, or at least not common, in the hearing for small claims plaintiffs). The defendant (often an insurance company’s insured) might have their *insurance adjuster* or lawyer show up – Alabama small claims doesn’t ban lawyers (some states do), so they could. Often for tiny claims, the insurer just sends the adjuster or authorizes the defendant to negotiate with you at court. If their lawyer does come, don’t be intimidated; just stick to facts. In any event, you can manage a simple case – small claims judges are used to unrepresented plaintiffs in such matters and will usually help ensure you present your side fully.

- **Collection of judgment in PI cases:** If you win against an individual (like the other driver), their insurer usually will pay the judgment up to policy limits. If it’s clearly within their policy, they often just handled it to avoid the insured being personally hit with a judgment. If they don’t, you can enforce it (garnish wages, etc. – see post-judgment). If you sued a business for an injury (like store owner for slip-and-fall), similarly, their liability insurer would typically step in. On the other hand, if you sue an **uninsured individual**, collection could be slow if they have limited assets (but at least you get a judgment which you can use to garnish wages/bank etc., or it accrues interest at 7.5% ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-8/chapter-8/section-8-8-10/#:~:text=,from%20the%20day%20of%20entry))).

*(In summary, small claims can resolve **minor personal injury cases** where damages are modest and liability is clear. It’s a cost-effective way when hiring a lawyer would cost as much as the claim. Just be aware of Alabama’s tough contributory negligence rule. If your case is slightly above $6k in value, you might decide to waive the excess to use small claims – many do for expediency. If it’s well above, small claims is not appropriate – you risk leaving money on the table or the judge simply dismissing if it’s clearly out of jurisdiction.)*

### Specific statutory claims (state-specific)

This section highlights Alabama-specific statutes that create causes of action or special damages which you *can* pursue in small claims (if the amount falls under $6k). Some were already touched upon:

- **Worthless check damages:** As discussed, Ala. Code § 6-5-285 allows the payee of a bad check to recover **punitive damages and attorney fee** in addition to the check amount ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-6/chapter-5/article-16/section-6-5-285/#:~:text=though%20there%20has%20been%20no,trying%20the%20case%20may%20assess)). In small claims, you’d ask for, say, double the check or some extra, citing this statute. Judges have discretion. This is one of few Alabama statutes explicitly providing extra damages in small claims context. Just remember the **1-year time limit** from the date of the check ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-6/chapter-5/article-16/section-6-5-285/#:~:text=have%20a%20right%20of%20action,trying%20the%20case%20may%20assess)). (And also possible criminal charges, but that’s separate – you can pursue civil regardless of criminal outcome ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-6/chapter-5/article-16/section-6-5-285/#:~:text=have%20a%20right%20of%20action,trying%20the%20case%20may%20assess)).)

- **Deceptive Trade Practices Act (UDAP) claims:** Alabama’s DTPA (Ala. Code § 8-19-1 et seq.) gives a private right of action to consumers for a wide range of unfair or deceptive acts (false advertising, bait-and-switch, fake guarantees, etc.). We’ve integrated ADTPA considerations in various categories above. Key points: It allows recovery of **actual damages or $100 (whichever greater)**, and the judge *may* award up to **3× actual damages** if the unlawful act was willful or gross, plus **court costs and a reasonable attorney’s fee** for the consumer ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,100%2C%20whichever%20is%20greater%3B%20or)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,reasonable%20attorney%27s%20fees%20and%20costs)). Also, if the defendant acted in bad faith by trying to sue *you* frivolously under DTPA (like a groundless counterclaim), they could owe your fees – but usually it’s the consumer suing under DTPA. In small claims, you must keep the claim at $6k or less (small claims jurisdiction), so if actual is $2k and you want treble, that would be $6k – okay. If actual $3k, treble $9k – you’d have to waive asking beyond $6k. ADTPA also requires you to file within **1 year** ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,14)) (one year from the time the deception occurred or was discovered with reasonable diligence). And as noted, you should send the business a **written demand letter 15 days before suing** (the Act says a prevailing consumer can’t get attorneys fees if they didn’t send a demand 15 days before ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,reasonable%20attorney%27s%20fees%20and%20costs)); even though you have no attorney, sending one shows good faith and sometimes results in a quick resolution). In small claims, the judge might not be extremely familiar with ADTPA since these cases often go to circuit with attorneys, but you can bring a copy of § 8-19-10 and highlight the remedy section ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,100%2C%20whichever%20is%20greater%3B%20or)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,practices%20were%20committed%20intentionally%3B%20and)). ADTPA is *not* applicable to all industries: it exempts e.g. insurance companies, utilities, transactions over $100k with a consumer’s business (so big commercial deals), etc. But for standard consumer purchases and leases, it’s applicable. It also forbids class actions (only the AG can bring those) – but you’re an individual, so that’s fine. If you win an ADTPA claim in small claims, realistically the judge might give you something like 2× damages (if it was egregious) as a compromise that stays under cap. Eg., actual $1,000 lost, they might award $2,000 plus costs. They likely won’t articulate it as ADTPA treble specifically; they’ll just say judgment for $X. But that $X could reflect a punitive element allowable by ADTPA. They also must mail a copy of the complaint to the Attorney General’s office when you sue under this Act ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,shall%20mail%20a%20copy%20of)) – practically, the clerk might do that (it’s in the law). That doesn’t affect your case but puts AG on notice (don’t expect the AG to intervene in your little small claim though). ADTPA is a powerful tool to mention if, say, a business blatantly lied to you or defrauded you. It can scare the defendant into settling (because if you went to circuit and hired a lawyer, they’d pay triple + fees possibly). In small claims, just use it to justify getting all your money back and maybe a bit more as punishment. And remember the 1-year limit – many ADTPA claims are lost by waiting too long.

- **Motor vehicle Lemon Law:** Alabama’s Lemon Law (Ala. Code § 8-20A-1 to 8-20A-6) covers **new cars** (purchased or leased in AL) that have a serious defect within the first 1 year or 12,000 miles which couldn’t be fixed after 3 attempts or 30 days out of service ([www.carlemon.com](https://www.carlemon.com/amp/alabama-lemon-law.html#:~:text=Alabama%20Lemon%20Law%20%288,with%20lemon%20vehicles%20may%20enjoy)) ([www.carlemon.com](https://www.carlemon.com/amp/alabama-lemon-law.html#:~:text=,with%20lemon%20vehicles%20may%20enjoy)). It requires the manufacturer to replace or refund the vehicle (less a usage allowance) if the car qualifies as a “lemon.” Enforcing the lemon law typically goes beyond small claims because it involves the entire car’s value (often > $6k). Also, the buyer typically must go through the manufacturer’s arbitration program (if one exists) before suing (§ 8-20A-2). If they do sue, it would be in circuit court likely seeking a repurchase of a $30k car. Small claims might only come into play if, say, the car’s price is under $6k (maybe you bought a new motorcycle or very cheap car that qualifies), or if you’re only seeking related damages. For example, some consumers use small claims to get reimbursed for **incidental costs** the manufacturer refused to cover (like towing or rental car costs under the lemon law’s provisions). However, those are usually small amounts that the manufacturer may eventually pay voluntarily. It’s uncommon to enforce lemon law in small claims because the main remedy (repurchase or replacement) is beyond its power. If you tried to just sue for the “lemon law penalty” (like say you want to recover the drop in value because it’s a lemon but keep the car), that’s not how the statute is designed – it intends repurchase. And small claims can’t order the maker to give you a new car. So, lemon law is included here for completeness, but realistically you wouldn’t handle a true lemon case entirely in small claims. Perhaps only if the manufacturer offered a repurchase that you think shorted you $1,000 (maybe disagreement about usage offset) – you could sue for that difference. (But even then, arbitration might cover it.) So, small claims usage of lemon law is minimal. If you have a lemon, it’s recommended to use the informal dispute settlement and if needed, hire a lemon law attorney (they often recover fees from manufacturer per statute if they win). 

- **Identity theft and credit fraud:** Alabama doesn’t have a unique civil cause of action for identity theft losses beyond general tort or ADTPA if a business allowed data breach. But if someone stole your identity and, for instance, you ended up paying money out-of-pocket (maybe you paid a debt that wasn’t yours to avoid credit ruin), you could sue the thief for restitution in small claims (if you know who it is). Often, unfortunately, identity thieves are not known or collectible. If it was someone you know (like an ex opened a credit card in your name and ran up $5,000), you could sue them for that $5,000 as fraud. Explain the situation to the judge (e.g., you got stuck with the bill or it damaged your credit requiring you to pay some to settle). You’d likely have a police report to back up the fraud. There’s also a federal law allowing an ID theft victim to sue for some statutory damages (Fair Credit Reporting Act can give up to $1,000 per willful violation by someone using your report illegally, etc.). But going after the perpetrator in small claims is possible if they’re within reach. This is a niche scenario. Typically these matters are handled through the credit bureaus or criminal courts. But the option is there for known fraudsters (like a relative who used your SSN and you had to pay something). Alabama’s ADTPA covers **identity fraud** indirectly as an unfair practice if done by a business, but if it’s an individual thief, ADTPA may not apply (ADTPA covers business transactions, not pure criminal theft by an individual). So you’d sue for fraud or conversion. If the amount is big, probably beyond small claim. If it’s moderate, you might do it more for principle (maybe they won’t show up and you get default – can at least put lien on them or ruin their credit reciprocally). 

- **Wage and labor law penalties:** Alabama doesn’t have its own wage/hour penalties aside from the federal FLSA double damage we discussed. Alabama also has no state law for things like wrongful termination or unpaid wages penalty – these are either common law (like if you had a contract) or federal (FLSA, which we did cover). If, for example, you believe you’re due a “waiting time penalty” like in CA – Alabama has no such law. So no claim. If you try to claim triple wages as a “penalty” just because it was willful, you’d base it on FLSA or ADTPA if applicable (ADTPA might cover a dishonest employer in a consumer context but usually employment issues aren’t under ADTPA because it’s not commerce with a *consumer*). So not much state statutory ground there.

- **Small claims for Sunshine or public records**: Not relevant – if someone references a statutory claim for open records or similar, those are enforced by petition in circuit court, not in small claims. 

*(The above list covers key Alabama statutes one might invoke in a small claim. Always pay attention to the **time limits** and any **pre-suit steps** specified in those laws (like the ADTPA demand letter, the deposit 60-day rule, etc.).)*

### Other state-specific claim types

This is an open-ended category to ensure we didn’t miss any common dispute that Alabamians might bring in small claims. Since we’ve covered the big ones, here are a few miscellaneous ones that **can** be handled in small claims:

- **HOA or condo fee disputes:** If you live in a homeowners’ association or condo, and there’s a money dispute (e.g., HOA wrongly fined you $200 or you want a refund of an overpaid fee), you can use small claims. Many HOAs have covenants requiring internal dispute resolution, but if not, or if that failed, small claims is fine for small sums. If the HOA sues you for unpaid dues, in some states they use small claims (but often if > $6k they go to district/circuit). In Alabama, an HOA can file in small claims if the amount is ≤ $6k (and probably often do for quick collection). As a homeowner, if the HOA charged you improperly (say, charged for a repair that is HOA’s responsibility), you can sue for that reimbursement or credit. There’s no special HOA court; so it’s contract law (bylaws are contract). Also note, if an HOA has recorded a lien for the amount, you can still contest the debt via small claims (though to release the lien you might have to pay or petition a higher court; but a small claims judgment in your favor can help clear it). 

- **Mold or habitability reimbursements:** If your apartment had mold or other issues and you had to spend money (like on an air purifier or temporary lodging) and the landlord wouldn’t reimburse, you can claim those costs as a breach of habitability (like we talked about under tenant repair claims). Alabama doesn’t have a specific mold statute, but it falls under general habitability obligations. So yes, small claims can handle your $500 of extra electricity and hotel cost because AC failed, for instance.

- **Towing or booting fees (parking enforcement):** This is covered above, but to emphasize: if a private company booted your car and charged $50 to remove the boot, and you think it was improper (maybe sign wasn’t clear or you actually paid the parking meter), you can sue that company for the $50. It’s small, but people do it out of principle (and to discourage predatory booting). Or if you parked in an apartment lot as a guest and got towed unlawfully, yes, claim your $150.

- **Airbnb or short-term rental disputes:** If you rented a short-term property and it was significantly not as advertised or the host breached cancellation terms, you might try small claims for a refund. Many short-term rental platforms have arbitration clauses and resolution centers. If you simply bypassed that and sued the host in small claims for, say, $500 refund, you might need to serve the host (if they’re local). If they live out of state, hard to get jurisdiction unless property in AL. But if it’s a local host and they lied about listing (like said “whole house” but it was shared, etc.), ADTPA could apply. Actually, ADTPA explicitly exempts transactions regulated by a state/federal agency – short-term lodging might not be regulated beyond general law, so ADTPA might cover a deceptive ad (like claiming a rental had amenities it didn’t). On a small scale, you’d probably just sue for a refund for whatever you didn’t receive (plus maybe out-of-pocket costs like had to go to a hotel). Check the platform terms though – sometimes they say disputes must go through them or arbitration. But if platform involvement failed, small claims is an option against the host.

- **Cell phone or utility deposits:** If you gave a deposit to a utility (power, gas, water) or phone/internet company and they haven’t returned it long after you closed service, you can sue for it. Those companies often have internal processes, but small claims is a backstop. Keep in mind, if it’s a regulated utility, there might be an avenue through the Alabama Public Service Commission for complaints. But for something like a cell phone company deposit, small claims works. Many service contracts do have arbitration clauses (most cell carriers do). But for $100 deposit, they might just pay when faced with a court date. Or an ISP charged an early termination fee you feel was unlawful under contract – you could challenge that in small claims (though they might send you to collections and you’d then dispute that). If you proactively sue them for refund of a fee you paid, be prepared for them possibly to compel arbitration. But sometimes they simply don’t show up or settle because $200 isn’t worth a lawyer. You then win by default. Some customers do this for principle (maybe claiming an “automatic renewal” was illegal under ADTPA – Alabama has an Auto-Renewal law requiring clear disclosure and consent for subscription renewals, codified at Ala. Code § 8-1-2. If a gym or subscription violated that, ADTPA might allow you to recover fees paid due to an undisclosed auto-renew). That’s a state-specific angle: Alabama’s law effective 2018 says auto-renewing consumer contracts must have clear notice and cancellation methods. If a company violated that and charged you, you could sue for those charges as an unfair practice under ADTPA. It’s a novel small claims issue, but plausible.

- **Gym membership cancellations:** Covered earlier – Alabama doesn’t have a cooling-off for gym contracts beyond the 3-day door-to-door rule which doesn’t apply if you signed at the gym. But if you cancelled per contract and they kept billing, that’s ADTPA deceptive practice. People have sued gyms/senders in small claims for continuing to charge after valid cancellation and gotten refunds. Yes, do that – show proof of cancellation notice and continued charges (bank statements). 

- **Invasion of privacy / harassment (small scale):** This is tricky – small claims is not ideal for personal torts like defamation, harassment, etc., because putting a dollar on those is complicated. Alabama recognizes tort of invasion of privacy and wantonness etc., but serious claims require jury evaluation and often exceed $6k in intangible harm. If someone minorly harassed you and you had $500 in costs (maybe changed your number $50, took time off work $200, etc.), you could attempt to sue for those costs. But for emotional distress alone, small claims is unlikely to award money unless you prove some relative out-of-pocket loss. Also, Alabama likely requires at least some showing of actual damage. So I’d generally avoid using small claims for purely emotional or reputational harm (defamation per se has no needed proof of damage in higher court, but in small claims a judge is likely uncomfortable awarding say $5,000 for slander with no concrete economic loss evidence). They might dismiss it as too complex or not a suitable claim. 

- **Sales tax or overcharge issues:** If a business accidentally or intentionally overcharged you (like charged wrong sales tax or a higher price than tag), for a small amount, you might not bother suing – but if it was systematically deceptive pricing (like a store advertising one price but charging more), ADTPA is in play. Realistically, individuals seldom sue for a few dollars in small claims. But a pattern could be reported to AG. If it was more significant (say a contractor charged you 20% more than agreed), that’s breach for the difference which you’d pursue (which we covered under services). 

By covering all these categories, we’ve essentially described “everything you can sue for” in Alabama small claims: if it’s a **debt or damage of a monetary nature up to $6,000, and not explicitly excluded by law, you can likely bring it to small claims**. Alabama’s small claims courts are quite broad in civil subject matter. The next sections detail the processes (counterclaims, exclusions, etc.).

### Counterclaims

**Counterclaims** are the defendant’s claim back against the plaintiff, heard at the same small claims hearing. In Alabama small claims, a defendant has the right to file a counterclaim for any amount up to $6,000 (the same jurisdictional limit) without automatically getting the case sent out of small claims ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,promulgated%20by%20the%20Supreme%20Court)). Counterclaims in small claims are common, especially in situations like landlord vs. tenant (tenant might counterclaim for deposit or repairs if landlord sues for rent) or credit disputes (a defendant might counterclaim the product was defective, etc.). Key points:

- **How to file:** The defendant should fill out a **Small Claims Counterclaim form (Form SM-06)** ([eforms.alacourt.gov](https://eforms.alacourt.gov/search/?q=SM-06#:~:text=E,11%2F16%2F2005)) ([shelby.alacourt.gov](https://shelby.alacourt.gov/small-claims/small-claims-faq/#:~:text=filed%20within%2014%20calendar%20days,you%20have%20against%20the%20plaintiff)) and file it with the clerk, usually by the time of the trial or earlier. In Alabama, the small claims rules (Rule F) say a written answer is due within 14 days of service of the complaint ([shelby.alacourt.gov](https://shelby.alacourt.gov/small-claims/small-claims-faq/#:~:text=Mail%20or%20UPS%20Commercial%20Carrier,a%20conflict%20with%20the%20trial)). A counterclaim is typically included with or filed at the same time as the answer (the Shelby small claims FAQ explicitly mentions “the Answer form is available... As the defendant, you may also choose to file a Counterclaim” ([shelby.alacourt.gov](https://shelby.alacourt.gov/small-claims/small-claims-faq/#:~:text=you%20must%20answer%20the%20Complaint,you%20have%20against%20the%20plaintiff))). Practically, many defendants just show up in court and orally state their counterclaim to the judge. However, it’s better to file it in writing beforehand so the plaintiff is on notice and the court file reflects it. The Alabama AOC E-Forms site provides **Form SM-06 (“Defendant’s Counterclaim”)** ([eforms.alacourt.gov](https://eforms.alacourt.gov/search/?q=SM-06#:~:text=E,11%2F16%2F2005)) which the defendant can fill out, listing the amount and reason. There is usually **no additional filing fee** for a counterclaim in small claims (the initial plaintiff’s filing fee covers the case) – *but* Ala. Code § 12-19-71 mentions that if a case exceeds $1,500 in controversy it uses higher docket fee ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,are%20not%20small%20claims%20cases)). Typically, if a counterclaim pushes the case over $1,500 total, the clerk at filing might require the defendant to pay the difference to equal the normal district court fee (this is per § 12-12-31(f) and § 12-19-71(a)(3) ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/#:~:text=,are%20not%20small%20claims%20cases)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-12/chapter-19/article-3/division-2/section-12-19-71/#:~:text=%283%29%20One%20hundred%20ninety,in%20determining%20the%20jurisdictional%20amount))). In practice, some counties charge a modest fee for filing a counterclaim above the small claims base (Shelby’s fee list shows no separate fee for counterclaims under $1,500, but implies the higher initial fee covers it if >$1,500). It’s best to ask the clerk. Usually, for simplicity, if the whole case remains in small claims, they might not charge extra – but law says if claim over $1,500 total, docket fee same as district. It’s a bit confusing. Regardless, expect possibly ~$30-$50 cost if your counterclaim is large (some counties list a $50 fee if a default or dispositive motion is filed and amount >$3k ([russell.alacourt.gov](https://russell.alacourt.gov/small-claims/#:~:text=Additional%20Plaintiffs%20%20,%2417.00%C2%A0each)), but not specifically for counterclaim; likely it’s just considered another “claim” so additional $ + service cost if serving a third party). However, since the plaintiff is already there, no new service fee needed for a counterclaim – they get it by mail or at trial.

- **Scope of counterclaims:** In Alabama, a counterclaim in small claims does *not* have to be on the same transaction or occurrence (small claims follows a simplified process, and the usual Alabama Rule of Civil Procedure 13 would allow even unrelated counterclaims as permissive). However, practically, small claims judges prefer it to be somewhat related, because if it’s totally unrelated, they might sever it or advise filing separately. But the law doesn’t forbid an unrelated counterclaim – for instance, if John sues Mary for a debt, Mary *could* counterclaim that John damaged her car in a separate incident. The court could hear both if within limit. If the counterclaim is clearly more appropriate as a separate case, the judge might ask, but generally, anything within jurisdiction is allowed. If a counterclaim is for more than $6,000, the **district court has concurrent jurisdiction above $6k to $20k** ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-11-30/#:~:text=%281%29%20CIVIL,exclusive%20of%20interest%20and%20costs)). However, the small claims *rules* limit small claims cases to $6k. So if the defendant counterclaims for, say, $8,000, one of two things can happen: either (1) the case is **transferred** out of the small claims docket to the general civil docket (District Court regular) where formal rules apply, or (2) the defendant can **waive the amount over $6k** and just claim $6,000 to keep it in small claims. Ala. Code § 12-12-71(b) says if a compulsory counterclaim would exceed jurisdiction, the small claims court may transfer the entire case to District civil (or if both parties consent, they can stay and waive the excess). Many defendants in small claims intentionally reduce their counterclaim to $6k to avoid transfer – they might not want to go to the trouble of higher court either. Example: landlord sues for $5,000 unpaid rent; tenant thinks property damage by landlord is $7,000 – the tenant could counterclaim $6,000 (forgo $1k) to stay in small claims. If the tenant insisted on $7k, the court *must* either get parties’ consent to limit recovery to $6k or bump it up to District Court (where it could be consolidated with any other pending claims if needed). Typically, small claims judges will ask the counterclaimant at trial, “Are you limiting your counterclaim to $6,000 for this court?” If they say yes, they proceed; if they say no, judge may remove it. So generally, assume $6k is the effective cap for counters too.

## Sources

[1] Ala. Code § 12-12-31 (Small Claims jurisdiction & procedures in District Court) – FindLaw (updated 2024). <https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-12-31/>

[2] *The Troy Messenger* (Aug. 5, 2015). **“Small Claims Court increases claim amount limitations.”** (Act 2015-224 raised Alabama’s small claims limit from $3,000 to $6,000 effective 2015.) <https://www.troymessenger.com/2015/08/05/small-claims-court-increases-claim-amount-limitations/>

[3] Marion County Circuit Clerk – **Small Claims Court Information and Fees** (Alabama 25th Judicial Circuit). (Confirms $6,000 limit and lists filing fees: $50 up to $1,500; $124 up to $3,000; $213 up to $6,000; $10 per additional defendant.) <http://marion.alacourt.gov/small-claims/>

[4] Lee County Circuit Clerk – **Small Claims FAQs and Fees** (Alabama 37th Judicial Circuit). (Confirms $6,000 limit; filing fees: $102 up to $1,500; $176 up to $3,000; $265 up to $6,000; $10 per added defendant; sheriff service $20; etc.) <http://lee.alacourt.gov/small-claims/>

[5] Russell County Circuit Clerk – **Small Claims Filing Fees** (Alabama 26th Judicial Circuit). (Fees: $85 up to $1,500; $159 up to $3,000; $248 up to $6,000; additional defendant $15; sheriff service $15; default judgment fee $35 for claims >$3,000; subpoenas $17 each.) <http://russell.alacourt.gov/small-claims/>

[6] Ala. Code § 12-19-71 (Civil filing fees in circuit & district courts) – Justia (2024). (Base docket fees: $35 for small claims ≤$1,500; $109 for ≤$3,000; $198 for >$3,000 up to $20,000; provides same fee schedule applied in small claims cases over $1,500) <https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2024/title-12/chapter-19/article-3/division-2/section-12-19-71/>

[7] Ala. Code § 6-3-2 (Venue of actions against individuals) – FindLaw (2024). (For individuals: contracts – sue where defendant resides; other personal actions – sue where defendant resides or where the act/omission occurred; also venue provisions for nonresidents) <https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-6-civil-practice/al-code-sect-6-3-2/>

[8] Ala. Code § 6-11-20 (Punitive damages – standards & definitions) – FindLaw (2024). (Requires clear and convincing evidence of oppression, fraud, etc., for punitive; defines wantonness; not directly mentioning small claims but sets general standard) <https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-6-civil-practice/al-code-sect-6-11-20/>

[9] Ala. Code § 6-5-285 (Civil action for **worthless check**; damages) – Justia (2024). (Holder of a bad check may sue the maker for both **punitive and compensatory damages**, including attorney’s fee, even if no criminal action; suit must be within 1 year of the act) <https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2024/title-6/chapter-5/article-16/section-6-5-285/>

[10] Ala. Code § 8-19-10 (Alabama **Deceptive Trade Practices Act** – private right of action) – Justia (2024). (Allows any consumer harmed by unlawful deceptive act to recover **actual damages or $100**, whichever greater, and up to three times actual in the court’s discretion ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,100%2C%20whichever%20is%20greater%3B%20or)), plus costs and reasonable attorney’s fee ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,reasonable%20attorney%27s%20fees%20and%20costs)). Requires suit within 1 year ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,14)) and clerk to mail copy of complaint to AG & DA ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/4653/8-19-10.html#:~:text=,shall%20mail%20a%20copy%20of)).) <https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2024/title-8/chapter-19/section-8-19-10/>

[11] Ala. Code § 35-9A-201 (Residential **security deposits**; landlord obligations) – Justia (2025). (Landlord cannot demand >1 month’s rent as deposit (except pets, etc.) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=,obligations%20under%20a%20rental%20agreement)); must refund or itemize within 60 days of end of tenancy ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=,tenancy%20and%20delivery%20of%20possession)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=35,tenancy%20and%20delivery%20of%20possession)); failing that, landlord **owes double** the deposit to tenant ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/#:~:text=both%2C%20is%20sufficient%20compliance%20with,this%20chapter)).) <https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2025/title-35/chapter-9a/article-2/section-35-9a-201/>

[12] Shelby County Circuit Clerk – **Small Claims FAQ** (Alabama 18th Judicial Circuit). (Explains small claims procedures: who can sue, how to file, 14-day answer deadline, counterclaims, trial process, no jury, **appeal within 14 days** to Circuit for trial de novo with $302 fee for non-jury or $402 for jury ([shelby.alacourt.gov](https://shelby.alacourt.gov/small-claims/small-claims-faq/#:~:text=If%20either%20of%20you%20,Office%20also%20has%20this%20form)), etc.) <https://shelby.alacourt.gov/small-claims/small-claims-faq/>

[13] Alabama Administrative Office of Courts – **E-Forms – Small Claims** (Form listings). (Provides official fillable forms: SM-01 Statement of Claim – General, SM-02 Statement of Claim – Specific Property, SM-03 Defendant’s Answer, SM-06 Defendant’s Counterclaim, etc., with latest revision dates). <https://eforms.alacourt.gov> (navigate to “Small Claims” category)

[14] Ala. Code § 12-11-30 (Circuit Court jurisdiction; concurrent jurisdiction with District Court) – FindLaw (2024). (Circuit courts have exclusive original jurisdiction over claims >$20,000, and **concurrent jurisdiction with district** for claims >$6,000 up to $20,000 ([codes.findlaw.com](https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-11-30/#:~:text=%281%29%20CIVIL,exclusive%20of%20interest%20and%20costs)). This means if a small claims counterclaim exceeds $6k, the case can be moved to circuit unless parties remit amount to $6k.) <https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-12-courts/al-code-sect-12-11-30/>

[15] Ala. Code § 8-8-10 (Interest on **money judgments** in Alabama) – Justia (2024). (All judgments based on contract bear the contract’s rate; all other judgments bear **7.5% per annum** from date of entry ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-8/chapter-8/section-8-8-10/#:~:text=,from%20the%20day%20of%20entry)). This statute set post-judgment interest at 7.5% after Sept 1, 2011.) <https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2024/title-8/chapter-8/section-8-8-10/>

[16] Ala. Code § 8-8-1 (Legal interest rates in Alabama) – FindLaw (2024). (Unless otherwise agreed in writing, **interest rate is 6% per year** for money lent or overdue accounts ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-8/chapter-8/section-8-8-1/#:~:text=Alabama%20Code%20%C2%A7%208,or%20for%20a%20longer%20or)); by written contract parties may go up to 8% (higher for certain loans under other laws). This is basis for claiming 6% pre-judgment interest on liquidated debts.) <https://codes.findlaw.com/al/title-8-commercial-law-and-consumer-protection/al-code-sect-8-8-1/>

[17] Ala. Code § 10A-1-7.21 (Foreign businesses **transacting without registration**; bar to suing) – Justia (2024). (A foreign entity transacting business in Alabama without registering may not maintain any action in Alabama courts until it registers ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-10a/chapter-1/article-7/division-c/section-10a-1-7-21/#:~:text=,has%20registered%20in%20this%20state)). This means an unregistered out-of-state company must register before using small claims as a plaintiff. It does not impair contracts or defending suits.) <https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2024/title-10a/chapter-1/article-7/division-c/section-10a-1-7-21/>

[18] Ala. Code § 11-47-23 (Claims against municipalities – **notice requirement**). – Justia (2024). (Any claim for money against a city must be presented to the city clerk within **6 months** (for tort) or 2 years (for contract) of accrual, as a prerequisite to suit ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2006/8577/11-47-23.html#:~:text=Section%2011,View%20Our%20Newest%20Version%20Here)). In practical terms, a small claims suit against a city may be barred if this notice wasn’t given. Often applies to slip-and-fall on city property, damage by city vehicles, etc.) <https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2024/title-11/chapter-47/article-3/section-11-47-23/>

[19] Ala. Code § 6-9-211 (Judgments constitute **liens** on defendant’s property) – Justia (2025). (When you obtain a judgment and file a certificate of judgment in probate court, it becomes a lien on the defendant’s property in that county for **10 years** ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/title-6/chapter-9/article-9/section-6-9-211/#:~:text=Every%20judgment%2C%20a%20certificate%20of,judgment%3B%20provided%2C%20that%20when%20an)). This is relevant for post-judgment collection: an Alabama small claims judgment can be recorded to secure payment.) <https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2025/title-6/chapter-9/article-9/section-6-9-211/>

[20] Ala. Code § 6-9-190 (Revival of judgments after 20 years) – Justia (2024). (A judgment cannot be revived (extended) after **20 years** from its entry ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2017/title-6/chapter-9/article-8/section-6-9-190/#:~:text=Alabama%20Code%20%C2%A7%206,2017)). An Alabama money judgment is valid for 10 years and can be renewed once for another 10; after 20 years from original, it’s unenforceable. Small claims winners should act within that time.) <https://law.justia.com/codes/alabama/2024/title-6/chapter-9/article-8/section-6-9-190/>
